title: Manufacturing practices and operational performance: the roles of supplier and internal integration
abstract: Operational performance is considered vital and at top priority in all organizations for profit maximization, which is achieved through the cost and flexibility. Following that, important pillars such as TPM, TQM, supplier integration, and internal integration are crucial for organizations. Therefore, this study focuses on the linkage of these organizational factors to improve cost and flexibility, which enhances performance for identifying and understanding the sustainability and expansion goals of the organizations. More specifically, the current research aims at exploring and understanding the relationships of manufacturing practices (i.e., TPM, TQM) and SCM (i.e., supplier integration, internal integration) along with operational performance (i.e., cost, flexibility) of the industrial firms. This study was conducted through fundamental rules and the basis of the positivist paradigm, which favours using the quantitative method in order to investigate the research problem. The research philosophy follows the objectivism approach through deductive reasoning. This research incorporated quantitative data from 224 Jordanian industrial firms through traditional and online questionnaires. This data was analysed by using SPSS and AMOS software in order to obtain valid, reliable, and generalisable findings and conclusion. In this way, the current study contributed to highlight and develop a better understanding of the potential determinants of operational performance through generally proving its significant correlation with the efficient SCM and the proper implementation of manufacturing practices. Furthermore, findings from this research revealed that supplier integration is significantly as well as positively impacting the cost and flexibility performance of the organisation, while internal integration is found to have significant positive effect on flexibility performance of the organisation. Moreover, the study concluded that understanding the different operational performance’s determinants (cost and flexibility) allows stakeholders in the industrial sector to make thoughtful decisions that can help them producing more unique products with a competitive quality, which enables the industrial organisations to manage their profitability concerns.

ANGLIA RUSKIN UNIVERSITY 
 
ABSTRACT 
FACULTY OF BUSINESS AND LAW 
DOCTOR OF PHILOSOPHY 
MANUFACTURING PRACTICES AND OPERATIONAL PERFORMANCE: THE 
ROLES OF SUPPLIER AND INTERNAL INTEGRATION 
MOHAMMAD MEZIAD AL-JUNIDI 
February 2022 
Operational performance is considered vital and at top priority in all organizations for profit 
maximization, which is achieved through the cost and flexibility. Following that, important 
pillars such as TPM, TQM, supplier integration, and internal integration are crucial for 
organizations. Therefore, this study focuses on the linkage of these organizational factors to 
improve cost and flexibility, which enhances performance for identifying and understanding 
the sustainability and expansion goals of the organizations. More specifically, the current 
research aims at exploring and understanding the relationships of manufacturing practices (i.e., 
TPM, TQM) and SCM (i.e., supplier integration, internal integration) along with operational 
performance (i.e., cost, flexibility) of the industrial firms. This study was conducted through 
fundamental rules and the basis of the positivist paradigm, which favours using the quantitative 
method in order to investigate the research problem. The research philosophy follows the 
objectivism approach through deductive reasoning. This research incorporated quantitative 
data from 224 Jordanian industrial firms through traditional and online questionnaires. This 
data was analysed by using SPSS and AMOS software in order to obtain valid, reliable, and 
generalisable findings and conclusion. In this way, the current study contributed to highlight 
and develop a better understanding of the potential determinants of operational performance 
through generally proving its significant correlation with the efficient SCM and the proper 
implementation of manufacturing practices. Furthermore, findings from this research revealed 
that supplier integration is significantly as well as positively impacting the cost and flexibility 
performance of the organisation, while internal integration is found to have significant positive 
effect on flexibility performance of the organisation. Moreover, the study concluded that 
understanding the different operational performance’s determinants (cost and flexibility) 
allows stakeholders in the industrial sector to make thoughtful decisions that can help them 
producing more unique products with a competitive quality, which enables the industrial 
organisations to manage their profitability concerns.  
Keywords: Operational Performance; Organisational Performance; Manufacturing 
Practices; Supplier Integration; Internal Integration

Chapter 1 : INTRODUCTION 
 
1.1. Overview 
Operational performance is seen as a key element of organisational success; however, this 
success could only be achieved when the key factors, which are contributing to operational 
performance, are focused on by the organisations. Operational performance implies the 
effective conversion of inputs into desired outputs in a specified time (Polder et al., 2013). 
Hence, the inputs, outputs, and their interlinked ratios are the main determinants of 
organisational performance, which could be fulfilled through the careful management of the 
operational performance of organisation and its contributing factors. It is essential for 
organisations to identify as well as understanding the internal and external factors; those are 
significantly contributing to the production operations. Internal factors such as preventive 
maintenance, quality management, supplier and internal integration, operational flexibility, and 
cost decisions may affect the operational performance levels of an organisation (Biener et al., 
2016). 
To achieve a sustained competitive advantage, supply chain management (SCM from here 
onwards) practices and total quality management (TQM from here onwards) practices have 
raised to the top of the priority list. TQM, total productive maintenance (TPM from here 
onwards), and SCM have been studied as the most significant predictors of company 
performance by researchers from around the world using various models (i.e Bouranta, et al., 
2017; Danese, 2013; Huo, 2012; Kaur et al., 2012). A set of procedures known as TQM 
emphasizes continuous improvement, ongoing results measurement, meeting customer 
requests, a team-based approach to problem-solving, competitive benchmarking, lowering 
workload, long-term planning, and strong relationships with suppliers (Soares et al., 2017). 
Meanwhile, SCM has become one of the most important and effective competitive strategies 
for manufacturing companies in recent years. The fundamental tenet of SCM can be 
summarised as taking input from suppliers, adding value, and giving it to the customer. 
Based on the above concepts, organizations need to keep a constant focus on such factors, if 
they want to attain the desired levels of organizational performance through the enhancement 
of operational performance (Polder et al., 2013).  Moreover, through relying on the 
 
2 
 
aforementioned discussion, the current study focuses on identifying the key determinants of 
operational performance in the industrial sector, by exploring the impact of manufacturing 
practices like TQM and TPM in the operational performance (Cost, Flexibility), while studying 
the role of supplier and internal integration in the Jordanian industrial sector. The focus of this 
research is on the significance of internal and external integration in companies as a strategy 
for improving operational performance and other outcomes. These include the assessment of 
the identified benefits; those can be derived from the adoption of TPM and TQM in 
organisations to enhance operational performance and excellence. It focuses on these 
innovations in the industrial sector, which are designed to improve the efficiency and the 
operational performance of manufacturing processes. Moreover, it is essential to conduct 
empirical research to identify how these innovations influence suppliers and internal 
integration as well as operational performance in the industrial sector.  
1.2.  Research Context 
The core focus of this study is to evaluate the role of manufacturing practices and supply chain 
integration in enhancing the operational performance of Jordanian industrial sector. 
Operational performance is considered as a critical success factor for organisations across the 
globe. It is best indicated as the level at which business units of an organisation achieves 
organisational goals through their combined efforts (Nabass et al., 2019). Many organisations 
create specific job functions or departments that mainly focus on the transformation of 
organisational assets into enhanced performance (Saleh et al., 2018). The enhancement of 
operational performance requires organisations to modify their manufacturing practices. Such 
modification leads to agility, precision, and efficiency to consistently outperform the 
competition (Saleh et al., 2018). Enhancement of operational performance has been widely 
focused by businesses for organisational transformational initiatives. Innovative approaches, 
technologies and tools are enabling the firms to enhance their performance with flexibility, 
agility, and precision (Nawanir, 2016). In recent competitive environment, such 
transformations have become vital for businesses. Enhanced performance could no longer be 
achieved through money, time, and resources only. Therefore, most of the businesses are 
moving beyond traditional options for reducing costs and improved performance. However, 
the focus is solid; ensuring business success through enhanced performance (Ali et al., 2020).  
In this regard, manufacturing organisations primarily concentrate on their manufacturing 
practices to accomplish their operational performance excellence. Manufacturing practices are 
 
3 
 
considered as a set of activities that leads towards achieving the targeted organisational goals. 
Organisations adopting latest manufacturing practices or transforming their traditional practice 
to the latest required ones, are more viable to achieve success (Nawanir, 2016). In recent 
globalised markets, the businesses are not limited to the domestic markets only, rather the 
organisations focus on attaining international standards (Nayal, et al., 2022). To compete in the 
international markets, operational performance excellence is widely reported as a key factor 
that is significantly influenced by the manufacturing practices adopted by the organisations. 
However, in manufacturing organisations across globe TQM and TPM are widely implemented 
manufacturing practices (Nabasset al., 2019). Therefore, these practices are a key concern for 
researchers in supply chain field as well. Literature reports evidence of studying the 
relationships between manufacturing practices and operational performance. In this context, 
other aspects of supply chains are also reported to have significant relationship with operational 
performance including supply chain integration (Matar and Eneizan, 2018). The literature lacks 
the interrelationships of all these main factors including manufacturing practices, operational 
performance, supply chain integration etc (refer to table 2-1). Therefore, further research needs 
to investigate and highlight the nature of relationships among these key factors.  
Furthermore, the manufacturing organisations and industries are the backbone of any economy. 
These contribute significantly to economic development of the country. Jordan is not a different 
case. Jordan is a developing, non-oil producing country that is situated in the heart of the middle 
east region, where many businesses are interested in venturing, due to the economic growth 
that this area has witnessed.  Choosing Jordan as a venturing destination provides companies 
and businesses with several benefits; a wide consumer base and higher revenue numbers (Ali 
et al., 2020). Being the venturing destination, the international business in Jordan is moving 
rapidly. But for achieving profitability, there is a need for the industrial sector to implement 
strategies for TQM, TPM, strategic integration, and internal integration through operational 
performance (cost and flexibility) for profit maximization. Therefore, this research focuses on 
elaborating how manufacturing practices and supply chain integration influence the operational 
performance of the Jordanian industrial sector. Jordanian industrial sector significantly 
contributes to the economic development of the country (Saleh et al., 2018). Such sectors need 
the critical focus of researchers and policy makers to conduct their critical role in highlighting 
the factors that can enhance the operational performance of manufacturing industries. 
However, different parameters and measurements must be thought about in advance for the 
 
4 
 
effective decisions regarding the selection of the right processes to develop the Jordanian 
industrial sector (Dahi, 2014).  
The economic stability and strategic relations allow Jordan to be among the viable countries to 
run businesses as well as attracting further relations with other countries across the world. 
Jordan is also regarded as an emerging destination in which a solid base for the renewable 
energy exists that aim to achieve clean technology. These advantages constitute Jordan’s 
unique strategic position that helps to facilitate the socio-economic growth and sustainable 
development (Alrgaibat, 2017; Mugableh, 2019). According to a report from the economic 
policy council, it seems clear that Jordan’s economic growth is one of the reasons that attract 
businesses and investors. There is an economic growth plan ranging from 2018 to 2022, which 
puts Jordan in a position to achieve sustainable growth and trajectory. Through the plan, the 
economic growth in Jordan is expected to be doubled in the coming years. The report also 
expects an increase in the number of businesses targeting the Jordanian market due to the 
flexible economic environment for conducting business as well as the political stability and 
security and the vital and modern infrastructure that give investors surety of getting returns on 
their investments (Alrgaibat, 2017).  
The Jordanian economic status is deemed to be attractive to foreign investments, due to the 
high skilled workforce and the high levels of employment opportunities that can be achieved 
from the process. Jordan is a major touristic destination comparing to other countries in the 
middle east. Among the tourism types that Jordan is known with is the medical care and tours, 
entertainment adventures and historical site investigations. According to the world bank, there 
are major reforms that support the strong economic pathway not only for investors, but also for 
the locals in Jordan (Al-Hawary and Al-Hamwan, 2017). These reforms furnish the ground for 
long-term prosperity in which employees and working individuals are able to cover the income 
tax, handle different business regulations as well as understanding investment promotions and 
laws.  
The support of different global free trade, as well as bilateral agreements, is also worth noting. 
Jordan 2025 vision has been among the guiding principles for the investors and the country at 
large towards steering solid economic growth. Through this vision, there is an establishment 
of an economic growth plan, which shows a gradual economic increase from 6.5 recorded in 
the year of 2021 to 7.5 that should be achieved by 2025. The vision is generally aiming at 
ensuring a jumpstarting of Jordan’s economic development to be beyond the expectation and 
 
5 
 
support the Jordanian government to grow (Sahoo and Yadav, 2020). These are the main 
reasons why the industrial sector in Jordan has managed to grow immensely and businesses are 
coming from foreign directions to be welcomed in Jordan (Lubbad and Adam-Bagley, 2021). 
Another significant factor that becomes the attraction of Jordanian economy for internal 
organisations is its educated youth. Educated young people contribute significantly to the 
success of organisations and understand the latest strategic and structural changes required in 
industries (Sahoo and Yadav, 2020; Mahafzah, 2015; Lubbad and Adam-Bagley, 2021).  
Moreover, Jordan as a country also has the opportunity of offering different location 
advantages that are vital for the attraction of investments to the Jordanian industrial sector. 
These include the provision of a high-quality air transit for access; strong railroads 
infrastructure with better highway infrastructure developed. The Jordanian industrial sector 
contains a different array of essential sub-sectors. These sub-sectors help the industries to 
exhibit the different sophistication, and bring about proper dynamisms, especially the economic 
boost process. The industrial sector is characterised by reciprocity and dealing with the issues 
complementarily. With these issues, sub-sectors in connection with the industries require the 
production to be appropriate for a better development (Matar and Eneizan, 2018). 
Despite having all the above-mentioned advantages of the Jordanian economy, for attracting 
the international investors, there is a need to highlight the facts about manufacturing sectors 
including the ways of enhancing operational performance. Moreover, highlighting the key 
factors can contribute significantly in enhancing the level of production of these manufacturing 
industries, which ultimately attracts international investors (Mahafzah, 2015). Therefore, this 
research is concentrating on highlighting the key factors of Jordanian industrial sector that can 
contribute positively to enhancing the operational performance of manufacturing firms in 
Jordan. The main focus of this research is to highlight the contribution of TQM and TPM 
among other manufacturing practices because these two practices are widely considered as 
significant contributors of operational performance (Konecny and Thun, 2011; Kaynak and 
Hartley, 2009; Tiwari et al., 2015; Huo et al., 2018, Phan et al., 2019, Danese, 2013; Sahoo and 
Yadav, 2020; Ahmad et al., 2019; Sahoo, 2019; Wicaksono and Bambang, 2019; Phan et al., 
2019). On the other hand, among different SCM practices, internal integration and supplier 
integration are found to be comparatively better contributors to the operational performance 
than the customer integration (Gimenez et al., 2012; Mofokeng and Chinomona, 2019; Swink 
and Schoenherr, 2015). In this way, this research is a significant contribution as well, as it 
 
6 
 
reports all these significant factors combined in one study and their accumulated contribution 
to the operational performance of organizations.    
1.3.  Problem Statement 
In the recent era, no organisation could survive in the marketplace, unless having a keen focus 
on the enhancement of their operational performance, which also results in attaining the core 
purpose of stakeholders’ wealth maximization. Therefore, almost all organisations shed the 
light on improving their efficiency in every possible way, but successful are the only ones who 
have a keen focus on the contributing factors that could have a significant impact on inputs, 
outputs, and the overall performance of the organisation (Aspinwall and Elgharib, 2013; Phan 
et al., 2019). 
Therefore, there are several studies investigated the contributing factors towards organisational 
performance and efficiency. In this regards, the current literature reports the role of 
manufacturing practices on organisational performance in different ways, but among all other 
manufacturing practices; TQM and TPM are considered as the most significant contributing 
manufacturing practices in the recent era (Ahmad et al., 2019; Sahoo 2019; Phan et al., 2019; 
Sahoo and Yadav, 2020), because these are found to have a significant impact in the efficiency 
of organisational performance, as compared to other manufacturing practices of the firm. 
Additionally, the literature also reports that the most significant determinants of organisational 
performance efficiency are operational performance efficiencies like the cost efficiency and 
the flexibility efficiency (Ahmad et al., 2019; Wicaksono and Bambang, 2019; Sahoo and 
Yadav, 2020).   
Recent studies have reported these manufacturing practices (TPM, TQM) as significantly 
contributing to the operational performance; cost performance, flexibility performance (Sahoo 
and Yadav, 2020; Ahmad et al., 2019; Sahoo, 2019; Wicaksono and Bambang, 2019; Phan et 
al., 2019). In addition, some studies have also reported the interrelationship of TPM and TQM 
to evaluate their impact in organisations’ performance (Ahmad et al., 2015; Konecny and Thun, 
2011). However, findings of these studies lack reporting whether manufacturing practices 
directly affect the operational performance of the Jordanian industrial companies. 
In a similar pattern, some other studies including (Konecny and Thun, 2011; Kaynak and 
Hartley, 2009; Tiwari et al., 2015; Huo et al., 2018, Phan et al., 2019, Danese, 2013) reported 
that organisational performance and the manufacturing practices of organisation, like TPM and 
 
7 
 
TQM are influenced by supplier integration, and internal integration (will be discussed in detail 
in chapter 3), but most of these studies reported the impact of these supplier, and internal 
integration in manufacturing practices (TPM, TQM) and organisational performance 
separately. Like the direct impact of internal and external integration on firm performance is 
reported by (Droge, et al., 2004), and the direct influence of supplier, internal and external 
integration on business and operational performance of firm is reported by Flynn et al. (2010). 
However, the impact of supply chain integration in the performance of firms is also reported 
in different ways (Gimenez et al., 2012; Mofokeng and Chinomona, 2019; Swink and 
Schoenherr, 2015), while the current literature lacks evidence regarding the impact of supplier 
and internal integration in manufacturing practices (TPM, TQM) and the operational 
performance of the firms in a single setting.  
Moreover, most of these studies are focusing on the resource-based view of the firm, which 
indicates that a firm achieves its objectives, due to the internal resources at its disposal and 
how it organises them. In this respect, the supplier integration is seen as a way of organising 
the link between the organisation and its suppliers. In the same way, the internal integration 
involves strategies, such as TPM and TQM, which are adopted as a tool for organising and 
enhancing the performance of the organisational operations as a whole. Hence, this study is 
based on the need to undertake research on the relationship between TPM/ TQM and cost/ 
flexibility by assessing the available resources of the organisation and how they are organised 
and integrated. Because based on previous studies, it is found that there is a significant gap in 
the current literature regarding the impact of manufacturing practices in organisational 
performance, while investigating the role of supplier and internal integration in developing 
such relationships. Thus, in order to fill this gap, the current study concentrates on investigating 
the impact of manufacturing practices (TPM, TQM) on the operational performance (cost, 
flexibility) of industrial firms with the role of supplier and internal integration. 
Moreover, this research is mainly focusing on two manufacturing practices (TPM, TQM) 
because as reported earlier, these manufacturing practices are recently found to be the most 
significant contributors towards organisational performance, as compared to other 
manufacturing practices across globe (Sahoo and Yadav, 2020; Ahmad et al., 2019; Sahoo, 
2019; Wicaksono and Bambang, 2019; Phan et al., 2019).  Similarly, TQM and TPM are also 
found to be the significant contributors towards Jordanian organisations’ performance. Al-
tarawnah (2021) reported that TQM not also significantly contributes to enhancing 
 
8 
 
organisational performance; but also creates a competitive advantage for Jordanian 
organisations, while (Al-Refaie, et al., 2022) reported TQM and TPM as significant 
contributors towards performance of firms indicating that TQM and TPM have comparatively 
larger impact on performance than other manufacturing practices in the Jordanian industrial 
organizations.    
Secondly, operational performance (Cost, flexibility) is the main focus of this research, due to 
its contribution towards defining organisations’ success (Ahmad et al., 2019; Wicaksono and 
Bambang, 2019; Sahoo and Yadav, 2020). In Jordanian industrial firms cost and operational 
flexibility are found to be the key determinants of operational performance (Yousaf et al., 2019; 
Alrwashdeh et al., 2022) that’s why the current study is primarily focusing on cost and 
flexibility in terms of operational performance of Jordanian industrial firms. Operational 
flexibility is referred to the capability of organisational operations to respond according to the 
changes in needs and requirements of the market (Yu et al., 2021). In recent era, operational 
flexibility has become one of the critical requirements for attaining the competitive edge. 
Operations of the organisations are required to be flexible enough to be modified according to 
the needs and demands of customers. Operational flexibility is considered to be among the 
most significant aspects that lead to enhanced operational performance (Yu et al., 2015). The 
other important aspect being focused by the current research is the cost of operations. 
Organisations always emphasize on reducing the costs of their operations that is termed as the 
operational efficiency of the organisation. Cost is also considered as a significant aspect of 
operational performance as operational performance could never be enhanced while having a 
control on the costs, otherwise organisations are unable to gain the competitive advantage (Yu 
et al., 2021). Therefore, this research concentrates on the operational flexibility and cost for 
operational performance of Jordanian industrial organizations. 
Thirdly, while studying the supply chain integration, many previous studies have explored the 
impact of customer integration in different ways (Chavez et al., 2015; Martinelli and Tunisini, 
2018; Alzaydi et al., 2018; Schumacher et al., 2018), but rare evidence is found regarding the 
impact of supplier integration, and internal integration in the relationship of manufacturing 
practices (TPM, TQM) on operational performance; therefore, this gap needs to be filled and 
the current research aims at filling this gap by focusing on these indirect relationships.  
Moreover, the current research is mainly focusing on supplier and internal integration while 
customer integration is not included because of the targeted population of the study i.e., Jordan. 
 
9 
 
Because in Jordan, supplier and internal integration are found to have greater impact in firms’ 
performance as compared to customer integration. Abdallah et al. (2021) and Sharabati et al. 
(2020) reported internal integration as more significantly contributing towards Jordanian firms’ 
performance, while (Pakurar et al., 2019) reported supplier, and internal integration as more 
significantly contributing to Jordanian firms’ performance, as compared to customer 
integration. Therefore, the current study concentrates on the supplier and internal integration 
of Jordanian industrial firms. In fact, Jordanian business society is not currently in a state to 
involve customers in their business processes i.e., business culture in Jordan is a bit different 
than the developed nation’s business cultures. 
However, the impact of supplier and internal integration has been investigated on 
organisational performance in different ways, but the inter-relationships of supplier integration, 
and internal integration in relation to TPM and TQM and their combined effect on performance 
is lacking in the literature. Especially, in the context of Jordanian industrial firms as on one 
side supplier and internal integration are found to be the significant contributors towards 
organizational performance (Abdallah et al., 2021; Pakurar et al., 2019; Sharabati et al., 2020), 
while on the other side, TPM and TQM are found to have significant contribution to 
organisational performance of Jordanian firms (Al-tarawnah, 2021; Al-Refaie et al., 2022); 
therefore, there is a dire need to investigate their interrelationship and inter linked contribution 
towards organisational performance of Jordanian industrial firms. The integration of internal 
and external stakeholders in the development of TPM and TQM strategies can be highly 
beneficial in developing a sustainable organisation in which the material supplied is suitable to 
the needs and requirements of the entire value chain (Ahmad et al., 2019). This allows reducing 
the cost and quality challenges that firms may encounter, during their production processes. 
TQM focuses on the overall quality of the operations of organisations, while TPM focuses on 
the enhancement of employees’ efficiencies to enhance the production from operations, while 
the combined effect of these results in enhanced performance of operations (Al-tarawnah, 
2021). The critical issue in this research is investigating how supplier and internal integration 
can improve the organisational outcomes in terms of cost reduction and flexibility. There is a 
dearth of literature on the impact that TPM/ TQM can have on organisational outcomes through 
internal and supplier integration in line with the resource-based view of the firm.   
 
10 
 
1.4.  Research Questions 
The review of previous literature indicates that the manufacturing practices (TPM, TQM), and 
supplier and internal integration are significantly contributing to the organizational 
performance particularly the operational performance of organizations. Especially, in the 
context of Jordanian industrial organizations, TPM, TQM, supplier and internal integration are 
found to be the most significant contributors towards organizational performance, but it is also 
found that manufacturing practices and SC integration are separately studied in this particular 
context. Hence, a huge gap exists in literature for studying their interrelationships and inter-
linked contribution towards operational performance of organisations, especially in Jordan. 
Therefore, the current research aims at fulfilling this gap by addressing the following research 
questions.  From the resource-based view perspective, this research addresses three major 
research questions:  
1- How do TPM and TQM jointly impact cost and flexibility performance in the Jordanian 
industrial sector? 
2- How do supplier and internal integration jointly impact TPM and TQM in the Jordanian 
industrial sector? 
3- How do supplier and internal integration jointly impact cost and flexibility performance 
in the Jordanian industrial sector? 
1.5. Research Aim and Objectives  
The current research aims at investigating the impact of manufacturing practices (TPM, TQM) 
and supply chain integration (specially focusing on supplier integration and internal 
integration) on the operational performance (i.e., cost, flexibility) of the Jordanian industrial 
sector. The core purpose is to investigate the interrelationships and inter-linked contribution of 
manufacturing practices and supply chain integration towards operational performance of 
Jordanian industrial firms. The research focuses on developing a better understanding of the 
internal and external factors that influence operational performance. Thus, in order to achieve 
the main aim of this research, the following objectives must be addressed: 
1- Investigating the joint impact of TPM and TQM in the cost and flexibility performance 
in the Jordanian industrial sector? 
2- Investigating the joint impact of supplier and internal integration in the TPM and TQM 
in the Jordanian industrial sector? 
 
11 
 
3- Investigating the joint impact of supplier and internal integration in the operational 
performance in the Jordanian industrial sector? 
1.6. Research Motivation 
In recent dynamic market environment, when the choices and demands of customers are 
frequently changing, achievement of competitive advantage has become challenging for 
organisations. Therefore, organisations concern of improving their operational efficiencies to 
compete the market. At one end, they need to focus on sustaining their competitive position, 
and on the other side they also need to attain the core objective of profit maximisation for their 
stakeholders. Such scenarios have diverted the attention of industrialists at one side, and the 
researchers on other side to highlight the key factors that can enhance the operational efficiency 
of organisations. Operational efficiency of the organisations enables them to convert their 
inputs into desired outputs while having their wealth maximisation as well. In this regard, 
researchers have started contributing their efforts through investigating and highlighting the 
key factors that must be the key focus of organisations. Despite having continuous efforts 
exerted by researchers, still there are key areas that need to be focused by researchers as well 
as industrialists. This fact is the key motivation of current research. Literature reports different 
factors that contribute in enhancing the operational performance of organisations, but the 
interrelationships of many factors are missing in literature. Therefore, the major motivation of 
this research is to highlight the fact whether the key factors reported by literature, have direct 
impact on the operational performance of organisations, or the interrelationships of these 
factors effects the operational performance of organizations. The current study is, hence, 
exploring the key factors that contribute towards better operational performance of industrial 
firms. It is also motivated to highlight the fact that the factors reported separately in different 
studies can have combined impact on the operational performance of organisations. This 
research is motivated by the need to implement innovative management philosophies in Jordan 
to support its industrialization. Jordan needs to improve its industrial sector by determining 
how to best utilize the available resources and connections with its active stakeholders. 
Moreover, it is indicated by the previous literature that at one side manufacturing practices 
(TPM, TQM) significantly impact the organisational performance while on the other hand 
supply chain integration plays a significant role in enhancing organisational performance. 
Especially in the context of Jordanian industrial organisations, TPM, TQM, supplier and 
internal integration are found to have significant contribution towards Jordanian organisations’ 
 
12 
 
performance. But it is also found that these indicators are studied separately by the previous 
studies while a huge gap exists in literature for studying the combined or inter linked effect of 
these indicators. Hence, this gap is another motivation for conducting this research so that the 
key factors could be highlighted, and Jordanian industrialists could focus on these factors for 
enhancing their organisations’ operational performance. Moreover, it is clear from the available 
literature that TPM and TQM have significant benefits on organisational outcomes, which can 
be addressed through effective integration of internal resources as well as the suppliers. 
Furthermore, this research is also motivated by the dearth of literature on the specific context 
facing the Jordanian industrial sector. It is meant to highlight how the individual aspects of the 
sector can be improved through the adoption of TPM and TQM.  
1.7.  Research Contribution 
Operational performance of organisations has become the source of competitive advantage for 
the organisations. Therefore, organisations focus on the key factors that lead to enhance their 
operational performance. Researchers have also started contributing by highlighting the 
contribution of key factors. Literature reports different evidence in this regard but lacks 
evidence about the combined effects of different industrial factors that contribute to the 
enhancement of operational performance. This research is a significant contribution to fill this 
gap in literature. Therefore, this research highlights the role of manufacturing practices 
including TQM and TPM, combined with the supply chain practices including supplier 
integration and internal integration towards operational performance. It highlights the fact that 
how different industrial factors accumulatively enhance the organisational outcomes. This 
research not only contributes significantly to literature but also have significant contribution to 
the economic development of Jordan. It highlights the facts about the manufacturing practices 
and supply chain practices being implemented in Jordan industrial organisations. It also 
highlights further improvements required by Jordanian industrial sector that can help enhancing 
the operational performance of firms. The focus of the research is to provide information to 
managers on how they can incorporate supplier integration and internal integration of their 
operations to improve their performance. It will highlight the relevant practical considerations 
for improving cost effectiveness and flexibility in organisations by effectively utilizing their 
internal resources and organising them effectively in line with the resource-based view of the 
firm.  This research also has significant contribution to attract foreign direct investment for 
Jordanian economy. By highlighting the key factors and their combined influence on the 
operational efficiency of the organisations in Jordan it enhances the knowledge of key 
 
13 
 
stakeholders of Jordan’s industries. The improvement in Jordanian industrial sector can be the 
source of attracting foreign investments that ultimately leads to economic development of the 
country. Hence, this research not also significantly contributes to the literature, but also to the 
economic development and growth of Jordan.

15 
 
Chapter 2  Literature Review  
TQM AND TPM  
This chapter critically reviews the extant literature regarding manufacturing practices (TPM, 
TQM) and their impact on the organisational operational performance. It also highlights the 
importance of understanding the relationships of manufacturing practices (i.e., TPM, TQM) 
and SCM (i.e., supplier integration, internal integration) along with operational performance 
(i.e., cost, flexibility) of the industrial firms. Therefore, the chapter focuses on discussing both 
TPM and TQM as a base to promote the ability of the industrial organisations to effectively 
reduce the cost and enhance the flexibility of their operational performance. Moreover, these 
manufacturing practices are discussed in light of the resource-based view as a theoretical lens 
that is adopted in research work.  
2.1.  Total Quality Management (TQM) 
The term ‘(TQM)’ is defined as a management approach that is focusing on the long-term 
success of an organisation by enhancing customer satisfaction through constant improvements. 
It is a management approach in which all members of an organisation participate in creating 
the improvement strategies for the products, processes, and overall organisational culture. It is 
a management system for integrating the quality discipline into the management culture of an 
organisation (Juran, 1988; Feigenbaum, 1991; Ross and Perry, 2003). TQM is a change 
management quality approach for improving effectiveness, flexibility, and competitiveness of 
the business in line with customer requirements. It focuses on the overall quality measures for 
an organisation with the aim of meeting customer expectations (Karyotakis and Moustakis, 
2014). It is a customer centred approach to the management of organisations that focuses on 
ensuring that their critical issues and needs are effectively addressed. The approach manages 
an organisation based on quality control, design, development, quality assurance and 
continuous improvement.   
2.2. The evolution of TQM 
Some of the first seeds of TQM were laid down as the principles of scientific management in 
the 1920s. However, the actual principles of TQM evolved from the quality assurance methods 
that developed during world war I in response to the needs of the war; factories engaged in 
large scale manufacturing of goods that were poor in quality. In response, quality inspectors 
 
16 
 
were introduced into the factories to correct the quality issues and ensure that failure levels due 
to quality would be minimised (Houston and Dockstader, 1998). After the war, quality 
inspection became a key element of manufacturing and was addressed through Edward 
Deming’s theory of statistical quality control. According to the theory, variations in the 
production processes contributed to the variability in the end product. This could be eliminated 
through a more standardised production processes to ensure high-quality products (Ooi et al., 
2008). 
After the world war II, manufacturers in Japan were producing poor quality goods and needed 
to design effective ways of controlling quality levels. Their response to the challenge was that 
the Japanese union of scientists and engineers invited Dr. Edwards Deming to provide training 
them on how to manage quality processes. This was a key stepping-stone for TQM as the 
quality control and statistical analysis methods were heavily adopted by Japanese executives 
and engineers in the end of the 1950s. Joseph M. Juran taught the concepts of quality control 
and managerial breakthrough with a focus on the reduction of defects. The Japanese managers 
coined the term quality management systems (Juran, 1989; Ross and Perry, 2003).  
By 1970, the Japanese named their approach to quality management, ‘companywide quality 
control’. These approaches were critical to the ascension of Japan as a leader in quality. The 
aspect of quality control involved all employees in an organisation from the top management 
to all low-level workers. The results seen in Japanese companies were a critical driver for the 
interest from other countries in the quality management strategies (Juran, 1989). During this 
period, the high quality of Japanese products that were made under the conditions of total 
quality control made Japan a key manufacturer of goods for the international market. The 
Japanese companies were producing high quality products at low prices, especially electronics 
and automobiles, which present a stiff competition for the United States and European nations 
(Houston and Dockstader, 1998). Companies in the United States started exploring the 
application of the quality control techniques and, thus, initiated the practices of TQM 
(Arumugam et al., 2008).  
The origin of the term TQM was in the 1980s and was inspired by a number of publications 
such as “What Is Total Quality Control? The Japanese Way” by Kaoru Ishikawa and Armand 
Feigenbaum “Total Quality Control”. In 1984, the US Navy asked civilian professionals to 
assess its statistical process controls and make recommendations on how to improve 
operational efficiency. It was recommended that they adopt the processes and work of Edwards 
 
17 
 
Deming and, as a result, they branded the approach ‘TQM’ in 1985 (Houston and Dockstader, 
1998).  The methods spread from the Navy throughout other arms of the federal government 
and the private sector. Organisations in the private sector found the TQM approaches to be 
highly significant in enabling them to compete and recapture their market share and bid for 
government contracts (Ahluwalia, 2008). 
2.3.  The value of TQM 
TQM is a management framework that focuses on improving the overall capacity of the 
employees to respond to the needs of customers. They have the mandate to ensure that 
organisational quality is managed in a systemic manner. It describes the quality policy of an 
organisation to develop clear strategies for defining how the employees work together to 
address them. TQM is a management system that focuses on different elements that enhance 
the value provided to organisations and customers (Bhat, 2010). These elements are highlighted 
in the eight principles of the practice: customer focused approach; total employee involvement; 
process thinking; an integrated system; a strategic and systemic approach; continual 
improvement; fact-based decisions and communication. These elements work together to 
improve consensus in the organisation, coordinate all aspects of the organisation and engage 
in continuous improvement efforts (Phan et al., 2019).  
TQM is a tool that directly influences operational performance in an organisation. This is 
achieved by focusing on employees to drive both quality product and service provision and 
efficient customer handling procedures (Ross and Perry, 2003). Putri, et al. (2017) reported 
that employees’ comfort and satisfaction in their jobs positively influences the firm operational 
performance. Employee job satisfaction includes actively involving these employees in the 
firms’ activities from the start to the end, which promotes the achievement of positive 
performance and higher firm operational performance. Firms that involve their employees in 
every phase of the production process usually report better financial performance and 
operational performance as opposed to their counterparts (Yu et al., 2021). Involving 
employees in the different production phases can also reduce wastage of materials and 
resources on unnecessary renovations. Employees are considered as a critical aspect of the 
TQM framework that promotes increased operational performance through ensuring that the 
hands-on work employees are enjoying their jobs and contribute to the achievement of firm 
objectives (Yu et al., 2015). 
 
18 
 
Further, TQM management aims at building corporate culture in which customer satisfaction 
is seen to be the focal point. Customer satisfaction through TQM is maximised through 
continuous improvements of work processes and the active involvement of employees in 
production. TQM is a long-term process that requires setting goals and working to achieve 
them through continuous improvement and revision of the included work processes. Sufa and 
Ambarsari (2020) observe that customer satisfaction is not only about involving the employees, 
but also the various stakeholders in the production process such as business partners, society, 
suppliers and owners. Incorporating and harmonizing the quality dimensions of stakeholders 
can go beyond increasing customer satisfaction through quality goods to ensure the improved 
operational performance through the multiple stakeholder investment efforts.   
The implementation of TQM in a firm is not enough as it requires establishing key success 
factors that are needed to support the critical role of TQM in achieving firm goals. TQM among 
other roles contributes to the quality control in firms, management of time and resource use, 
customer relationship and employee satisfaction. While these roles are quite clear in most of 
the firms that implement TQM, sustaining these benefits has proven to be a major hurdle for 
these firms. Thus, achieving performance or operational performance standards in a firm 
depends on its ability to establish and develop new attractive products that are competitive to 
excite customers and/or exceed the expectations of customers, form the ground-breaking 
climate that promotes business innovation and develop products that incorporate customer 
needs and concerns (Furterer and Elshennawy, 2005). Such firms can achieve the TQM through 
the development of business practices that regularly reduce defects in products while reducing 
waste and cycle time (Qazi and Simsekler, 2021). The establishment of strong relations with 
key suppliers increasing the number of committed employees in an organisation and equally 
provides avenues for achieving TQM practices.   
Due to such positive characteristics, organisations are currently prioritising the Quality 
management frameworks such as TQM. But still there is a lack of focus on establishing the 
contribution of TQM to operational performance due to the absence of the suitable performance 
indicators. Pertinent to the achievement of the TQM framework is the establishment of 
performance measurement indicators. This is because the performance indicators establish the 
guidelines to what to do and by whom it should be done. Juran (1995) established that 
operational performance can be categorised into five broad categories namely, competitive 
 
19 
 
performance, quality improvement, the performance of microprocessors, product performance, 
the performance of macro processes and the cost of poor quality (Truong et al., 2017).  
Firms that establish these performance indicators have clear guidelines on what is expected at 
every stage and therefore can monitor the success rate of the implementation process. The core 
principles and strategies, on which TQM operates, play a major role in enabling the 
organisations to achieve their goals of quality and responsiveness to customer needs. One of 
the key issues or values that TQM offers for organisations is associated with the costs involved. 
However, studies such as Lam et al. (2011) indicate that the direct and indirect costs of quality 
challenges present a significant problem that is greater than that of implementing TQM. TQM 
aids organisations to avoid the price of non-conformance, which consists of costs of prevention, 
appraisal and failure as presented in the PAF model. The prevention costs are incurred before 
the processes are undertaken and they consist of the design, implementation, and maintenance 
of the TQM system. These planned costs include material requirements, quality assurance and 
quality planning. Training and empowering employees contributes to enhancing the value they 
can provide for the organisation, including the provision of services (Psomas et al., 2017). 
Empowering employees to improve quality also contributes to higher levels of innovation in 
organisations (Matias and Coelho, 2014). This means that TQM contributes to a more 
motivated workforce that can effectively respond to quality issues in a holistic manner. 
Employee empowerment also helps controlling the failure costs of the organisations. The 
products and materials require appraisals to align with the specifications and this is effectively 
done by the employees when they are empowered to do this. Failure costs not only arise within 
the organisations but externally as well. This means that the costs such as waste, scrap and 
reworking can be eliminated with the development of an effective TQM framework 
(Mosadeghrad, 2012). Failure analysis is adopted as a key element of TQM and aids in 
identifying the root causes of quality problems in order to develop positive strategies for 
addressing them. A key aspect of TQM is that it leads to the development of an optimised 
supply chain that provides the producer the right material in the right time (Matias and Coelho, 
2014). The processes are also optimised to respond to customer requirements and produce 
commodities that meet their expectations, which lead to higher numbers of sales (Gharakhani, 
et al., 2013). 
Based on the aforementioned facts, it is noted that the major benefit of implementing TQM is 
the establishment of an employee empowered organisational environment, that leads to the 
 
20 
 
operational efficiency and ultimately customer satisfaction. In fact the total commitment of 
employees is critical to the success of TQM and, thus, the organisation as it improves their 
ability to address customer needs (Matias and Coelho, 2014). A committed workforce 
understands the vision and goals of the organisation and has the competence to work towards 
accomplishing them. Employees are given, and accept, the ownership of the different issues in 
the organisation. An organisation implementing TQM is a continually learning because the 
employees are encouraged to seek opportunities for improving their knowledge. This 
organisation should be, therefore, highly coordinated where employees can discuss problems 
and find suitable ways of how to address them (Gharakhani, et al., 2013). 
2.4.  Impact of Total Quality Management on Organisational Operational 
performance 
Organisations, across the world, now consider that effective quality management practices have 
become the need of organisations to stay competitive because globalization has not only 
brought opportunities for businesses but critical challenges as well. Especially, the 
manufacturing firms have to face many challenges to compete in the market and they could 
only cope with such challenges by improving their operational performance especially having 
a major focus on quality (Lee and Lee, 2014). Moreover, mounting competition in the 
international markets has compelled manufacturers that operational performance should not be 
the indicator of organisational efficiency but also the organisational effectiveness. Yet the 
difference between these two is usually overlooked. For example, if organisation is completely 
following its process specifications for production it is utilising its resources efficiently but if 
the organisation is not producing according to the demands and needs of customers it means 
the organisation fails to utilise its resources effectively. In fact, there is no point in churning 
out high-quality products that nobody wants (Yunis et al., 2013). Therefore, organisations are 
now required to majorly focusing on the demands and requirements of the customers which are 
dynamically changing day by day. Customer satisfaction has become the ultimate competitive 
advantage for organisations that they need to attain to remain competitive in their market 
(Wickramasinghe, 2012). 
Satisfied customers are the ultimate competitive advantage for the organisations so to retain 
the existing customers and attracting potential customers the organisations are always focused 
on delivering value to their customers that should not only be effective but also higher than the 
competitors. Even the customer satisfaction could be transformed into customer delight when 
 
21 
 
the provided value exceeds customers’ expectations (Wickramasinghe, 2012). Hence, 
developing and understanding of the customers’ wants and demands is crucial for 
organisations. When the organisations are successfully directing all of their efforts towards 
attaining such kinds of goals the operational performance would always be according to the 
expectations and wants of customers. This ultimately results in enhanced sales and operating 
profits for the organisations because in such cases the customers are ready to buy more and pay 
more for the quality products meeting their expectations and fulfilling their wants.  It also 
restrains inventory pileup due to rejected and returned products and unsold items as well as 
saves the warranty claims, recall costs and product liability costs (Nilda et al., 2017).  
To enjoy profitability, the organisations must acknowledge value and operational performance 
from customers’ perspective not from their engineering teams’ perspective. Therefore, the 
internal processes of the organisations must be mechanized towards producing the products 
and services that are useful and valuable according to their customers (Liu and Liu, 2014). The 
TQM philosophy ensures the effective efficiency and considers operational performance not 
only in terms of the high-quality end products, but the high-quality products and services 
valued by customers, and emphasises on enhancing organisational operational performance in 
this perspective. Hence, the TQM philosophy contributes in enhancing the organisational 
operational performance in different perspectives (Zeng et al., 2015). 
When organisations carefully take customer requirements into consideration, the operational 
performance of the organisations also aligns with the customer satisfaction. It also provides 
cost efficiencies by eliminating defective products, reworks cost and eventually saves the 
throughput time as well. Hence, customer focus significantly contributes to enhanced 
operational performance by eliminating the internal and external failure costs along with 
assurance of production of goods that are in demand (Khan, 2003). 
TQM practices help organisations attain their key objectives of customer satisfaction and profit 
maximisation by having a major focus on continuous work improvement. Lee and Lee (2014) 
indicate that TQM practices focus on continuous work improvement so various organisations 
implement these practices in various perspectives, but the major purpose remains same that is 
the satisfaction of organisations’ internal and external customers. A major part of the costs of 
total quality could not be controlled by the management alone unless the workers are involved, 
so TQM also focuses on employee empowerment and involvement to enhance organisational 
operational performance. Management alone can only aim for delivering the quality but 
 
22 
 
employee commitment to deliver quality actually results in achieving the goal (Rachmat, 
2019).  
When employees are themselves committed to deliver high quality, they take initiatives 
towards meeting process and product specifications, improvement of process and product 
designs, detection and elimination of bottle necks, and setting reasonable yet challenging 
targets. Employee empowerment encourages their involvement at critical stages of production 
as well as taking required initiatives without any hesitation. Employee involvement results in 
diversity of opinions and views based on diverse experiences of workers and results in creative 
ideas for enhancing customer satisfaction and ultimately fosters operational performance (Phan 
et al., 2011).  
In a similar context, Liu and Liu (2014) investigated the relationship between TQM practices 
and employees’ wellbeing and found that TQM practices have significant positive impact on 
employee job satisfaction and ultimately on operational performance of the organisations. The 
authors argue that quality management practices bring significant change in workplace 
characteristics that result in improvement of employees’ work-related attitudes and behaviours 
and their work life outcomes. It indicates that quality management practices not only focus on 
operational performance but also employee wellbeing that makes the employees more 
committed towards improving organisational operational performance.  
Different researchers have reported the implementation of TQM in different perspectives. The 
study conducted by Faeq, Garanti and Sadq (2021) offers an empirical basis for measuring and 
enhancing TQM practises in construction business performance. The study elaborated the 
TQM adoption in terms of management-leadership commitment, employee interactions, 
supplier management, project design, training, quality data and reporting, process 
management, continuous improvement, and customer focus using practical implications. It 
supports the claim that TQM practises help businesses perform at higher levels in a variety of 
areas, including the quality of work, external customer satisfaction, safety, market share, the 
effectiveness of planning, labour efficiency, the percentage of successful tenders or quality 
contractors selected, competency in managing human resources, risk management, and 
management (Faeq et al., 2021). 
Pambreni et al. (2019) indicate that TQM helps organisations defining their objectives and the 
ways to attain these objectives through efficiencies attained within the organisations. An 
 
23 
 
organisation that had implemented TQM had a strategic plan that included broad objectives, a 
vision and mission, as well as a set of activities that needed to be taken to accomplish the broad 
objectives. TQM is a method for defining the goals of an organisation, developing its policies, 
deciding how to get there, and managing the resources needed to carry out those plans. These 
steps help organisations perform better and more effectively in line with their successfully 
attained goals. It is used strategically based as one of the independent variables called top 
management commitment as the most crucial factor for the success of TQM. According to the 
study, senior management commitment has a crucial influence in the prediction of operational 
performance (Pambreni et al., 2019). 
Wickramasinghe (2012) argued that implementation of TQM practices also bring change in 
human resource function of the organisation. Human resource management practices become 
more effective, and employee oriented under the umbrella of TQM because the focus changes 
from only guiding employees to follow the instructions towards getting them involved and 
encourage their feedback and initiatives for improvement of processes and products. It 
ultimately results in enhanced organisational operational performance in effective as well as 
efficient manner. In a similar context, Lee and Lee (2014) argue that TQM practices progress 
employee empowerment and customer satisfaction in parallel. The authors argue that TQM 
practices not only focus on enhancement of customer satisfaction through enhanced quality 
and operational performance but also have significant focus on employee satisfaction assuming 
that satisfied employees would be able to satisfy the customers by delivering products that are 
most desired by customers. Hence, employee satisfaction leads in efficient organisational 
operational performance.  
The enhancement of organisational operational performance is among the key objectives of 
organisations which are ensured by the TQM practices of the organisations. The systematic 
management approach focused by TQM ensures that the operational performance of the 
organisation is quite consistent with the desired targets of operational performance i.e., the 
organisation is producing what it actually wants to produce. It also ensures the efficient 
utilisation of the resources as well as the achievement of efficiency through enhanced outputs 
while declining inputs (Khan, 2003).  
Under systematic management approach, organisational operational performance is enhanced 
through better products, improved responsiveness, shortening of cycle times for novel products 
and services, effective marketing, production, and engineering strategies. Moreover, by 
 
24 
 
elimination of errors, wastage and defects, the organisations are also capable of enhancing 
operational performance by having sufficient control on costs (Nilda et al., 2017). Businesses 
are motivated to submit applications for employee engagement initiatives in order to improve 
corporate performance, growth, and competitiveness on the local or international market. The 
success of TQM deployments depends heavily on the employee focus, which identified as one 
of the independent variables. According to the study, effective people management 
significantly correlated with rising customer happiness, employee morale, and improved 
production performance. One of the four TQM components, people management which is the 
source of managing flexibility and cost, significantly impacted both financial and operational 
performance (Pambreni et al., 2019). 
Hence, previous literature (Lee and Lee, 2014; Liu and Liu, 2014; Wickramasinghe, 2012) 
indicates that TQM have significant contribution in enhancement of organisational operational 
performance by having its focus on different organisational perspectives. Rachmat (2019) 
argue that TQM practices have significant relationship with operational performance of 
organisations by bringing changes in organisational strategy, structure, and performance. But 
at the same time, the effective implementation of these practices is very important. Al-Refaie 
et al. (2022) argue that implementation of TQM practices changes the employees’ level of 
learning and learning direction, hence leading towards improved knowledge implementation 
towards their tasks. It also brings the improvements in behavioural factors by influencing the 
work effort level of employees, hence significantly affecting the organisational operational 
performance.  
Despite having all the key benefits and characteristics reported by the researchers, the 
researchers also emphasise on focusing on the effective implementation of these practices. 
Nilda et al., (2017) argue that only adoption of TQM practices is not sufficient for the 
organisation, rather effective implementation of these practices plays a significant role. 
Organisations who fail to implement these practices on various aspects of organisation, do not 
achieve the desired results i.e., if the organisation is solely focusing on improvement of its 
operations and the end product, it could never be able to get the desired outcomes because 
TQM implementation focuses on all including operations, products, customers, and most 
importantly the employees of the organisation. Employees’ involvement is significantly 
important in effective implementation of these practices and achieving the desired outcomes of 
operational performance.  
 
25 
 
Some manufacturing companies apply the quality management practises incorrectly, and the 
primary cause of this situation is internal problems with their knowledge, understanding, and 
skills related to quality management practises. These issues result in the "use of the wrong tool 
to solve a problem," "use of the same tool to solve the entire problem," and "use of the same 
set of tools on each problem." (Sahoo and Yadav, 2018). Unfortunately, a lack of technical 
skills frequently leads to the ad hoc adoption of certain methods without the corresponding 
system-wide philosophy and ethos. External consultation may be used to overcome this lack 
of experience, but this alternative is frequently not available because these manufacturing 
SMEs would need financial resources both to pay consultants and to support the actual 
implementation of such ideas (Saleh et al. 2018). Most SMEs lack sound financial management 
skills and have inadequate funding arrangements. Another issue is that most SMEs offer quality 
management initiatives shorter time periods than they do elsewhere and apply them more 
quickly to deal with manufacturing SMEs add more capacity as their product design evolves. 
Ample financial resources are also needed for teaching individuals how to use TQM tools and 
processes. Therefore, inadequate training and a lack of resources are two main obstacles to 
implementing the TQM programme in SMEs (Asad et al., 2020). 
TQM techniques for the manufacturing sector require a lot of resources but eventually pay off.  
Manufacturing SMEs' entrepreneurs in India are unsure of the possible advantages of quality 
management systems as a result of these divergent points of view. Since the breadth of 
improvement through the application of TQM within the manufacturing firm is expanding, 
organisations may want to consider more modern methods of integrating the business 
operations outside of their physical boundaries (Asad et al., 2020). Businesses find it difficult 
to persuade and influence business partners to restructure their business operations and 
embrace a quality management philosophy, especially when dealing with such external ties. 
Additionally, SMEs might not have the market strength to persuade their networks of business 
partners, particularly their suppliers, to implement quality management and control procedures 
(Saleh et al. 2018). 
In a similar context, Zeng et al., (2015) argue that although the adoption and implementation 
of TQM practices is full of advantages for almost all kinds of organisations, but it requires 
critical analysis of the organisational structure, strategies, policies and working environment. 
Because any kind of change proposed for the organisation, even it’s in the favour of 
organisation and its staff, has to face reluctance at some initial stages, which most of the time 
 
26 
 
declines with the passage of time or by positive experiences of the organisations’ employees. 
Hence, adoption of TQM practices also requires effective implementation for getting desired 
outcomes. Otherwise, organisations may face even more difficulties and negative impact on 
operational performance. 
Emphasising on the effective implementation of the TQM practices, Khan (2003) argues that 
TQM practices could not be implemented without sufficient and relevant training of the 
employees being involved in the process. So, the training period may have negative impact on 
the operational performance of the organisation in a way that employees have to stop their 
regular work for the time of training, but the impact lasts for a short-term period i.e. only for 
the training period unless the employees again start accomplishing their routine tasks. Yunis 
and Chen (2013) also argue that TQM practices may also have negative impact on 
organisational operational performance in a way that it mostly leads towards incremental 
modifications on regular basis that may result in unfavourable reaction of individuals who are 
comfortable with the existing working environment. Their reactions or feelings may have 
negative influence on their performance and eventually affecting the overall operational 
performance of the organisations.  
Focusing on the effective implementation of the TQM practices, Wickramasinghe (2012) 
highlighted another key factor, the reluctance of employees.  The author argued that employees 
never welcome change immediately, although some of them adopt it with the passage of time 
while others still stay reluctant. Similarly, the structural and strategic changes due to the 
adoption and implementation of TQM practices may result in developing a feeling in 
employees that they may lose their jobs due to such changes. Such negative feelings also result 
in negative influence on the operational performance of these employees and having a 
significant negative impact on organisational operational performance.  
Contrary to the above arguments, Rachmat (2019) argue that negative impacts due to the 
adoption and implementation of TQM approaches are not long lasting. Some initial investments 
in terms of employee trainings and lost operational performance ultimately results in positive 
outcomes. Such practices not only improve the production processes by eliminating defects 
and delays but also help employees in improving their operational performance at individual 
levels. So, employees initially reluctant for adoption of such change gradually become the part 
of the process and contribute positively to enhancing organisational operational performance. 
 
27 
 
Researchers have reported different views about the adoption and implementation of the TQM 
practices. Some focusing on its key features, while the others focusing on its effective 
implementation. Some have even mentioned it as a fact that could never be ignored by the 
organisations for their survival in the market. Zeng et al. (2015) argue that TQM has become 
the essential element for the survival of organisations in manufacturing and services industries. 
Organisations who are successfully achieving efficient operational performance along with 
effective operational performance are able to gain the competitive advantage in the industry 
and efficient operational performance is achieved through effective implementation of TQM 
practices. Nilda et al., (2017) indicate that TQM practices significantly contribute to enhancing 
organisational operational performance through employee and customer satisfaction, so the 
benefit is enjoyed at the both ends. The aforementioned discussion indicates the significant role 
of TQM practices in the enhancement of organisational operational performance. Having 
significant focus on various determinants of organisational operational performance and 
providing an effective direction to all these resources, TQM not only enables organisations to 
achieve effective operational performance levels but also efficient operational performance 
according to the needs and wants of customers.    
2.5.  Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) 
The term ‘(TPM)’ is known as a system of plant maintenance for improving the effectiveness 
of production processes where maintenance is put into focus as a necessary part of the business 
and manufacturing process (Chandran, 2015). This element of productive maintenance is 
achieved through integration of assets, people, and processes to maximise the availability of 
equipment. TPM is defined as a productive maintenance approach that is accomplished by 
small groups of employees in an organisation (Madanhire and Mbohwa, 2015). In other words, 
TPM implies an innovative strategy to maintenance, which optimises systems functionality by 
including every employee in the maintenance practices. While increasing the effectiveness of 
systems, implementing TPM eliminates breakdowns by ensuring daily maintenance during 
routine operations. Ahuja and Khamba, (2008a) observe that TPM was developed from various 
productive maintenance concepts. The concepts behind the development of TPM include 
breakdown maintenance, preventive maintenance, corrective maintenance, productive 
maintenance, and computerised management system (Chandran, 2015). 
These concepts were combined under TPM as one holistic maintenance tool that focuses on 
the proactive approach to maintenance during daily operations in organisations. The theory 
 
28 
 
behind TPM holds that performance of a system depends on its capability and availability. 
TPM incorporates all employees into maintenance activities to enhance capacity and operations 
performance, which contributes to the overall system performance. Reliability, maintainability, 
and support performance are all functions of TPM (Madanhire and Mbohwa, 2015) that seek 
to provide a lasting solution to the system breakdown problems in firms. System performance 
theory provides two different approaches through which TPM can be implemented. First, a 
deductive approach, which starts by defining the quality standards to be maintained to ensuring 
that maintenance, is optimised. Secondly, the analytic approach where the areas of defect in a 
system are diagnosed and the right procedures for corrective measures are deployed to enhance 
the performance of operations (Singh et al., 2013). 
2.6.  The Evolution of TPM 
Seiichi Nakajima (1950) developed the concept of TPM in the 1950s although it was not known 
by that term. Nakajima is a Japanese engineer who worked as an interpreter for presentations 
by George Smith on preventive maintenance. Since Smith was at the forefront of the 
maintenance improvement approach of management, he provided inspiration to Seiichi 
Nakajima (1950). He focused on integrating the concepts of American preventive maintenance 
with other maintenance practices from his experience such as quality management, reliability 
engineering and operator assisted maintenance (Singh et al., 2013). The new approach that he 
developed was based on the 5S workplace organisation methodology. The approach consisted 
of five elements: sort; set in order; shine and organise; standardise and sustain. Considering the 
success of the approach, it was observed that a leadership mindset that involves frontline teams 
was essential to enhance small group’s performance in the maintenance of operations (Díaz-
Reza et al., 2019). 
The Japanese Institute of Plant Maintenance was a key player in developing the methodology 
beyond the work of Nakajima. It accomplished this by integrating the concepts of lean 
manufacturing into the new approach. The TPM process became widely accepted in 1971 with 
the first company to apply it as Nippondenso, which created components for Toyota. It was the 
first company to win the PM award, which was an internationally accepted TPM benchmark 
developed by JIPM (Díaz-Reza et al., 2019). The JIPM developed the eight pillars of TPM, 
focusing heavily on preventive and proactive maintenance techniques (Asefeso, 2014). 
The eight pillars of the method were autonomous maintenance, planned maintenance, focused 
improvement, quality management, equipment management, education, Administrative and 
 
29 
 
supportive TPM and safety (Prabhuswamy et al., 2013). The effectiveness of the method in 
developing more reliable plants promoted its increased adoption in manufacturing operations 
across the globe.  
2.7.  The Value of TPM 
TPM is about involving the employees in maintaining their equipment through emphasising 
proactive and preventive maintenance techniques. The basic goal of TPM is to develop a better 
production environment in which no defects are detected in the production and no breakdowns 
are happening to the equipment. It is also meant to improve operational efficiency by reducing 
downtime and enhancing the overall equipment effectiveness. The approach of TPM introduces 
the aspect of preventive and proactive maintenance where the operation of machinery is put at 
the core of production processes to maximise its availability (Asefeso, 2014). Efficient and 
effective use of the machinery in question is a key aspect of TPM and is addressed by having 
small multidisciplinary teams addressing core aspects of the production environment. The 
integration of a proactive and predictive maintenance approach is a key advantage of 
implementing TPM because it lowers the costs of repairs and reduces the expenses associated 
with unplanned downtime (Díaz-Reza et al., 2019; Ahuja and Khamba, 2008).  
A core component of TPM, 5S is a method of setting up the workplace to ensure consistent 
performance while including the workers. It is built around the following five fundamental 
tasks: (Sort, Set in Sequence, Shine, Standardize, and Sustain). Sorting entails tidying up the 
workspace and removing everything unnecessary. Determine the location and number of the 
necessary objects before setting the order. Shine entails fixing problems in the workplace and 
cleaning it up. Using standardised tools and practises, standardise documents and the work 
technique. Sustain refers to continuing to improve, assisting staff in forming ethical work 
practises, and finally incorporating 5S into corporate culture (Xiang and Feng, 2021).  
To close the skills and knowledge gap and boost employee morale and experience, education 
and training are essential to TPM (Méndez and Rodrguez, 2017). Adesta, Prabowo, and 
Agusman (2018). Employees progress through many skill phases across the pillar to become
