proficient The level of education and training must be evaluated by the training system in order 
to select the best training modules and schedule. Equipment operators, for instance, learn how 
to spot anomalies during routine and infrequent inspections, whereas equipment maintenance 
staff learns maintenance concepts and practises and develops specialised maintenance abilities. 
 
30 
 
The goal of the safety, health, and environment pillar is to find and remove occurrences that 
correspond to these goals in order to create the perfect workplace (Asad et al., 2020).  
The overall equipment efficiency arising from the implementation of TPM results in an 
environment, where production schedules are not altered. This means that the potential of 
customer complaints declines significantly with the adoption of TPM. The focus of the 
approach on health and training of the employees is a major aspect that contributes to reduction 
of workplace accidents and their associated costs. With this in mind, the overall manufacturing 
costs of a plant decline considerably (Méndez and Rodrguez, 2017). The implementation of 
TPM has also been found to have a wide range of indirect benefits such as increased product 
quality and employee confidence levels. Since the employees are trained and responsible for 
the maintenance of the different equipment, they are more confident about the capacity of the 
machinery as well as their skills for maintaining the working order of the equipment (Arthur et 
al., 2015). The adoption of TPM incorporates ownership over all core elements of production 
processes. This means that employees have positive attitudes towards the organisation and 
production processes in general. Since TPM involves cross-disciplinary teams, it triggers 
higher levels of knowledge and experience sharing across departments, which contributes 
heavily to promoting the capacity of the organisation and its innovative practices. 
The establishment of cross disciplinary teams also helps boosting employees’ morale. The 
breakdown of machines on repeated occasions negatively affects staff morale and reduces the 
production capacity of the firm. Initiating the TPM approach to business modifies the 
employees’ mindset and promotes a work culture those values getting the work done in a more 
efficient manner (Ahuja and Khamba, 2008). This is achieved through engaging functions from 
all organisational levels in the production equipment to enhance overall production efficiency. 
Initially, maintenance is the role of the maintenance department to ensure the prevention of 
machines collapse and production systems failure. Recently, this narrative is seen to be less 
viable and, thus, it has been changed through using TPM equipment to engage every individual 
in an organisation in the maintenance activities (Asefeso, 2014). Furthermore, TPM involves 
all employees in the overall maintenance approach of an organisation. Agustiady and Cudney 
(2018) added that the key strength of TPM is the ability to coordinate all vendors, operators 
and maintenance personnel together in various events. Team working in the various level of 
the organisations, as well as promoting zero breakdowns and product defects, helps to ensure 
that the operational performance is achieved. The outcome for the organisation is zero defects 
 
31 
 
and breakdowns and, hence, higher efficiency. The higher efficiency coupled with the reduced 
wastage and costs that are associated with the absence of defects make the operations more 
profitable (Chandran, 2015). 
Based on these facts, the businesses need to focus on TPM for effective management of their 
operations and production. TPM is highlighted as the process of using different machines, 
equipment, employees and supporting processes. When these mechanisms are used, they help 
in maintaining or ensuring there is an improvement in quality as well as integrity in the 
production units. In simple terms, TPM is the mechanism or the process of getting the different 
employees involved in ensuring that they maintain their own equipment. In the process, the 
employees are trained or emphasised that they should be proactive and have preventive 
maintenance techniques. The TPM is an aspect that focuses on the achievement of perfect 
maintenance within the production unit and brings about growth, not only among employees, 
but also in the institution at large (Al Maazmi and Al Hamadi, 2021). The main point of focus 
is that the process will ensure the following: 
1. 
There will be no breakdowns. 
2. 
The process will be smooth with no stops or slow procedures in the organisation. 
3. 
There will be strategies to ensure that there are no defects. 
4. 
There will be elimination and, thus, no accidents at all. 
In general, improving the Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) through the application of 
TPM is something that is done after having to form small and effective teams. These teams 
must be multidisciplinary to help addressing the different core areas as preventive and 
automotive maintenance processes (Lackie and Murphy, 2020). The institutions also must train 
the employees who can operate machinery and improve on security in the institution. These 
combination processes bring about standardization in work processes. The issue of TPM is an 
aspect that focuses on efficient as well as effective use of the different means of production. It 
means that all the departments should be combined and involved in the process. In this case, 
small teams can work together for the purpose of increasing operational performance and, at 
the same time, can help in decreasing downtime mechanisms through a focus on equipment 
reliability.  
 
32 
 
From the above statements, the goal of total productive management is to ensure that there is 
an improvement of operational performance through having to reduce any aspects of drawdown 
that might occur in the company. Downtime is reduced by making sure that there is an 
implementation of strong TPM programs, which has a great impact on the equipment 
effectiveness. Therefore, for the success of these mechanisms, the idea of preventive 
mechanisms and maintenance should be prioritised in everybody’s mind (Lackie and Murphy, 
2020). Working as a collaborative approach enhances better growth and brings about 
satisfaction in the institution (Shinde and Prasad, 2018). An example to note is the running of 
machines with having the mindset of ‘we’ll fix it’ when the machine breaks should not be 
considered as options when handling the TPM. Using the TPM programs helps in eradicating 
the mind-set and turns the statement by putting the machinery having strong core focus on two 
main issues that is operation and attainment of available resources. 
2.8. Impact of Total Productive Maintenance on Organisational 
Operational performance 
Thakur and Panghal (2021) argue that in contrast to TQM in Lean philosophy which 
emphasises on improved quality through having a major focus on relevant processes, TPM 
emphasises on improved operational performance depends on the performance of equipment’s. 
In fact, in recent advancements the major focus is to make the production process more labour 
free (unmanned) owing to automation and robotics in manufacturing industries. Such 
automation has also made the equipment’s even more complicated hence the resulting 
production outcomes are becoming dependent on the quality and performance of this complex 
equipment’s.  
Attaining perfect levels of targeted organisational operational performance along with zero, 
defects, break downs and accidents, looks like a dream for most of the organisations, but they 
can make this dream come true through the effective implementation of TPM. Nallusamy 
(2016) argue that TPM facilitates organisations in achieving their desired operational 
performance levels through reducing unplanned maintenance time, improving working 
environment safety, enhancement of quality outputs and proven impact through equipment 
effectiveness.  
But this is also a key fact that the deployment of TPM requires a complicated system with 
significant capital outlay. Procedure and TPM implementation should be carefully chosen in 
order to obtain the best economic benefits. To successfully apply TPM in Indian SMEs, top 
 
33 
 
management must possess a high level of maturity. At the sub-system level in this study, the 
major enablers are motivation, total staff involvement, employee empowerment, team spirit, 
good attitude, varied maintenance systems, education and training, available resources, etc. 
Therefore, it is crucial to comprehend the nature of different enablers and their effects on the 
effective implementation of TPM in Indian SMEs (Jain et al., 2018). 
Therefore, TPM have a significant focus on the maintenance methodology and improvement 
of maintenance skills hence leading towards enhanced operational performance and has 
become the need of the era.  TPM significantly influences the organisational operational 
performance in different ways. Just like TQM, total operational performance maintenance 
approach also greatly emphasises on employee empowerment and encourages providing the 
ownership of machinery to the employees so that the production uptime could be enhanced 
(Chaurey et al., 2021).  
Therefore, instead of allocating the jobs separately to the maintenance personnel, these 
particular jobs are allocated to the plant personnel so that individuals operating machinery or 
plants become responsible for up keeping their equipment’s and machinery. It enhances 
operational performance in two ways; the operators take care of basic manufacturing 
maintenance and keep their equipment’s and machinery in operating condition which saves 
maintenance time as well as eliminate delays for maintenance. On the other hand, it frees up 
the maintenance staff for performing more value-adding services. In this way, organisations 
are able to reduce unplanned maintenance time by adopting TPM approach (Mendez and 
Ramon, 2017).  
To enhance the efficiencies of the maintenance staff, TPM approach incorporates four stages, 
including preparation, introduction, implementation, and institutionalisation (Eugen, 2010 as 
cited by Xiang and Feng, 2021). Since the introduction of the TPM, its implementation process 
has gone through several developments, including the model developed by Sharma and Sharma 
(2013) using DMAIC approach. Similarly, the twelve implementation steps of Nakajima's 
(1988) TPM approach were expanded upon by (Jain et al., 2018) to create a rather 
comprehensive TPM technique. Despite having bit differences in the focus of its 
implementation, the process starts in a similar manner in all contexts. The process begins with 
a suitable announcement of TPM, followed by education, a support system, the establishment 
of policies and goals, a deployment strategy, the implementation of several pillars, and, lastly, 
continual development. A year later, they (Jain et al., 2018) presented a considerably simpler 
 
34 
 
approach that consists of six steps: data gathering, staff training via technical seminars, trial 
runs, fine-tuning, and final implementation. 
Despite having detailed elaboration of the approach, its adaptation and implementation is not 
an easy task for organisations.  The key hurdle for its adoption is the reluctance of employees 
due to their hesitation at the very initial stages of the process.  The key people mainly reluctant 
about its adoption are the operators due to their perception of additional responsibilities being 
assigned to them in terms of maintenance of machinery. But the positive outcomes with the 
passage of time make the employees convinced for adoption of this approach because it 
enhances their operational performance. So, employees initially hesitant of the adoption of this 
approach, later on feel proud of taking the possession of their machines as their own asset that 
results in enhanced operational performance (Thakur and Panghal, 2021). 
In this way, the TPM approach contributes positively to organisational operational performance 
by providing a better and safer working environment. The continuous up keeping of machinery 
and equipment’s by the operators’ results in clean and vigorous machines that are less likely to 
have issues like lubricant leakage, loose ball bearings, or any other kinds of hidden issues. In 
this way, it reduces the risks of injuries which are more likely to happen in dirty working 
environments with poorly working and disruptive machineries and equipment’s (Nallusamy, 
2016). Moreover, the disruptions of equipment’s and machineries also negatively influence the 
resulting operational performance from these equipment’s and machines; hence continuous 
maintenance by the operating staff through TPM approach eliminates such delays and 
contributes positively in operational performance (Chaurey et al., 2021).  
Another significant contribution of the TPM approach to the operational performance of 
organisations is its significant focus on quality maintenance which leads to quality 
enhancements of the production processes. Therefore, TPM approach is found to have 
significant positive contribution in customer satisfaction through improved quality of 
organisational operational performance. By having a focus on enhanced quality, the approach 
emphasises on detection of defects within the production processes and then assigning the 
quality component tasks for individual points of production (Mendez and Ramon, 2017). These 
checkpoints at different levels of production processes result in quality outputs which help in 
eliminating customer complaints and attaining the core purpose of customer satisfaction. It also 
helps enhancing employees’ morale through positive experiences of eliminating reworking and 
headaches of dealing with defects and complaints (Thakur and Panghal, 2021).  
 
35 
 
By enhancing the efficiencies of the workers in eliminating the defects and complaints, TPM 
approach helps organisations in attaining their objectives of perfect production through overall 
equipment effectiveness which facilitates organisations in quick identification of loss areas, 
benchmarking the progress and enhancement of equipment operational performance. It also 
helps identifying the performance of organisation in comparison to the industry to find out the 
effectiveness of the TPM approach adopted by the organisation (Chaurey et al., 2021). Hence, 
TPM approach enhances equipment steadfastness by taking its ownership and developing the 
feelings of pride in its up keeping. Implemented effectively, the TPM approach leads to 
quantifiable sustained outcomes including enhanced quality outputs, enhanced manufacturing 
maintenance regime, declined change over and a proactive environment of taking pride of the 
machinery (Thakur and Panghal, 2021).  
For motivation of employees to be the proud part of such proactive environment, TPM has a 
major focus on eight key areas, which are the pillars of TPM approach including Autonomous 
maintenance, scheduled maintenance, quality maintenance, targeted improvement, equipment 
management, training and education, environmental health and safety, and administration. The 
full equipment life cycle is the subject of TPM procedures. Support from top management is a 
crucial differentiator for TPM. Every level of the organisation needs to be on board. As a result, 
top management must closely coordinate with front-line managers, middle management, and 
support roles. Through involvement in important events, TPM brings all employees together, 
including vendors, operators, engineers, and maintenance staff. TPM encourages and puts into 
practise team-based activities at all organisational levels with the same objective in mind: zero 
breakdowns and defects. (Nurprihatin, et al., 2019) 
Although maintenance of equipment by the operators doesn’t result in avoiding every failure, 
but the development of checkpoints facilitates the production personnel in determination of the 
best course of action that should be taken when machines go down, help them identification of 
the reason of breakdown and assessment of the requirement for external advice (Nallusamy, 
2016). TPM is a Lean tool that has been widely used. The overall equipment efficiency (OEE) 
indicator serves as its foundation. Three distinct factors—availability, performance, and quality 
ratio—are used to create this index. TPM is suited for economic analysis thanks to the OEE 
index since it allows for the quantification of benefits in monetary terms that management can 
comprehend. The cost of quality (COQ) model, commonly incorporating opportunity costs 
(Zeng and Matsui, 2015). 
 
36 
 
By enabling the employees to have a significant focus on the elimination of failures and 
empowering them to take the best course of action, TPM helps organisations in enhancing 
organisational operational performance in different perspectives like eliminating defects, 
reducing the maintenance time and cost through continuous up keeping of the equipment’s, 
enhanced working of the equipment’s and machinery due to their good working condition 
maintained by the operators, hence resulting in increasing the resulting outputs generated by 
these equipment’s and machines(Mendez and Ramon, 2017).  Moreover, it enhances the 
efficiency of the operators because when it becomes the responsibility of the operators, they 
operate the equipment’s and machines with enhanced care, hence reducing delays.  
Employees’ self-maintenance helps eliminating the equipment issues as well. Equipment issues 
reduce productivity, leading to a lot of workarounds and extended production timelines. When 
deadlines are barely reached, workers get overworked, scheduling is poor, and processes are of 
poor quality. In such cases, equipment will start to deteriorate. These dangers can be avoided 
by decreasing or eliminating downtime and improving your effective working time. Failures 
of tools, unforeseen maintenance, overheated bearings, and motor failure are a few examples 
of breakdowns. There are many setups and adjustments, such as setup/changeover, material or 
operator shortages, significant adjustments, and warm-up periods. Next, minor adjustments, 
sensor blockages, delivery blockages, component jams, and cleaning/checking are examples of 
small stops. Incorrect setting, equipment wear, alignment issues, start-up problems, scrap, and 
rework are a few examples of slow running. Finally, rework and scrap are examples of 
production problems (Agustiady, and Cudney, 2018). Longer manufacturing activities with 
many more stoppages and modifications characterise traditional maintenance, whereas TPM 
involves operator interaction and communication, results in fewer stoppages, and emphasises 
more predictive and proactive maintenance. 
On the other hand, Thakur and Panghal (2021) indicate that although TPM contributes 
positively to organisational operational performance, but at its initial stages it is not free from 
risk. As the operators become responsible for regular cleaning and maintenance of the 
equipment and machines, some of them may be just aware of the particular working of machine 
while lacking knowledge about its parts. Hence, making them responsible may create some 
technical problems at initial stage unless the operators become completely aware of the 
handling of equipment’s and its parts. Hence, adoption of such approaches requires appropriate 
 
37 
 
planning and effective implementation otherwise organisations may also face loss instead of 
desired benefits.  
TPM encourages group activities, such as sharing responsibility for routine inspection, an 
immediate repair procedure, cleaning, and organisation, to increase the effectiveness of the 
machines and equipment. TPM helps enhance employee morale within the organisation while 
also playing a significant role in scheduling the maintenance process and enhancing production. 
It increases employee accountability for the equipment they utilise, which lengthens the 
equipment's lifespan (Chaurey et al. 2021).  
Focusing on the contribution of TPM to the operational performance of organisations, 
Chaureyet al. (2021) argue that approaches like TPM are always in favour of the organisations 
by contributing positively towards organisational operational performance. Despite facing 
some challenges at initial stages of the adoption of such approaches, when effectively 
implemented these approaches successfully attract employees of the organisations towards the 
resulting benefits. The enhanced operational performance level at employee as well as 
organisational level itself becomes the convincing tool for organisational employees and they 
proudly become the part of the approach. Hence, initially getting employee satisfaction, the 
TPM approach leads towards customer satisfaction through effective and efficient operational 
performance particularly targeting customers wants.  
Hence, all above discussion indicates that TPM contributes significantly to enhancing 
organisational operational performance. In recent competitive environment, organisational 
operational performance may help organisations attaining the competitive advantage if 
organisations are able to utilise their resources effectively and efficiently, so TPM helps 
organisations in transforming their effective organisational operational performance into 
efficient organisational operational performance.  
2.9.  Operational Performance 
Operations performance can be maximised through the modification of several factors in the 
firm. On the top of these factors is the SCM through integration efforts. In the production 
process, supplier integration has been established as a paramount strategy to enhance flexibility 
and contribute to the achievement of operational standards. Coupling technical and managerial 
aspects with supply chain integration enhances the streamlining of day-to-day operations and 
improves operations quality (Juran, 1986). Businesses face several challenges that need supply 
 
38 
 
chain intervention in the initial stages to reduce the adverse effects associated with these 
challenges (Abualoush et al., 2018). For example, the breakdown of machinery and its parts 
has been recognised as a rampant cause of halt in many stoppages of work processes in firms. 
Top management of a firm plans for the allocation of financial, building, and physical material 
and human resources. They plan for eradication of resource wastage practices; however, they 
find these efforts futile when the supply chain is eliminated from the equation (El-Kassar and 
Singh, 2019). Supply chain integration contributes largely to the reduction of the breakdown 
of the production processes those results from resource scarcity and wastage.   
Supply chain integration incorporates the interaction of key stakeholders at different end of the 
supply chain, to achieve the enhanced efficiencies at different levels. Supplier integration 
contributes positively to the reduction in the breakdown of the production process by 
controlling inventory. For example, some production lags are usually caused by the breakdown 
of machines or some of their parts. Supply chain integration provides avenues for achieving 
organisational goals through managing inventory; that is the firm does not have to keep excess 
inventory for the supplies that are not needed in the production process. Instead, firms can only 
order the most important parts of the production process whenever the need arises. Again, 
supplier integration in firms promotes corporation among various employees in an organisation 
and also reduces the costs of mistakes that are committed at work due to faulty processes. 
Further, it ensures that the total cost of quality is kept to it minimum levels (Dubey et al., 2018). 
Supplier integration, thus, promotes operational performance in firms by bringing all essential 
stakeholders together in the production process.  
Operational performance of the organisation is focused through different perspectives by 
different organisations, including cost, quality, speed, flexibility, and dependability. Out of 
which when businesses directly compete on rates, cost is a crucial consideration. A corporation 
can cut its customer-facing prices by keeping production costs as low as possible. Information 
for internal users is produced by cost management. Cost management identifies, gathers, 
measures, classifies, and reports data that is helpful to organisational management in figuring 
out the cost of goods, clients, and suppliers, as well as other pertinent objects, and in planning, 
managing, making continuous advancements, and making decisions. The focus of cost is 
substantially broader than that of conventional costing systems. In addition to the price of 
anything, it is also interested in the elements that influence the price, such as cycle time, product 
quality, and process productivity. Consequently, cost management needs a thorough grasp in 
 
39 
 
industrial sector (Zhang et al., 2018).  Therefore, effective cost management necessitates a 
thorough knowledge of the organization's cost structure. The ability of management to 
calculate both the long-term and short-term costs of actions and processes which helps to 
examine the root causes of these costs, and these must also be properly investigated. 
Following that, flexibility is a consistent indicator of manufacturing business performance and 
a crucial component of efficiency (Nayal et al., 2022). SCs can maintain their efficiency and 
competitiveness in a globalised market without sacrificing performance by maintaining 
flexibility inside their organisations. Due to changing demand, manufacturing organisations 
that don't invest in innovation will experience operational delays (Nayal, et al., 2022). 
Therefore, firms consider flexibility as an important process in determining the strategies for 
TQM and TPM.  The other factors like speed, quality depends on the cost and flexibility 
somehow, and if the cost would be less, the stakeholders will motivate to produce the good 
quality products and they will buy products frequently which help them to improve their profit 
maximization.  
For this purpose, firms tend to keep their suppliers closer to amplify the realisation and 
acceptance of new realities in the business field. The supply chain is an important source of 
information needed to increase firms’ capabilities and the chance of meeting and exceeding the 
current demands. During the design phase, supplier participation enhances customised quality 
in products due to their rich information on customers. Large firms in the global market space 
have reported increased success of operations due to the established strong relationships with 
suppliers, which have not only provided them with quality supplies, but also gained from the 
large economies of scale. While this integration environment serves to advance the 
competitively of businesses, small and medium-sized enterprises have reported fewer positive 
contributions of supplier integration due to their inability to establish such stable relationships. 
In this regard, SMEs adopt contrary perspectives on the benefits associated with supplier 
integration due to their sizes. 
All organisations including SMEs mainly focus on increasing quality and productivity, 
decreasing inventories, the production line, processing space and time and equipment 
downtime, lean procedures lower total operating expenses (Bento and Tontini, 2018). They 
also ensure excellent operational performance. Internal lean methods have good associations 
with quality, delivery, flexibility, and cost. Quality, delivery, cost, and flexibility are four 
operational performance factors that have been linked favourably.  According to prior research, 
 
40 
 
operational performance is comprised of cost, new products, flexibility, quality, delivery, 
overtime, inventory, turnover, lead time and setup (Bento and Tontini, 2018). 
However, the effectiveness of operational performance depends on the implementation of 
different strategies to achieve superior performance. This has been an issue of frequent concern 
among managers who are seeking ways of ensuring operational and organisational success. An 
important issue in assessing the performance of operations is the consideration of performance. 
The impact of different management practices in assessing the performance of operations is 
mixed according to a wide range of studies focusing on financial performance (Bronzo et al., 
2013). Some of the key reasons behind the absence of a unified view about the impact of the 
financial measures on organisational performance are that a wide range of factors affects 
financial performance. This makes it difficult to isolate and control the main effects associated 
with management practices or other factors (Hwang et al., 2020). Identifying the interactions 
among the financial variables is difficult and raises the complexity of adopting the different 
performance measures (Bronzo et al., 2013).  
Organisations are well aware of the key fact that operational performance is the backbone of 
excellence for any organisation, which involves the capacity to fulfil the mission and dedication 
to meet its objectives. According to Van Looy and Shafagatova (2016), operational 
performance refers to the performance of an organisation in line with its standards such as 
operational performance, cycle time, waste reduction an environmental responsibility. 
Operational performance involves achieving a high level of effectiveness and efficiency in 
achieving the operational standards in order to meet customer needs. Neely (2003) focused on 
financial performance as a factor that negatively affects the capacity of a manager to identify 
areas of weakness and opportunities for operational improvements. As a result, an organisation 
tries to improve areas of operational performance to aid in improving its corporate strategy.  In 
line with the corporate strategy, an organisation should identify the relevant operational 
performance objectives. The five main performance objectives for a firm include quality, 
speed, flexibility, costs, and dependability (Dumas et al., 2013). While the five aspects of 
operational excellence help to achieve better operational performance, this review focuses on 
the cost and flexibility as the most significant factors that are associated with TPM and TQM 
implementation (Hwang et al., 2020). 
 
41 
 
2.10. Cost 
In recent dynamic market environments, organisations are not only focused on sustaining their 
competitive position in the market, but at the same time the core purpose of profit maximisation 
is also the key focus. For this purpose, the operational performance of organisations is also 
focused in terms of the costs associated with them (Thakur and Panghal, 2021). The objective 
of using the cost includes the assessment of how much the unit cost of a product varies. This is 
measured based on changes in a variety of factors such as the volumes produced and the 
diversity of the product portfolio. Products with greater variety are produced in lower volumes 
and have higher unit costs. The cost of a product includes factors such as the use of raw 
material, equipment, and correction of errors. The cost of a product affects the sales price and 
the margins obtained by a firm from the particular product (Van Looy and Shafagatova, 2016). 
On the other hand, cost as a measure of operational excellence refers to the assessment of the 
expenses incurred by an operation in achieving its objectives. The day-to-day operations of an 
organisation have to be addressed through strategies that enable maintaining costs at relatively 
low levels. The cost performance as an aspect of operational performance involves the 
assessment of additional costs such as reworking and warranty costs that are associated with 
errors and defects. Cost is cited as a key aspect of the quality of output because it refers to the 
ability of an operation to produce consistent quality at low expenditure. The cost performance 
of an operation involves the assessment of its alignment with the objective of achieving cost 
savings by eliminating unnecessary expenses (Hwang et al., 2020). 
The cost incurred in producing an output involves a wide range of considerations such as the 
cost of material, equipment operation and maintenance and conformance with quality 
standards. Since organisations seek to generate financial returns for their shareholders, they 
operate with the aim of lowering and optimising their operating costs. Optimising costs is 
another key element of operational excellence that leads to efficiency. Cost competitiveness of 
an operation contributes to its efficiency and effectiveness in addressing the needs of customers 
and achieving the goals of the shareholders. As a result, the aspect of cost performance is given 
a lot of significance as an objective of operational performance (Van Looy and Shafagatova, 
2016). 
Following the beliefs of TQM that prevention of defects is better than dealing with failures, 
hence the cost of defect prevention is cheaper for organisation than the cost of quality failures. 
 
42 
 
In other words, if organisations successfully attain zero defects level, then the total quality costs 
incurred by the organisation are minimised. Some important quality costs incurred by 
organisations are prevention cost, internal failure cost, appraisal cost and external failure cost 
(Zeng and Matsui, 2015). 
In this regard, Rachmat (2019) argue that organisations must incur cost to make efforts for 
reducing poor quality termed as the prevention cost. It includes improvements in the designs 
of the products to make them durable, providing training to the employees for enabling them 
to perform a good job, certifying suppliers for ensuring the supply of high-quality products and 
services by the suppliers. Prevention costs also include the costs incurred on the preventive 
maintenance of the machines and equipment’s as well as the documentation of the quality 
procedures and improvements in the production processes. Organisations that do not adopt 
TQM practices, fail to focus on the prevention cost that usually incorporates a very minor 
proportion of total quality cost (Khan, 2003). 
Researchers have identified different types of costs in this regard. Phan et al. (2011) indicate 
that another major type of quality cost incurred by organisations is the appraisal cost which 
includes the costs incurred on the testing and inspection of raw material, finished goods and 
the work in process. Moreover, appraisal cost also incorporates the costs incurred on statistical 
control processes, sampling, and quality audits. These are some kinds of costs that 
organisations must incur for getting desired outcomes like testing and inspection of raw 
material is important because quality products could not be achieved with substandard raw 
material. Moreover, if quality defects are identified in the final product before delivering the 
product to the customer, organisations incur this quality cost known as the internal failure cost. 
Internal failure costs are incurred in reworking of the product, scrapping the product, or it may 
be in the form of lost operational performance due to labour errors or any kind of machine 
break downs (Nilda et al., 2017).  
Rachmat (2019) argue that internal failure costs are usually more expensive than other types of 
quality costs because before the detection of defect a great deal of labour and material has 
already been invested on the product. Hence, organisations implementing TQM practices could 
eliminate such costs through continuous improvement at various stages of production (Chaurey 
et al., 2021). Moreover, empowered employees could also help eliminating these costs by 
detecting defects and taking appropriate actions at any initial stages of the production process 
before reaching the product at its final stages.  
 
43 
 
Focusing on the internal failure costs of operations, Nilda et al. (2017) argue that TPM helps 
organisations eliminating the internal failure costs especially that are incurred due to machine 
break downs because when equipment’s and machinery are under supervision of the operators, 
they keep these equipment’s well maintained and in efficient working conditions hence 
eliminating the defects in operational performance. Similarly, if any problem occurs, they are 
able to resolve it immediately without disturbing the production process and the final products. 
Hence, as a result the final products are always defecting free. Similarly, researchers have also 
highlighted the impact of productive maintenance on external failure costs. 
Khan (2003) argues that TQM practices and TPM also help organisation eliminating the 
external failure costs. External failure costs are the quality costs bear by the organisations in 
case the defect in products is identified by the customers after product delivery. It is considered 
as one of the most expensive classes of quality costs. It includes product returns, warranty 
claims, repairs and most importantly the lost reputation and ultimately the loss of business of 
the organisation. Hence, quality management and TPM help organisations eliminating these 
costs by ensuring high quality end products being delivered to the customers.  
Highlighting the impact of manufacturing practices on the quality of products that ultimately 
helps organisations achieving their cost objectives (Rachmat, 2019) argue that organisations 
must adopt TPM and TQM approaches for ensuring the quality of products being delivered to 
the customers because in other case they must face loss in the shape of quality costs incurred 
in different forms. More importantly, the costs associated with the satisfaction of customers 
must be borne by the organisations by fixing the problems at design or some initial phase, 
rather than dealing with it when the product is in customers’ hands.  
In a similar context, Phan et al. (2011) argues that if implemented effectively and consistently 
over time, TQM can diminish costs across the organisation especially in the areas of field work, 
scrap, re-work, and reduction of warranty costs. Such kinds of costs reduction do not require 
any additional costs incurred by the organisations, so in this way the TQM approach helps 
organisations in enhancing profitability. On the other hand, Yunis et al. (2013) argue that 
despite having several benefits in the form of reduced costs by implementation of the TQM 
practices, but such practices initially require significant training of the employees being 
involved in the process. Since the training requires employees being taken away from their 
regular responsibilities so it may have a short-term negative impact on the costs. Similarly, 
TQM practices also require incremental changes on a continuous basis so it may also result in 
 
44 
 
adverse reaction of some employees who are in favour of prevailing system, or by developing 
a feeling of losing their jobs. Such kinds of negative reactions may result in enhanced costs by 
increasing employee turnover and increased costs of hiring and training of new individuals.  
2.11. Flexibility 
Operational flexibility refers to the ability of operations to respond to the changing needs and 
requirements of manufacturing and production (Yu et al., 2021). The changes in manufacturing 
and production of organisations occur due to the changes in the market. For compete the market 
and to sustain their competitive advantage organisations need to be flexible in their operations. 
Operational flexibility could never be achieved by the organisations unless they have effective 
and efficient manufacturing practices (Yu et al., 2015). Flexibility in operations refers to the 
capacity to configure and vary product lines in order to deal with different customer 
requirements. An operation is effective if it has capacity of an operation to alter its product 
lines quickly to respond to new customer requirements (Bronzo et al., 2013). Quick adjustment 
to requirements is associated with the speed objective. A high performing operation should be 
able to produce different quality products and adapt its operations in line with market 
conditions and demands. Changing the operational strategies of a firm improves its ability to 
suit different delivery schedules without suffering stock outages or supply chain issues (Dumas 
et al. 2013; Hwang et al., 2020). Some of the key performance indicators in this dimension of 
operational performance include the time it takes for the operation to respond to customer 
demands (Dumas et al. 2013; Van Looy and Shafagatova 2016). 
Other key aspects of flexibility, as a key aspect of operational performance, include volume 
flexibility and delivery flexibility. These refer to the ability of an operation to alter its volumes 
or production levels in line with the demands in the market. Volume and delivery flexibility 
are related since delivery flexibility focuses on the altering of production schedules to ensure 
that products are delivered to customers at the most appropriate time. Product/service flexibility 
involves the adaptability or agility of a production system to change the product mix in line 
with customer expectations (Bronzo, et al., 2013). The element of product mix flexibility 
focuses on having a production system or process that is agile in terms of quickly changing 
what it produces at low costs. Product introduction flexibility as an element of the flexibility 
dimension is a complex aspect because it involves having the speed and capacity to introduce 
a new product/service to the market. This aspect affects the time to market for a product by 
having systems that are dynamic and responsive enough to ensure that a new product can be 
 
45 
 
integrated effortlessly to the product portfolio. The production processes should be agile in 
terms of equipment and personnel interaction to enable a new product to be quickly researched, 
designed, developed and introduced to the market (Van Looy and Shafagatova, 2016). All this 
depends on the operational flexibility of the organisations. 
Operational flexibility helps organisations remain competitive in dynamic market 
environments. According to the competition-based approach, an organization's primary goal is 
to maximise long-term profit and create a durable competitive edge over rivals in the external 
marketplace. This shows that using a marketing-focused strategy helps the business keep on 
top of its rivals and find ways to serve clients more effectively than they do in order to increase 
performance. The majority of studies on flexibility have focused on the market rather than the 
larger strategic environment, and little has been done to investigate market-related flexibility, 
including marketing (Alamro et al., 2018). They suggested three strategies for organisations 
seeking flexibility: resource reduction, investment in underutilised resources, and 
diversification employed for certain objectives (Alamro et al., 2018). 
Phan et al. (2019) argue that operational flexibility is considered as the competitive advantage 
and have become the dire need for enhancement of organisational performance. In recent era, 
the dynamic market demands compel manufacturing organisations to be operated under 
flexible and dynamic systems that could be highly responsive towards uncertainties and 
changes. Moreover, operational flexibility provokes a global trend of offering extensive 
product variety. In addition, as the organisations are trying to attain high quality at lowest 
possible production cost, so flexibility is considered at the ending stages towards productive 
excellence, but it is also a significant fact that operational flexibility has become vital for 
organisations competing in global markets to cope with the changing demands (Maria and 
Verdu-Jover, 2005).  
Other key facts for which operational flexibility is required by the organisations include 
customer satisfaction and responsiveness towards customers’ demand which have become the 
source of competitive advantage for organisations especially in manufacturing and service 
industries. At the same time, customer demands are also changing frequently so organisations 
having operational flexibility may win the race (Chaudhuri et al., 2018). Enhanced competition 
has also changed the success criteria for businesses in almost all industries because customers 
are always surrounded by lots of options in the market hence the ones who are able to attain 
their satisfaction may get the competitive advantage. But such satisfaction is not a onetime 
 
46 
 
phenomenon, but organisations must make lots of efforts to sustain their satisfaction level by 
responding quickly and innovatively towards their changing needs and demands (Mishra et al., 
2018).   
Such kind of quick response and innovative behaviour is only possible by the organisations 
having operational flexibility so that they can modify their operations according to the changing 
demands of the markets, their existing customers as well as to attract the potential customers. 
Operational flexibility not only provides reactive capability to the organisations but also it 
enables the organisation to satisfy mounting sophisticated customers’ needs and wants without 
incurring higher costs, quality defects, process disruptions and elongated delivery times 
(Abdallah and Nabass, 2018).  
Operational flexibility is not limited to the manufacturing of products but also incorporates the 
product mix and volume flexibility so that firms are able to respond according to the fluctuating 
demands of the market through elimination of excess inventory and time delays. Hence, 
organisations having higher flexibility may achieve higher customer satisfaction. By and large, 
improving flexibility is essential for building up the innovative capabilities and ensuring the 
organisations’ competitiveness (Alolayyan et al., 2013).  
Operational flexibility is found to be closely associated with the TQM practices 
implementation since the two concepts have some common orientations. Organisations adopt 
TQM practices for ensuring improved products’ quality, reduced costs and enhancement of 
customer satisfaction. Likewise, organisations try to attain flexibility performance that may 
eliminate unnecessary expenses and excessive production costs (Escrig‐Tenaet al., 2012). In 
this regard, Alolayyan et al. (2011) argue that TQM practices provide organisations’ higher 
adjustment ability in their strategies and structural administration, according to the changing 
market requirements. Moreover, it is found that TQM emphasises on moving beyond the firm-
centric approach towards expansion of quality management practices to up steam and down 
steam supply chain partners so that organisations could be able to cope with high velocity 
business environments.   
2.12. TQM/ TPM and the Resource Based View 
2.12.1. Theoretical foundation 
The resource-based view (RBV) is a managerial framework that determines the strategic 
resources a firm can exploit for sustainable competitive advantage. The development of the 
 
47 
 
resource-based view is associated with Barney’s (1991) seminal article ‘Firm resources and 
sustained competitive advantage’. The focus of the theory is on how firms develop sustainable 
competitive advantages based on the resources at their disposal. It proposes that firms differ 
because they possess heterogeneous resources (Armstrong and Shimizu, 2007). Firms adopt 
different strategies because of their different resource mix (Barney, 1991). In this theory, the 
managerial attention is given to the firm’s internal resources of firms in an attempt to identify 
the competencies, assets and capabilities that help to deliver competitive advantage.   
The RBV is an interdisciplinary approach to strategic planning that presents a change in 
thinking by focusing on internal, as opposed to external, resources. The internal resources and 
capabilities of a firm are analogous to the entry barriers cited in other theories. The resources 
become sustainable drivers of competitiveness if there are factors isolating them and preventing 
their diffusion to the rest of the industry. According to the theory, internal resources are a means 
of organising the processes of an organisation to achieve sustainable competitive advantages 
(Tozan and Ertürk, 2015). The theory posits that firms need to develop unique, firm-specific 
resources and competencies that enable them to compete and outperform their competitors. The 
resources must be valuable, rare, and imperfectly imitable and supported by the organisation 
(Hunt and Derozier, 2004). 
2.12.2. The evolution of RBV theory 
The resource-based view presents a beneficial approach in assessing how firms compete and 
their focus on the available assets and resources. This theory provides a significant way of 
looking at how companies develop sustainable competencies in their operations. These 
competencies are based on how firms organise their resources and utilise them to generate 
sustainable competitiveness. The focus on sustainable competitive advantages is highlighted in 
the theory as a key element of the strategies that should be adopted in response to the needs 
and changes of the market (Armstrong and Shimizu, 2007).  
At the heart of the RBV is the understanding that firms can have or create a resource advantage. 
Resources present an advantage that is sustainable and effective in generating competitiveness 
if they enable the firm to adopt unique measures. The development of the theory is based on 
the work of a number of scholars such as Day (1994), Prahalad and Hamel (1996). Achieving 
competitive advantage lies at the heart of strategic planning in the areas of marketing and 
management. These must be addressed through resources and strategies that can be effective 
 
48 
 
in developing the necessary products and deploying the right strategies for better positioning 
in the market (Hunt and Derozier, 2004; Richard et al., 2009). 
Organizations are being forced to reengineer their cultures, processes, and systems to support 
customer-focused and quality-driven competitive business strategies as a result of rising 
customer sophistication and the globalisation of company activities. In order to support their 
strategic decisions, firms implement quality management (QM) practises, such as TQM 
(Ahmed, and Ferdousi, 2020). TQM is a comprehensive strategy aimed at enhancing the 
processes, goods, and service quality continuously to meet or exceed customer expectations. It 
is "a method of conducting business that seeks to increase an organization's competitiveness 
via continuous improvement of the quality of its people, processes, and environments," 
(Ahmed, and Ferdousi, 2020). 
The pursuit of improvement is an ongoing effort in TQM. All organisational stakeholders, 
including managers, employees, suppliers, and customers, must fully participate in the 
continual quest for improvement; otherwise, the TQM effort would be ineffective. According 
to the literature, TQM adoption helps organisations achieve a number of advantages, such as 
efficient internal communication, better problem-solving, increased employee commitment 
and motivation, stronger relationships with suppliers, a greater understanding of customers' 
needs, increased customer satisfaction, fewer mistakes and fewer defects, productivity gains, 
cost savings, a reduction in defects, and enhancements in quality (Ferdousi et al., 2019).  After 
the discussion of the RBV, the following section focuses on explaining the reason of why this 
theory has been chosen.   
2.12.3. Why RBV? 
The resource-based view of the firm posits that an organisation competes and is able to develop 
effective competencies on the basis of the resources at its disposal. The competitiveness of a 
firm arises from the presence of organisational resources and assets including managerial skills 
and employees’ knowledge and experience. These resources are applied in response to the 
challenges in the market where the organisation can generate relevant products and processes 
to meet the needs of the customers. In this theory, the assets and resources held by an 
organisation help to develop sustainable competences as long as they are unique. Limitability 
of the valuable and rare resources of a firm means that competitiveness is not sustainable and 
that others have the ability to come up with similar products/services (Armstrong and Shimizu, 
2007; Richard et al., 2009). 
 
49 
 
The resource-based view focuses on providing better understanding of how firms utilise the 
available resources to manage their movements in the market. With this in mind, the internal 
resources of a firm play a major role in promoting the development of business. Firms need 
valuable and rare resources to take advantage of the opportunities in their market. In this 
review, the focus of the theory is on justifying how the presence of resources and capabilities 
of the firm enhance its competitiveness. For a firm to have sustainable competitive advantages, 
it needs capabilities that are critical to meeting the needs of the customers. These resources 
must be acquired or developed internally and organised in a way that makes them difficult to 
be inimitable (Sanni, 2018).  
TQM has the ability to produce a long-lasting competitive advantage. An open culture, 
employee empowerment, executive commitment, top management commitment/leadership, 
teams, culture, training/education, and process efficiency were identified as potential sources 
of sustainable competitive advantage when considering TQM aspects (Ferdousi et al., 2019). 
The relationship between TQM and competitive advantage has been researched from a 
resource-based perspective. Researchers have known for a while that competitive advantage 
depends on both the external environment and unique internal organisational resources and 
talents. Resources, both internal and external, are essential for competitive success. The ability 
to establish a competitive position belongs to the company with valuable resources that are 
distinct from and superior to those of rivals. Resources are said to need to be both valuable and 
uncommon (Ahmed and Ferdousi, 2020). 
On the other side, the competitiveness could be achieved through the development of 
managerial strategies. The adoption of strategies such as TQM and TPM can noticeably 
improve the internal competitiveness and the competencies of a firm. Adopting TPM and TQM 
supports organisational competitiveness by giving a firm the internal competencies and 
operational strategies to achieve excellence (Neely, 2003). Firms achieve operational 
excellence by adopting these strategies because they increase the capacity to achieve high 
quality outputs and reduce the incurred costs. The assumption is that adopting the principles of 
TQM involves having coordination among employees and managers to achieve a high level of 
quality in all aspects. These methodologies also focus on the customers as key decision-makers 
who influence how products are made. The TQM approach involves continuous improvement 
and communication across all ranks. These issues contribute to the competitiveness of firms by 
 
50 
 
considering employees as key influencers in the decision-making framework (Bronzo et al., 
2013). 
Employees are the key people involved in the operations of organisations; therefore, they are 
the key focus of TPM approach. The concepts of TPM are designed to make a firm highly 
efficient in its operations. The TPM strategies and principles involve knowledge sharing to 
raise the commitment of workforce (Halawi et al., 2006). These factors, in addition to the fact 
that TPM makes an organisation proactive in its approach to maintenance and responsiveness 
to customer needs, make TPM a key internal resource. The management approaches and 
strategies of TPM and TQM are central to the development of internal capabilities. By adopting 
these measures and their strategies in an effective way, managers can make their organisations 
more responsive to the needs of their customers (Chandran, 2015).  Issues such as 
organisational learning, knowledge sharing, coordination across departments and commitment 
of the employees can be considered as critical aspects of the RBV theory. This is because they 
influence the internal capabilities and processes for responding to the needs of customers. The 
operational excellence associated with these interventions and management approaches 
increases the efficiency of firm performance. The outcome, thus, is that firms can develop both 
tangible and intangible assets, enabling them to better compete in the market (Hwang et al., 
2020). 
Due to the relevance of the theory and how the resource-based view can impact securing quality 
internal resources from the management approaches, it is appropriate for the assessment of 
organisational performance. The formulation of the RBV as a way of assessing internal 
resources makes it beneficial as a conceptual framework for evaluating how the adoption of 
TPM and TQM can enhance organisational competitiveness. The approaches contribute to the 
adoption of management strategies that improve the internal coordination and other aspects of 
organisation’s performance such as the commitment of employees, the efficiency of equipment 
and the nature of approach to quality management. Quality management and commitment to 
the strategies of the two approaches is critical to achieving organisational competitiveness 
(Hwang et al., 2020). This theory will provide a beneficial framework for assessing how 
organisations develop their competitiveness and respond to market pressures. The adoption of 
TPM and TQM can be considered as a way of organising the internal resources to capture 
value. If the organisation is effectively using both management approaches of TPM and TQM, 
it can generate sustainable competitive advantages (Armstrong and Shimizu, 2007).  
 
51 
 
Based on the above-mentioned key facts of these approaches, and the understanding of 
manufacturing fundamentals and business needs, a number of manufacturing organisations in 
both developed and emerging economies utilise either TPM or TQM solely, while others adopt 
both TPM and TQM at the same time. In order to survive and thrive in the current, highly 
competitive global environment, international organisations must implement ISO standards 
and quality management procedures (Sahoo, 2019). TQM is a management philosophy and a 
set of management techniques that strive to achieve world-class status and provide consumers 
with superior value by identifying their expressed and hidden demands. TQM is a powerful 
example of success. However, it is countered that TPM efficiently handles TQM's modest 
impact on machinery and equipment performance (Sahoo, 2019). 
However, critical evaluation and decision making is required by the organisations to adopt and 
implement such approaches to lead to the achievement of core business objectives. An 
organisation can develop world-class manufacturing competencies by integrating TPM 
concepts with an ISO-certified quality standard framework. Alternate functional approaches, 
like as TPM and TQM, are available for enhancing the effectiveness and efficiency of an 
organization's operations. Compared to the solitary application of TPM and TQM principles, 
TPM and TQM have consistently shown to work synergistically, leading to enhanced 
performance. These two manufacturing techniques are complementary, which means that when 
one is used, the marginal returns of the others increase as well. The adoption of manufacturing 
strategies across various sectors may vary due to changes in industry structure, environment, 
and operational success criteria. Businesses must be supported by strong manufacturing 
practises. The researcher therefore assumes that TPM and TQM efficacy may differ from 
industry to industry based on their policies, rules and regulations (Sahoo, 2019).  
In this regard, RBV serves as the theoretical underpinning for a sizable and expanding corpus 
of supply chain research, emphasising the growing significance of resources and capabilities. 
First, it is stated that strategic resources used in supply chain organising processes can be found 
within businesses or beyond boundaries in inter-firm routines and resources. Intangible 
resources, such as information and knowledge, are more challenging to acquire than physical 
resources. Second, resources should be integrated to support capability development because 
they are thought to be unable to deliver value on their own. The procedures by which resources 
are employed to gain a competitive advantage are the second theme. Researchers investigated 
how a combination of resources and capabilities affected performance (Yu et al., 2018).  
 
52 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Supply Chain Management  
2.13.  Supply Chain Management (SCM) 
SCM refers to how organisations manage the flow of services and goods across the value chain. 
The process of SCM can also be defined as the design, planning, execution, and control of 
supply chain activities to create a net value and build a competitive infrastructure. It involves 
taking advantage of global logistic networks to synchronise supply and demand (Wieland et 
al., 2016). SCM practices draw heavily on aspects of systems engineering, industrial 
engineering, procurement, and logistics. It involves a wide range of activities from the storage 
and movement of raw materials, work in progress and finished goods. It involves the 
interrelated networks or channels that combine in the provision of goods and services to final 
consumers. SCM is a major element of risk management that focuses on ensuring that 
 
53 
 
commodities are available, and that the value chain is not disrupted by issues affecting any part 
of the value delivered to customers (Tozan and Ertürk, 2015; Zhang et al., 2018). 
2.14. The Themes of SCM 
Recent developments in SCM have presented a wide range of themes that are considered highly 
relevant. The main themes of SCM that contribute to enhancing the value of the practice to 
organisations include issues such as collaboration and communication. Effective internal 
collaboration and communication among the various players within an organisation are critical 
to influence the supply chain performance. Collaboration plays an important role in enhancing 
the performance of a supply chain and its operational performance in response to the needs of 
internal and external stakeholders (Zhang et al., 2018).  
Since it has become popular in recent years, SCM has been the focus of an increasing number 
of academic papers. However, Polish scholars have not sufficiently examined the intersection 
of these two topics, i.e., the role of small and medium enterprises in supply chains and chain 
management from the standpoint of such organisations. Although the writers of earlier research 
used the terms "supply chain" and "small and medium firms" in their titles, the real goals of 
those studies had little to do with those concepts. As a result, these studies make little mention 
of how small and medium-sized businesses participate in supply chains (Kot et al., 2018) 
Small and Medium Enterprises can successfully link the chain and acknowledges their role as 
links in the supply chain. He thinks that because of characteristics like flexibility, adaptation 
to consumer needs, adaptability to environmental changes, and reduced plant costs, SMEs can 
reach strong positions in chains, albeit typically it won't be the responsibility of the chain's 
leader (Kot et al., 2018). For businesses to thrive in this dynamic environment where operations 
like product development, manufacturing, acquiring materials, and distribution must be 
adjusted both reactively and/or proactively, they must increase their capabilities. Either internal 
or external information integration can be improved to accomplish this. The external method, 
however, may be more successful than the internal one when it comes to these two types of 
information integration. Although internal information integration methods like ERP can also 
increase flexibility, their long-term impact on a company's performance still depends on how 
well the various divisions can work together as opposed to just purchasing expensive gear. 
Contrarily, businesses must implement a traceability system to integrate with their channel 
 
54 
 
partners in order to establish information integration, which improves operational performance 
(Yu et al., 2018).  
Supply chain quality management incorporates key components including Quality 
Management (supplier support, customer focus and orientation, strategic planning and 
leadership, continuous improvement and learning, empowerment and teamwork, human 
resource focus, management structure, quality tools), SCM, and quality tools. (Logistics and 
transportation, marketing, best practises, supply base integration, alliances and partnerships, 
and strategic management). Organizations work to integrate quality into the supply chain and 
identifying the critical success factors (CSFs) is one efficient strategy to raise the standard of 
SCM. By using enterprise resource planning systems, CSFs are thought to enhance data 
exchange, optimise material flow, and enhance quality processes. However, they frequently 
differ in various situations (Chau et al., 2021). 
Cost saving is another main theme of SCM that influences its effectiveness as well as how its 
performance is measured. Managers in SCM are interested in understanding how to measure 
and improve the expenditure of costs. Tracking the SCM network is a critical aspect of 
organisational evaluation that influences its performance including the impact it has on overall 
success of the organisation. Managers are interested in assessing how they can save on costs 
and use the SCM network to improve their overall operations (Tiwari, Tiwari, and Samuel, 
2015). 
An advancement in SCM is the 2019 publication "Supply Chains for the Twenty-first Century" 
(SC21) aims to increase competition throughout the whole supply chain, from the prime 
contractor to the SME suppliers (Manville et al., 2021). The SC21 programme seeks to use the 
TQM principles to significantly advance organisational improvement and effectiveness 
throughout the aerospace supply chain (TQM). It aims to correct the flaws in earlier TQM 
implementations where tools and processes were valued more than softer factors like 
management commitment, leadership, and culture.  Additionally, it aims to reduce the 
obstacles SMEs have in implementing comprehensive performance improvement frameworks 
through stakeholder relationships throughout the supply chain (SC21, 2019). 
SCM practices compel managers to focus on different aspects of the supply chain. In this 
regard, an important element of SCM is the focus of managers on having a continuous tracking 
of inventory levels and supply chain. Having an up-to-date numbers of inventory levels and 
 
55 
 
system operational performance positively affects the supply chain and the efficiency of its 
management. A clear view of the entire organisation by the supply chain managers helps them 
to make confident decisions about the supply chain. These decisions should not be made in 
silos and, thus, their positive impact on the organisation is guaranteed (Zhang et al., 2018). A 
centralised end-to-end view of the supply chain is known as a control tower and acts as a point 
of coordination for all key stakeholders. This may involve multiple capabilities such as 
logistics, data analytics, forecasting and contract management. This element of control means 
that organisations can have full visibility over the different functions, which helps determining 
how to address the operational performance of the supply chain and the value it delivers to 
stakeholders. The control tower concept is highly valuable in assessing the delivery of services 
and products across organisations and, therefore, can be used to improve the level of 
collaboration (Kaynak, and Hartley, 2009).  
To enhance the collaboration at different levels of supply chain, the main focus is to establish 
the interrelationships among different stakeholders. Therefore, enhancing supplier 
relationships is another key theme of SCM. This requires moving from the perception that 
suppliers and their customers only need a transactional relationship with a simple focus on 
compliance with procurement rules. Supply chains with limited relationships among their 
stakeholders are ineffective and focus only on short-term gains. Instead, organisations 
nowadays are interested in developing long term strategic relationships with suppliers to 
deliver value beyond low prices and mere availability of the products. The supplier relationship 
can be enhanced through sharing demand signals with them, having a relationship management 
framework and pursuing outcome-based approaches. The value of strategic relationships with 
the supply chain players is a critical part of the themes being considered by modern supply 
chain managers (Huo et al., 2018).  
Another major element of the SCM is the issue of having strategic control over the resources. 
In the field of SCM, organisations seek new ways of strategically controlling their inventories 
to enhance spending. The stockholding levels are not determined arbitrarily in this case. 
Instead, they are determined based on the demand levels and forecasting by the different 
departments. This can eliminate the holding of excess inventory and improve the performance 
of the organisation. This also pools the inventory risks across the entire organisation and 
reduces the strategic burden associated with the value chain (Huo, Gu, and Wang, 2018). The 
consideration of SCM as a strategic resource with the capacity to enhance organisational 
 
56 
 
performance is a key theme of organisations contributing to how supply chains are currently 
perceived (Chen et al., 2018). 
Organisations emphasise and focus on these approaches to sustain their competitive position. 
In order to achieve a sustained competitive advantage, SCM practises and TQM practises have 
risen to the top of the priority list. TQM and SCM have been studied by researchers from around 
the world using various models as the key factors influencing corporate performance. A set of 
procedures known as TQM emphasises continuous improvement, ongoing results 
measurement, meeting customer requests, a team-based approach to problem solving, 
competitive benchmarking, lowering workload, long-term planning, and strong relationships 
with suppliers (Soares et al., 2017). Meanwhile, SCM has become one of the most significant 
and effective competitive strategies for manufacturing companies in recent years. The 
fundamental tenet of SCM may be summed up as taking input from suppliers, adding value, 
and giving it to the customer (Basheer et al., 2019).  
2.15. The Value of SCM 
Research on the relevance and value of SCM as a part of organisational success has focused on 
a wide range of benefits. Some of the key elements of the value associated with the presence 
of effective supply chains for organisations include the possibility of making data driven 
decisions. For example, effective adoption and management of the supply chain for an 
organisation creates a room for its optimisation. Optimising the supply chain provides a room 
for improving the bottom line and overall financial performance. Some of the most important 
aspects or value components of SCM include better collaboration among the players (Chen et 
al., 2018). Oracle argues that most companies lack automated information flow across their 
value chains. This leads to lost sales and doubts the overall value of the organisations. The 
critical concern for firms is to have integrated software solutions for SCM that allow seamless 
information sharing and an end-to-end view. With this in place, the effective SCM approach 
provides better access to information and the opportunity for making more informed decisions 
(Zhang et al, 2018).  
While focusing on SCM practices, organisations face some critical challenges as well. Quality 
issues present a significant challenge for companies when they arise across the different parts 
of the supply chain. The cost of replacing or repairing an item in the supply chain poses a 
significant control issue for firms with no effective SCM networks. Firms with greater control 
over their suppliers and other players in their supply chains benefit from better quality control. 
 
57 
 
A firm can implement the standard minimum quality criteria for its suppliers, motivating them 
to partner with secondary suppliers who meet their requirements. This heavily contributes to 
accomplishing the agreed quality standards. Additionally, companies can implement process 
guidelines for their suppliers and, hence, enabling them to comply with quality requirements 
(Flynn and Flynn, 2005). In order to enhance the delivery of value for the organisations, the 
SCM can be used to ensure compliance with the quality guidelines, while at the same time, 
assessing compliance steps through frequent audits. The SCM strategies provide the 
opportunity for a managerial operating system to monitor the key performance indicators of 
the supply chain and its players. Some of the KPIs used for assessing the SCM players include 
on tie delivery, product quality, scrap rates and the result of supplier quality assessment. 
Through these assessments, the best vendors and suppliers can be considered for ensuring 
quality control (Koech and Ronoh, 2015). 
The core purpose of SCM is to develop an integrated network among different members of the 
supply chain. Hence, SCM is a set of techniques used to effectively integrate distributors, 
manufacturers, storage facilities, and stores so that the services or products can be produced 
and distributed at the proper time, quantity, and location to reduce costs and meet service level 
requirements. Contrarily, the supply chain is dynamic and involves a continuous flow of data, 
merchandise, and money between various stages for customers. SCM is greatly impacted by 
SMEs because they serve as suppliers, distributors, producers, and clients (Setyaningsih, and 
Kelle, 2021).  
SCM is particularly helpful for SMEs to increase their competitive advantage through in-the-
moment collaboration with partners, such as customer service enhancement, forecast 
improvement, logistic cost reduction, enhanced planning and scheduling. However, SMEs 
frequently face disadvantages because they lack a skilled staff for the supply chain or a 
sophisticated IT infrastructure to support the SCM system because of their small size and scale 
(Wu et al., 2006).  Limited number of studies has examined various SCM systems using cross-
country comparison views from various continents. Second, few research have examined the 
use of SCM in nations where micro-businesses make up the majority of SME (Setyaningsih, 
and Kelle, 2021). Therefore, there is a need to further elaborate the role of SCM in SMEs, so 
that it can help enhancing the operational efficiencies of SMEs as well. 
SMEs, MNCs or any other type of organisations, SCM contributes to the efficiency of 
operations by ensuring that real time data is available on all critical issues such as availability 
 
58 
 
of materials or demand. It also ensures that the managers are aware of the possibility for 
manufacturing delays that may arise from lack of material. With real time SCM data, 
companies can initiate backup plans and prevent adverse outcomes of stock outages, late 
shipment, and customer dissatisfaction. SCM implementation and optimisation can provide 
opportunities for smart automation where operational performance can be enhanced through 
strategies for leveraging data and effective communication (Abdallah et al., 2014). With SCM, 
efforts to keep up with demand changes are quickly and effectively communicated and, hence, 
reduce the bullwhip effect phenomenon. This phenomenon involves the views that an increase 
in customer orders can result in inflated orders at a factory due to communication delays. These 
delays are addressed through real time data and communication that can better predict demand 
and the necessary orders to meet them (Koech and Ronoh, 2015). 
SCM helps enhancing operational efficiencies in different ways. It plays a major role in 
optimising logistics because the effective implementation of SCM can contribute to the 
identification of efficient logistic methods and strategies. Having an effective SCM approach 
provides a framework for determining the most efficient logistic methods for different types of 
cargo to minimise costs and time of delivering commodities. These considerations present 
savings that can be passed on to customers through lower prices, which boost the bottom-line 
of the firms (Ronoh, 2016). The SCM network promotes the overall efficiency of determining 
the needed measures for handling different types of inventories (Kin et al., 2017). This can 
reduce the overheads for a business and develop an environment where high service and quality 
levels can be achieved at relatively lower costs (Abdallah et al., 2014).  
Another significant value for the SCM framework is providing opportunities to mitigate high 
risk level. Risk mitigation for firms is an essential factor to respond to potential issues that may 
affect the delivery of products/services. With an effective SCM framework, a firm can put in 
place the necessary back-up systems or strategies to respond to unexpected circumstances 
(Flynn and Flynn, 2005). The SCM provides avenues for proactive action where disruptions 
and quality control issues are addressed before they arise to avoid adverse outcomes. For 
example, according to Kin (2020), 87% of firms believe that they can reduce their inventories 
by 22% if they better understand the risks in their supply chains.   
Focusing on particularly SMEs, SCM has a somewhat different connotation for SMEs than it 
does for LEs. To increase the value of the finished product and, consequently, to ensure a long-
term partnership, SCM in this instance could refer to a set of business activities that include 
 
59 
 
purchasing from the open market, manufacturing or processing of subcomponents within the 
plant, and delivery to using hired transportation. By combining the processes of diverse 
partners on three levels—strategic, tactical, and operational—SCM is defined in SMEs as a 
method that helps the organisation run more quickly and efficiently (Asad et al., 2020). SCM 
may improve the performance of SMEs and raise their profitability by enhancing their capacity 
to receive supplies of the appropriate quality and at the right time. Globalization forces every 
firm to serve items at cheaper prices. Even still, the majority of SMEs do not fully utilise SCM, 
despite the fact that they are aware of its advantages. Strategic planning and supply chain 
integration are two major areas where SMEs' level of SCM adoption can be broken down (Asad 
et al., 2020).
