In general, supply chain integration entails information exchange, operational, tactical, and 
strategic planning, coordination, and control of inputs, components, and final items. The 
advantages of SCM integration include better customer service and lower expenses for 
managing inventory. The complexity of a SME's operations in terms of size and scope increases 
as its firm expands. SMEs might incur higher costs and run a greater risk (Setyaningsih, and 
Kelle, 2021; Yvon et al., 2019). Therefore, it requires basic financial plans and budgets so that 
SMEs can start planning for the future rather than reacting to changes in the industry. Strategic 
planning is essential to the survival and expansion of SMEs. It can be dealt with by working 
well with partners and sharing information more effectively. The following are the four 
techniques for strategic planning that SMEs might employ.(1) network optimization, which 
involves creating the least expensive network while concentrating on customer demand; (2) 
network simulation, which involves comparing various models to forecast supply chain 
behaviour; (3) policy optimization, which entails creating the best operating rules; and (4) 
robustness designing, which entails foreseeing unforeseen events and possibilities 
(Setyaningsih, and Kelle, 2021; Yvon et al., 2019).  
2.16. Integrated Supply Chain 
Integration of supply chains implies the collaboration of different stakeholders of the 
organisations involved at different points of the supply chain, with the core purpose of 
enhancing their performance through collaborative work towards creation and distribution of 
the organisational products while implementing management techniques. In large corporation, 
stakeholders are considered as divisions of business who are all bound by shared objectives 
and trust (Kwasi et al., 2019). 
 
60 
 
Although all types of supply chains are somewhat integrated, but this integration is further 
strengthened for coordination of the relevant resources offered by all participants fulfilling the 
needs of the supply chain for enhancement of the overall performance of that chain. The 
integration process incorporates processes, techniques, and skills to be implemented in 
structured manner for collaborating the capabilities and characteristics for better availing the 
prevailing business opportunities (Lotfi et al., 2013). The shared objectives of all involved 
participants of the supply chain are elimination of associated risks and enhancements of their 
interests (Yang et al., 2009). Therefore, all stakeholders focus on effective management of 
supply chains. 
Supply chains if not managed appropriately incorporate less consideration towards sharing 
risks and benefits and are focused on short term achievements while having little or no 
consideration towards long term achievements. Moreover, cost and delivery of products are 
prioritised over the value of the products and there are limited interactions among firms and 
their suppliers those results in restricted communications (Yang et al., 2009). 
In contrast to the traditional supply chain, the integrated supply chain focuses on the shared 
benefits of the concerned participants of supply chains and focus on enjoying such benefits at 
a longer period of time. Such long-term relationships are managed and sustained through 
development of open communication among all the key participants which helps them 
responding efficiently to the changes in surrounding environment and facilitate them in 
attaining the competitive advantage for them (Barbara et al., 2010). 
The integration of supply chains is an ongoing process where stakeholders jointly put their 
efforts for improvement of relationships, and they are always well informed about the 
prevailing activities of the supply chain at any level. In such integrated supply chain, suppliers, 
retailers, manufacturers work in a progressive manner through effective information sharing 
and feedback to both ends of the supply chain (Amoako-Gyampah, 2019).   
2.16.1. Why Integration is Necessary? 
Nontrivial contradictions have also been found when reviewing the supplier integration 
literature findings. Supplier integration has been linked in certain academic studies to new 
product development processes, product performance, supplier development, and visibility-
related metrics. Others, however, have discovered either no significant association or even an 
adverse relationship between supplier integration and operational performance.  The question 
of whether SCI should be simulated with operational performance in general without limitation 
 
61 
 
is another topic of debate in the literature (Lu et al., 2018). Therefore, the following global 
trends and forces are driving supply chains towards greater integration (Marian and Soare, 
2020): 
Improved cost competitiveness. Organisations are looking to significantly improve the 
efficiency of their internal operations and further reduce costs through enhancement of 
efficiency and synergies within the supply chain.  
Product life cycle is shortened. Mounting competitions due to frequently changing market 
demands have shortened the life cycle of products.  Products being competitive for many years 
may have been at the forefront for less than a year and continue to tend to shorten product 
lifecycles.  
Faster product development cycle; For achieving the competitive edge, organisations are 
required to shorten their product development cycles because organisations who successfully 
introduce new products at early stages could better capture the market share and are able to 
gain the cost efficiency as well.  
Globalization leading to customised products; Globalisation has made the accessibility of 
products so convenient that customers have a huge variety of products for meeting their needs 
so mass customization has become a new marketing mantra.  
Enhanced quality; As customers become more affluent and the competition to meet their 
needs intensifies, there is a need to improve overall quality. 
Hence, these increasing demands compel the organisations to focus on the having better 
controls over cost, design, distribution, and support of their products.  
However, integration of supply chains is a very challenging task especially in terms of 
associated complexity, risks, and costs for managing a highly integrated chain which should be 
focused by the organisation in a similar manner as focusing on the operations of their 
organisations. Therefore, very limited numbers of organisations go for integrating their supply 
chains especially due to the associated costs that create a challenge for the organisation. Some 
associated costs include time spent for the training and support of management and other 
stakeholders, efforts to have loyal customers, making investments in compatible information 
 
62 
 
systems for the entire supply chain, opportunity costs that may come from sacrificing other 
business opportunities and the risks involved in production downtimes (Ragatza et al., 2002).  
In contrast to the benefits associated with the integration of supply chains, there are certain 
challenges as well. The enhanced levels of integration in the supply chain may also result in 
enhanced confusion due to the degree of interdependence and interoperability, so organisations 
could not ignore the risk of production downtime. Such integrated supply chains also cause 
interdependence especially on the suppliers who, most of the time, are having minimal levels 
of inventory due to just-in-time practices so the organisations become dependent on their 
offerings. Moreover, the delivery also becomes dependent on suppliers as if there are delays in 
delivery, so organisation have to face delays in their production processes as well (Talib et al., 
2011).  
Some other challenges associated with the supply chains are the external factors. As, supply 
chains may also have to face potential threats like weather conditions, technical problems like 
computer hacks, power outages, environmental threats, terrorism, equipment failures, and 
communication failures etc. which could not be predicted, and they may have to incur heavy 
costs for these. So, such kinds of risks are required to be assessed at individual levels of the 
supply chains but could be better managed through tighter integration, enhanced procurement, 
controlling for inventory and development of strong relationships among the stakeholders 
(Gassmann, 2010).  
The most significant and attractive benefit of integrating the supply chain is having a better 
control on inventory levels, because such integration facilitates the speedy flow of materials 
and stocks among different levels of supply chains. Therefore, it helps organisations in 
controlling carrying costs of inventory by reducing the levels of inventory. Moreover, 
transaction costs are also reduced through having appropriate flow of information, eliminating 
the number of transactions, reducing the costs of transactions etc. that creates substantial 
benefits for all participants in the form of cost savings (Marian and Soare, 2020).  
Product costs could also be reduced by eliminating the supplier dismissal while enhancing the 
levels of production by other suppliers which also helps eliminating the costs incurred on 
management of supply chain. Although in this way the suppliers could be overburdened by the 
enhanced management and investment responsibilities, but it also results in improved decision 
making by appropriate maintenance of the flow of information among remaining parties 
(Amoako-Gyampah, 2019).  
 
63 
 
Therefore, in order to focus on the operational efficiencies of the organisations, they must also 
ensure that sustainability practises are integrated across their supply chain (SC). As "a strategic 
and transparent integration designed to achieve social, environmental, and economic goals 
through the systemic coordination of important inter-organizational business processes for 
improving the long-term economic performance of the individual company through supply 
chain integration. Supply Chain (SC) partners can improve their sustainable procurement, 
Sustainable design, and distribution systems through collaborative processes, practises, 
structures, methodologies, and technology Both internal and external integration assist 
businesses in overcoming inefficiencies brought on by resource scarcity and capacity 
limitations in order to create a sustainable supply chain integration (Shashi et al., 2018). 
Supply chain integration also provides some other significant benefits like less barriers, 
elimination of the waste of resources, less friction, elimination of procedures that are unable to 
add value, improved functional and procedural synergy, enhanced responsiveness for changing 
in market trends, reduced costs of operations, shorter product development cycles, reduced 
investment in manufacturing capacity, reduced costs of product development, enhanced 
competitiveness and financial benefits (Barbara, 2010; Lotfi et al., 2013). 
Consequently, today, organisations of all sizes inside the planned operations industry approach 
a full set-up of inventive and cost-proficient programming arrangements that empower full 
visibility and constant sharing of data from one end to another in their supply chain networks 
(Yang et al, 2009). The shift to paperless work processes doesn't need to involve disturbance 
of the existing frameworks or work rehearses. Arrangements as of now exist that offer the 
ability to tap in and quickly associate with a broad organisation, permitting you to hold your 
past speculations. We really want to recall, notwithstanding, that chain integration requires a 
change in mentality that starts with an agreement that traditional cycles and arrangements are 
unprepared to satisfy the needs of the present hyper connected clients (Lotfi et al., 2013). 
Supply chain integration has become the need of the era for almost all kinds of businesses. It 
incorporates integration among all stakeholders including supplier integration, customer 
integration and internal integration. 
Customer integration implies the acquisition of marketing, technological, production and 
inventory related information from the customers. This information is used by the 
manufacturers for making products that are able to fulfil customers’ expectations and can gain 
customer satisfaction. Customer integration is also termed as downstream integration as it 
 
64 
 
incorporates flourishing the flow of products and services towards customers by getting better 
information from customers about the changing trends in the market (Gassmann, 2010).  
Customer integration refers to developing healthy relationships between organisations and its 
customers for developing a clearer and better understanding of customers’ wants, preferences 
and changing demands. It also implies the incorporation of customers’ opinions and feedback 
in the decision-making process about goods production, so it emphasises adopting the ways 
and methods for enhancing coordination among customers and manufacturers (Gassmann, 
2010).  
SMEs enterprises are able to develop into new markets and streamline corporate processes 
thanks to integration with upstream and downstream relationships. Uncertain or divergent 
sustainability objectives, for instance, can undermine the SCs' members' cooperative 
environmental efforts (Shashi et al., 2018). Synergy with suppliers promotes the development 
and use of cutting-edge environmental technologies. As a result, the majority of the top 
manufacturing companies choose to work with partners who can provide sustainable goods and 
services. Customers, suppliers, and the media all have an impact on a company's sustainable 
practises and, in turn, operational effectiveness. The government, rivals, and the logistics 
network are further motivating variables for better operational performance (Shashi et al., 
2018).  
Supplier integration incorporates the flow of technical, operational, and financial information 
between suppliers and the manufacturers. Moreover, manufacturers also share information 
related to demand forecasts, production plans and levels of inventory with the suppliers of the 
organisation. Sharing such kinds of information becomes beneficial for the organisations 
because it facilitates the estimation of product and production requirements as well as enables 
the organisation to better utilise the production plants and suppliers’ capabilities and the cost 
structure of the organisation (Talib et al., 2011).  
Supplier integration implies the collaboration of suppliers and manufacturers for making 
decisions related to collaborative planning, inventory management, forecasting, flows of 
physical resources and replenishment. It is also termed as upstream integration because it 
incorporates the relationship between upstream suppliers and the organisation for getting 
information from suppliers and making them the part of decision-making process about 
products. Such relationships and the communication flow between the participants contribute 
 
65 
 
effectively to the production process because suppliers have better knowledge about the 
components being supplied for the production process (Marian and Soare, 2020).  
Supplier and customer integration are the parts of external integration. Contrary to these two, 
internal integration implies the integration of internal departments of the organisation from 
material intake to the distribution of final product. It involves the integration across different 
functions and departments under the control of manufacturer for meeting the customer 
requirements according to their expectations (Yang et al, 2009).  
Internal integration suggests that management should give more consideration to interplay 
among different functional departments including the production department, procurement, 
inventory, logistics, marketing, distribution, and sales. It demonstrates the degree, to which an 
organisation can build all of its practices and functions in more organised and collaborative 
manner for meeting customers’ expectations. Therefore, the departments and the functions 
within a manufacturer facility as a single integrated and coordinated system that works together 
for improvement of performance and meeting customers’ requirements (Amoako-Gyampah, 
2019).  
In internal integration, some elements contribute significantly in enhancing performance 
including shared information among different departments and functions, functional 
coordination, joint planning, coordination teams, and collaborative decision making for 
ensuring the elimination of delays and defects as well as enhancing customers’ satisfaction 
(Marian and Soare, 2020). Lotfi et al. (2013) argue that information sharing has become a vital 
tool and proves to be effective in supply chains for attaining effective and efficient 
organisational operational performance. One of the most significant dimensions of operational 
performance of the organisation is the quality of product that seeks more attention to compete 
in the market. Therefore, collaborative activities between all members of the supply chain 
contribute significantly to enhancing the quality of product and facilitate the organisations to 
improve its operational performance. Lotfi et al. (2013) argue that organisations that adopt 
collaborative and cooperative environment among all stakeholders are the ones who can 
successfully attain the competitive advantage than those who do not prioritise this. 
Additionally, Amoako-Gyampah (2019) argue that organisations having non-integrated supply 
chain has disconnected departments and functions who are all busy in accomplishing their own 
tasks without knowing about the functioning of other departments. In this way, each function 
or department functions in a way that it generates benefits for any single aspect of the business 
 
66 
 
i.e. cost, or timing or the accuracy, but not in all these three aspects. Such functioning may 
result in saving and efficiency in one area, but it may cost high to some other part of the 
business. It also restricts the flow of information among different units that results in difficulties 
in identification of issues and adjustments of operations (Kwasi et al., 2019).  
In contrast, Lotfi et al. (2013) indicate that organisations having integrated supply chains have 
enhanced flexibility in their operations for adjusting according to the customers’ requirements, 
competitors’ actions or even during any uncertain event in the market or industry. Amoako-
Gyampah (2019) argue that integrated supply chains help organisations eliminating waste and 
reducing costs, hence gaining the competitive advantage and benefits for their businesses. 
2.17.  Supply Chain Integration and Manufacturing Practices 
The core purpose of implementing manufacturing practices like TQM and TPM is to enhance 
the quality of products and attaining efficient operational performance according to the 
demands and expectations of customers. On the other hand, SCM is practiced by the 
organisations for controlling manufacturing processes for enhancing product quality and 
eliminating the risks of recalls as well as building a strong customer brand. Hence, both 
approaches have a similar purpose of quality enhancement and customer satisfaction. The only 
difference between both elements is the way to approach the quality i.e., within the organisation 
in case of manufacturing practices (TQM) and across the supply chain in SCM (Marian et al., 
2020). 
SCM and the TQM have been considered as two most significant approaches for firms like 
services, manufacturing, and small and medium enterprises (SME). These approaches have 
become the vital ingredient for organisational success and attaining competitive advantage in 
the industry. Initially these two approaches were not considered to be adopted at a same time 
in an organisation due to similarity of purpose but with the passage of time, the integration of 
these two approaches in an organisation proved to strengthen the competitive advantage of the 
organisation as well as enhanced customer satisfaction (Talib et al., 2011). Hence, in recent 
era, applications of TQM principles in supply chains have become common practices especially 
in the field of SCM (Marian et al., 2020). Anyway, Marian et al. (2020) debated that although 
all types of supply chain integration like supplier integration, internal integration, and customer 
integration result in adding benefits for the organisations but when it comes to the application 
of quality management practices, customer integration is proved to add less benefits than 
 
67 
 
supplier integration and internal integration. In quality management practices, customer 
integration impairs the effectiveness of innovative activities either at early innovative phases 
of the production processes or at few sub-phases (Gassmann et al., 2010).   
In recent dynamic environment, innovations have become vital for the survival of organisations 
in the market, while such innovative behaviour entails high research and development costs. 
Therefore, organisations have to rely on integrated external innovation sources like customer 
integration that can guide the organisation that what they need and want and ensure that the 
new developed product would satisfy their needs. Early innovation phase is the initial phase of 
the whole innovative process leading to two other phases of technical product development and 
then in last the commercialisation of the product. Hence, customer integration may have 
significant impact on the early innovation phase having significant impact on research and 
development activities. But customer integration has nothing to do with further stages of 
product development and commercialisation (Amoako-Gyampah, 2019). Contrary to the above 
discussion, Bassmann et al. (2010) argue that customer integration even at the initial stages of 
innovation or product development results in negative side effects especially when the focus is 
on quality enhancement with efficient operational performance. Therefore, the inclusion of 
customers is not advisable because it hampers the achievement of other targets of the 
organisations.  
Nowadays, the majority of firms view adopting sustainable practises as both a means of 
competitive advantage and a requirement for long-term survival. As a result, it is crucial that 
sustainability to be considered when evaluating a company's supply chain performance. The 
effect of sustainability on supply chain performance has, however, only been covered in a very 
small number of research (Katiyar, et al., 2018). Kwasi et al. (2019) argue that although 
customer integration is also one of the most significant and effective aspect of supply chain 
integration, but when it comes to the implementation of manufacturing practices like TQM, 
customer integration does not prove to have significant positive outcomes. Because TQM not 
only emphasises on the enhancement of quality of products, but it also has a major focus on 
production efficiency through controlling the production costs while integration of customers 
mostly results in increased production costs. The dilemma is the short life of innovation cycles 
that entails the risk that early customer integration may result in such innovations that may not 
be able to meet customers’ wants later on, hence increasing the cost. On the other hand, 
organisation may face delays in market introduction of the product according to customers’ 
 
68 
 
expectations that may be fulfilled by some other substitutes which imposes significant pressure 
on the organisation and its production efficiency (Amoako-Gyampah, 2019). 
While on the other hand, supplier integration and internal integration are found to have 
significant positive contribution in new product development when combined with quality 
management practices (Ragatz et al., 2002). Supplier integration allows the flow of information 
from suppliers to the manufacturers hence using the knowledge of suppliers and incorporation 
of their expertise for complementing internal capabilities helps reducing the concept to 
customer cycle time, quality issues, costs and enhance the overall product design (Amoako-
Gyampah, 2019). 
TQM practices are not only focused on enhancing the quality of products but also have 
significant emphasis on reducing the costs to improve profitability. Hence, supplier integration 
contributes significantly towards quality management practices because being in mutual 
beneficial relationship suppliers also strive for saving costs at different stages of product 
development (Lee and Lee, 2014). Supplier integration not only helps reducing the delivery 
costs but also helps controlling the quality issues, availability problems and delays leading to 
a better service for customers. Moreover, development of a good communication flow between 
suppliers and manufacturers results in developing a thorough understanding of the end products 
which enhances suppliers’ ability to serve the business accordingly (Amoako-Gyampah, 2019).  
Supplier integration helps improving the flow of operations by eliminating the delays from 
suppliers’ sides which ultimately results in elimination of delays in final product delivery. It 
also helps controlling the fluctuations in market prices of the products. As price fluctuations 
negatively affect the customer expectations, but the core purpose of TQM practices is to meet 
the customers’ expectations, so supplier integration also helps eliminating the negative impact 
of such fluctuations (Marian and Soare, 2020). Through developing healthy relationships with 
the suppliers through supplier integration, manufacturers may take advantage of fixed costs of 
materials for lengthier contract terms. Similarly, having a clear cost structure of the supplies 
helps organisations structuring their prices without any ambiguity that may hurt the customers’ 
expectations in future (Liu and Liu, 2014).  
A long-term relationship between both parties through supplier integration allows for free flow 
of ideas and feedback over time. Such kind of streamlined effective chain results in positive 
impact on both; customer services and costs at both ends. Implementing TQM practices, the 
 
69 
 
aspects of new product development, initiating new ordering processes and control of inventory 
becomes a joint venture that results in significant operational and financial benefits at both ends 
of the association. Working closely with the suppliers, organisations can tailor their supply 
chain according to their individual needs which also helps them consolidating the processes, 
reduction of costs and ultimately improvements in the end product for the customers (Yunis, 
2013).  
Nilda et al. (2017) indicate that internal integration enhances the effectiveness of TQM 
practices implemented in the organisation. The main focus and emphasis of TQM practices is 
to improve the quality of products while reducing waste, costs and delays. Therefore, when 
management systems or different functions within the organisation are integrated, then each 
function lines up towards a single goal enhancing the overall operational performance of the 
organisation. Internal integration incorporates coordinated efforts which are much higher than 
the sum of individual parts which not only improves efficiency but also effectiveness of the 
organisational operations. A study by Amoako-Gyampah (2019) indicated that internal 
integration has significant positive impact on the implementation of TQM practices in the form 
of enhanced quality, safety and resulting operational performance. It also helps eliminating 
risks associated with the production efficiency as well as helps eliminating defects at different 
stages of product development. Another significant positive impact of internal integration is 
the uniform understanding of organisational objectives, policies, resources, and processes that 
enhances efficient utilisation of organisational resources.  
Moreover, the implementation of quality management practices requires training and 
assessment of employees for effective implementation, so internal integration enhances the 
effectiveness of training through appropriate implementation of practices by the flow of 
information among employees from different functions of the organisation (Rachmat, 2019). 
Integration of multiple functions establishes cohesive processes, resources, objectives that 
align with the system and improve accountability that ultimately helps improving productive 
efficiency. However, Rachmat (2019) argue that internal integration improves the consistency 
of management systems in the organisation which eliminates the complexity of management 
system and helps employees developing a better understanding of organisational objectives. 
Employees having good understanding of organisational objectives work together to achieve 
these objectives; like optimising resources and processes of the organisation that result in 
 
70 
 
reduced costs. It also helps reducing the time taken for performing certain activities by 
employees as well as eliminating disruptions and delays that also contribute to cost reduction.  
Marian et al. (2020) argued that quality management systems like TQM and TPM are all 
focused on enhancing the product quality while meeting customer expectations, but when these 
practices are combined with the information and knowledge gained through supplier 
integration and internal integration the benefits are multiplied in the form of reduced costs, 
enhanced quality, and optimization of the processes and resources.  
In a similar context, Chaudhuri et al. (2018) argued that companies having good supplier 
relationships along with integrated internal systems have enhanced flexibility to respond 
according to the customers’ changing requirements as well as fluctuations in the market and 
industry. Organisations’ ability to be adaptive and flexible to varying market situations is 
significant in attaining the competitive advantage in industry. Integration at supplier and 
internal levels facilitate organisations to respond so quickly and effectively that could never be 
possible without such integration. Beyond that, Supplier integration is debated to help 
organisations in attaining the inventory efficiency like only carrying the needed inventory 
while ensuring the further availability as soon as it is required by the production facility. It 
helps eliminating carrying costs as well as saving the space at manufacturing facility. 
Organisations could not enjoy such benefits in the absence of supplier integration because in 
such case they may have to face certain delays as well (Kwasi et al., 2019).  
These approaches not only add to the efficiencies of operational performance of the 
organisations in terms of enhanced production, but also focus on eliminating waste to reduce 
costs and improve productive efficiency; hence supplier and internal integration help 
organisations eliminating waste at different stages of the production process. Better route 
management through such integrations not only helps saving the warehouse space but also 
saves the emissions leading to meet low waste environmental goals. Moreover, reducing the 
level of inventory also helps eliminating logistics costs and save money hence, being flexible 
while eliminating waste helps organisations in saving huge amounts that could be better utilised 
in other operations (Yang et al., 2009).  
Focusing on the impacts of internal integration, Amoako-Gyampah (2019) argue that internal 
integration improves the effectiveness of TQM implementation because it not only helps the 
free flow of information among different departments but also helps employees in sharing their 
 
71 
 
expertise when required by other especially at equipment handling stage. So, it helps 
developing a team-based environment that is more dedicated and knowledgeable which 
enhances the operational performance by improving the workflow across organisation. 
Moreover, instead of working independently the cost sharing attitude helps cutting the costs of 
operations without sacrificing the quality or deadlines (Kwasi et al., 2019), hence, internal and 
supplier integration better help organisations attaining the competitive advantage than the 
customer integration.   
2.18.  Business Impact of Supplier Integration 
Flynn et al. (2010) define supply integration as a process where firms partner with their 
suppliers to align their operations and share pertinent information for growth. Integration of 
suppliers into a firm aim at restructuring inter-organisational strategies to develop asynchrony 
in the production processes through efficient sharing of information between suppliers and their 
firms. While Zhang et al. (2018) observe that supplier integration is a critical tool for enhancing 
a firm’s operational performance. An integrated environment is also important to the supply 
chain. The supplier provides firms with important information regarding the requirements in 
the market based on the changing trends. Businesses on the other hand directly connect with 
the consumers where they get important information that informs the suppliers on the current 
needs of the consumers.   
The concept of supplier integration is supported by the Information processing theory supports 
the idea that an integrated business environment increases the competitive edge of businesses. 
The theory suggests that the capability to process the quality information that is provided for 
firms is an important determinant of success. The business environment today is a complex 
system that requires strategic decisions and accurate moves from firms to survive the harsh 
competitive pressures. Munir et al. (2020) argue that supplier and customer integration is a 
pivotal point in the provision of quality information needed to increase a firm’s operational 
performance. Suppliers rely on the information provided to plan the production schedules and 
manage their inventory. Suppliers access to quality information from the market and businesses 
enhance their ability to align their internal operations and capability to provide relevant 
products that will suit the market. Again, supplier ability to interpret the information provided 
by the market players enhances their ability to direct businesses to the right decision. Suppliers 
are also able to determine the proper supplies to deliver to businesses. Contrary to suppliers, 
businesses benefit from the information gained from the integrated environment to align the 
 
72 
 
internal capabilities and promote information sharing within the firm to enhance planning and 
operational alignment. Supplier integration is an important part for an organisation to provide 
information intelligence for efficient operational performance.   
An integrated environment aids businesses in attaining their goals and providing quality 
products to customers through custom-designed products. Supplier integration is also essential 
to inform organisations on the anticipated changes for them to use total maintenance practices 
to align the technologies with the current market demands. Lack of supplier integration has two 
implications on business. First, it leads to reduced operational performance and increased 
production costs. Second, it leads to a longer channel of distribution between the supplier and 
business, which reduces the costs of production. On the contrary, an integrated environment 
reduces the length of the distribution channel and, consequently, reduces the cost of production 
for the business. Suppliers benefit from the integrated environment by establishing stable 
relationships with their buyers to increase their sales. An integrated business environment, thus, 
creates a stable business environment for suppliers, which improves their business performance 
(Zhang, Lettice, Chan and Nguyen, 2018).   
Strategic partners must make a commitment to achieving their shared supply chain goals in 
order for a business transaction involving supply chain partners to last. Business partnerships 
become weak and insecure in the lack of commitment. Supplier integration emphasises the 
importance of having a relationship with a significant supplier that includes information 
sharing, teamwork during planning, and cooperative production development to overcome 
inter-organizational boundaries (Afshan and Motwani, 2021). It enables businesses to 
collaborate more successfully with a select group of significant suppliers who are ready to share 
accountability for the goods' success. In order to collaboratively improve service capabilities 
at lower supply chain costs, supplier integration synchronises the key competences and 
capabilities of buyers and suppliers (Afshan and Motwani, 2021). 
Researchers have highlighted the key features of supplier integration. In this regard, Flynn et 
al. (2010) define supplier integration as a partnership between an organisation and its suppliers 
that focuses on aligning operations and sharing information. This aims to structuring inter-
organisational strategies and to develop synchronised processes in which information is shared 
and knowledge is gained for achieving efficient operations. Zhang et al. (2018) argue that 
supplier integration is a critical tool to optimise a firm’s competitive advantage through 
improving inter-enterprise performance. Firms can integrate with suppliers through enterprise 
 
73 
 
integration or interoperability. Enterprise integration occurs when enterprises get into long-
term collaboration in which they homogenise their processes and harmonise their operations. 
In interoperability integration, on the other hand, each firm remains independent, but develops 
its own capability to collaborate and synchronise strategies, skills, and processes with each 
other (Kin, 2020). 
The importance of supply chain integration for firm performance is supported by the 
‘information processing theory’. Information processing theory bases its arguments on the 
information processing capability of firms. The theory suggests that firms need quality 
information and enhanced information processing capability to provide a ground for reacting 
to the daily changes of the complex business environment. The theory adds that the greater the 
risk that a firm is predicting, the greater the amount of quality information processing needed 
to manage risk impacts and aspire performance outcomes. Supplier and customer integration 
allows for the fast gathering of quality information to guide task execution (Munir et al., 2020). 
The information acquired from supplier integration enhances firm’s capability to detect 
traditional risks, prevent the extent of harm caused by the risk and to react to the risk posed by 
the complex business environment. This is aided by the internal capabilities and alignments 
that promote information to be shared within the firm to enhance planning and operational 
alignment. Supplier integration is critical for organisations to achieve information intelligence 
for efficient operations performance (Chen et al., 2018).     
Despite the widespread use of supplier integration, research in the field has shown inconsistent 
results about the relationship between supplier integration and performance (Duhaylongsod 
and De Giovanni, 2019). More research on supplier integration and performance relationship 
is needed, in particular, to better understand the causes of supplier integration and the results 
of integration efforts that are related to suppliers, according to several researchers who 
expressed concerns about the inconsistent findings (Lu et al., 2018). It is emphasised the need 
to look into the causes of supplier integration as well as supplier-related outcomes from 
integration efforts. With the growing significance of supply chain integration, it is crucial to 
record the performance results of various supply chain integration dimensions. This research by 
creating the scale for the immediate performance outcome of supplier integration, specifically 
contributed to the literature on supply chain integration (SRPO). Given the favourable 
correlation between SRPO and supplier integration, supply chain managers should have 
direction regarding the immediate performance benefits they can anticipate from supplier 
 
74 
 
integration, which will ultimately improve the operational performance of the company 
(Afshan and Motwani, 2021).  
Supplier integration also complements a firm’s effort in meeting consumer needs and helps it 
to respond to changes in the business environment. Customer needs vary with the changing 
technology, which makes the organisational operations obsolete and unable to provide the right 
requirements to fit the current market. Supplier integration is important in this respect to inform 
organisations on such anticipated changes for them to utilise the total maintenance practices to 
align the technologies with the market demands (Chen et al., 2018). A lack of supplier 
integration can not only lead to reduced operational performance, but also increase the costs of 
production. This is because creating products that do not meet the target market forces firms to 
modify the products at a later date, which increases the costs of production. Relying on 
suppliers to provide this timely intelligent is, thus, important to inform the management on the 
needed technological maintenance (Koech and Ronoh, 2015). 
Supplier integration creates several benefits for the organisations. The business environment 
fluctuates on a day-to-day basis providing both opportunities and threats for a firm. Supplier 
integration enlightens firms about the existing risks and, thus, improves the operational 
performance. For instance, an anticipated shortage in the supply of certain products is crucial 
for firms to modify their operations and to increase their operational performance. In instances 
where firms lack such information, they may not be able to align their operations to gain 
competitive advantages (Wong et al., 2011). Just-in-time practices and TQM requires quality 
information to provide the right adaptive measures to mitigate the risks in the external 
environment. Integration with suppliers enables firms to gain insights into supplier capabilities, 
processes, and constraints, which as a result inform internal planning and forecasting (Danese, 
2013).  
Supplier integration can be done in different forms, including process integration. Process 
integration with suppliers is also important in enabling firms to meet their goals such as 
financial goals. Process integration enables firms to create synchronised work processes across 
the supply chain. This provides an advantage to a firm by bringing suppliers into the internal 
circle of the business. Suppliers are considered as a force in the business external environment, 
which also known as the macro-environment. Suppliers as a force in the macro-environment 
factors have an adverse effect on the firm’s performance. Maintaining a good relationship with 
suppliers is a move that promotes firms success ambitions (Wieland et al., 2016). Porter 
 
75 
 
explains that high bargaining power of suppliers pose threats to a firm. Higher bargaining 
power of suppliers implies reduced competitive advantage in a firm; therefore, firms need to 
increase their capabilities to gain competitive advantages. These strategies include process 
integration with suppliers to increase transactional efficiencies to solve problems. Integrating 
the supplier in the internal operations of all stages of product development and planning enables 
both the firm and suppliers to coordinate better decision-making (Tozan and Ertürk, 2015). 
This includes the desired maintenance in the firm to arrive at increased gains in operational 
efficiencies. Process integration with suppliers, hence, positively influences the performance 
of firms.  
2.19.  Business Impact of Internal Integration 
Internal integration for a firm involves the consideration of how key stakeholders work together 
to determine and achieve the value that is expected from the final output. Put differently, 
internal integration for a firm refers to the adoption of strategies that work across organisational 
boundaries or departments. The focus of internal integration is the elimination of departmental 
silos that adversely affects the achievement of the overall organisational goals. Organisational 
success nowadays relies on healthy relationships between stakeholders to enable achieving the 
internal goals. Success and excellence require the accomplishment of internal collaboration 
among all stakeholders to ensure their full participation in responding to the changing needs of 
customers (Chen et al., 2018; Ahmad et al., 2019). Alongside the goal of internal integration 
for organisational success, firms engage in TQM as a tool for ensuring that they address their 
quality needs and issues across the value chain. The focus on ensuring the total quality is 
associated with the goal of securing the continuous availability of the most used resources. The 
focus of the research has been on the significance of responding to organisational needs by 
integrating employees and getting them to work together. The strength of employee’s 
integration can have a serious impact on organisational goals and excellence in several aspects 
such as quality, cost, and responsivity (Wieland et al., 2016). 
Internal systems are critical to the achievement of organisational goals because of the need to 
work together to avoid inefficiencies. Since internal systems enhance the performance of firms 
by enabling the value chain to operate effectively, their role is considered significant to respond 
to the weaknesses of inefficient coordination that adversely affects organisational outcomes. 
Inadequate coordination and communication among internal stakeholders (e.g. employees, 
departments) can influence the operational performance of the organisation. The functional 
 
76 
 
departments of an organisation need to coordinate their activities to ensure that they effectively 
respond to all issues and that they can communicate effectively regarding challenges in the 
operations. Open communication systems and strategies can influence the costs incurred by 
firms because they eliminate the likelihood of error occurrence (Flynn and Flynn, 2005). 
Although internal integration is proved to be beneficial in all types of organisations but it’s 
even more beneficial for the manufacturing firms (Ahmad et al., 2019). Manufacturing 
organisations' internal integration can improve the supply chain's (SC) collaboration to balance 
supply and demand. A system that is installed inside in a business to integrate all internal 
functions is known as internal integration. Customer and logistics collaboration integration, 
which is an example of external integration, has an impact on SC partnerships (Tarigan, et al., 
2021). SC resilience is impacted by internal integration in Malaysian manufacturing 
companies. In order to promote organisational agility, internal integration is performed in 
businesses through coordinating business information systems at the operational level, 
specifically, structural and social collaboration (Tarigan, et al., 2021). 
When internal integration is targeted to improve the operational performance of the 
organisations, it results in enhancing the profitability of the operations as well. Some of the 
most important factors that are considered when determining the profitability of an operation 
are the capacity to reduce costs while improving the operation efficiency. Cost effectiveness of 
an operation arises from having integrated and coordinated internal functions. The internal 
functions of a firm need to work together in coordinating responsivity to market changes as 
well as communicating how to compete effectively. The cost performance of a firm presents a 
significant point of competitiveness as it affects the capacity of using the available resources 
to capture higher value of money (Phan et al., 2019). Internal integration involves ensuring that 
all employees are onboard and that they can work together to achieve quality and excellence. 
With close coordination and cooperation among the internal stakeholders, firms can benefit 
from better responsivity to customer needs and lower costs associated with stock errors and 
outages. Employees can coordinate activities as with the case of maintenance and order 
processing to avoid delays that may cause loss of customers (Danese, 2013). 
Organisations now do not adopt any one approach to achieve their core objectives. Rather 
different approaches are adopted and implemented in parallel to have even more efficient 
outcomes (Ahmad et al., 2019). Linking internal integration of stakeholders and the application 
of TQM helps in determining and accomplishing organisational excellence. As indicated in 
 
77 
 
prior studies, such as Ahmad et al (2019), TQM is strongly linked to business performance. 
This indicates to the success of TQM as a tool that promotes responsivity to organisational 
needs by eliminating unnecessary costs and wastage across all levels. With internal integration, 
stakeholders can work together without departmental boundaries towards the same excellence 
goals. Functional boundaries do not present a barrier to knowledge sharing and the 
implementation of strategies for improving quality along the value chain. This means that 
internal integration can support the development of effective TQM strategies and their 
application in achieving the expected excellence levels such as high profitability, low costs, 
and improved quality (Huo, GU, and Wang, 2018).  
2.20.  The Role of Supplier Integration 
Strategic integration of a firm with its suppliers is important to reduce internal conflict through 
aligning targets and objectives. Involving suppliers in the strategic planning in an organisation 
creates analogy in attaining goals between partners in the supply chain (Lu et al., 2018). This 
creates a platform for building long lasting relationships and alignment of TPM practices that 
enhance firm operations. Existing studies indicate that there is an improvement in the 
operational performance for firms that implement supply chain risk management. Performance 
benefits that occur to firms that implement supply chain risk management include the 
prevention of disruptions and low operational deficiencies. Supply chain risk management 
provides firms with the opportunity to react to external environment and to improve operational 
performance. This is made possible by integrating suppliers and customers in the TPM, which 
increases operational performance through mitigating uncertainty in the business environment 
(Gurahoo and Salisbury, 2018). 
Strategic interdependence and formulation of common goals between firms and their suppliers 
reduces opportunistic behaviour and minimises the risks within the supply chain. In addition, 
strategic integration reduces the costs of production and transaction, while still promoting 
relationship investments (Flynn et al., 2010). This is also enhanced by the leveraged resources 
and knowledge. Making the supplier part of the strategy when planning allows firms to be 
functionally fit and achieve their goals. This creates trust between suppliers and firms to ensure 
that attaining a competitive advantage that enable firms to respond to the changes in customer 
needs and preferences (Ahmad et al., 2019). 
Firms that do not focus on the supplier part of their strategic plans ignore many competitive 
advantages because they fail to detect the changes that occur in the supply chain and risk losing 
 
78 
 
their market share to the competitors. Strategic performance, therefore, directly associates with 
better firm outcomes. Strategic contingency theory holds that responding to changes in the 
business environment depends on the firm’s strategic decision to adopt market fit strategy 
(Flynn et al., 2010); that is, the ability to analyse the changes in the external environment and 
equally responding to them by adopting the right strategies. TPM advocates for proactive 
approaches to addressing the anticipated changes in the supplier and customer tastes and habits. 
Moreover, supplier is vital sources of market intelligence to advice firms on the most important 
aspects of products to enhance its absorption in the market. In this case, collaborating with 
suppliers promotes market intelligence in firms and, hence, supports the adoption of relevant 
strategies to enable improved operational performance in a firm (Flynn and Flynn, 2005; 
Ahmad et al., 2019). 
The RBV supports the supplier integration to manage cross-firm business processes through 
entailing strategic information sharing and collaboration between a focal firm and its suppliers. 
When referring to services, capacity can be thought of similarly to inventory in the 
manufacturing industry in that it enables a supply chain to raise production in response to 
demand. As a result, information flows in the service supply chain, including feedback and 
information sharing, are crucial because they enable efficient management of uncertainties 
surrounding client demand. For the coordination of all actions between service providers and 
their supplier partners, it has been determined that information flow is very important. For the 
efficient coordination of all major operations in the service supply chains, information flow 
and technology management are regarded to be crucial (Teng, and Tsinopoulos, 2021). 
Supplier integration helps associations in accomplishing the stock proficiency like just 
conveying the required stock while guaranteeing the further accessibility when it is needed by 
the manufacturing facility. It helps reducing costs just as saving the space at assembling 
facility. Associations couldn't appreciate such advantages without any supplier incorporation 
in light of the fact that in such case they might need to confront specific postponements also 
(Kwasi et al., 2019). A study of Amoako-Gyampah (2019) contend that organisations having 
great supplier connections alongside coordinated inside frameworks have upgraded 
adaptability to react as per the clients' changing prerequisites just as vacillations on the lookout 
and industry. Associations' capacity to be versatile and adaptable to fluctuating business sector 
circumstances is critical in achieving the upper hand in industry. Coordination at supplier and 
 
79 
 
interior levels work with associations to react so rapidly and successfully that would never be 
conceivable without such joining. 
Integrated networks with suppliers help achieving organisational objectives in different 
perspectives. Supplier combination helps decreasing the conveyance costs as well as helps 
controlling the quality issues, accessibility issues and postpones prompting a superior 
assistance for clients. Additionally, improvement of a decent correspondence stream among 
providers and makers brings about fostering an intensive comprehension of the finished results 
which upgrades suppliers' capacity to serve the business appropriately (Amoako-Gyampah, 
2019). TQM practices are not just cantered on upgrading the nature of items yet additionally 
have critical accentuation on decreasing the expenses to further develop benefit. Henceforth, 
supplier combination contributes fundamentally towards quality administration rehearses in 
light of the fact that being in shared helpful relationship suppliers likewise take a stab at saving 
expenses at various phases of item advancement (Barbara et al., 2010). 
Supplier integration also helps achieving the cost objectives of the organisations. Cost can give 
a service provider a competitive advantage, just like it can in manufacturing. A company is 
more likely to receive reliable supply information with a high level of information exchange 
and collaborative planning with suppliers, which will improve service delivery plans and save 
inventory and capacity costs (Wang et al., 2018). Consequently, supplier integration allows 
service providers to take advantage of service delivery economies and reduce service prices. 
Additionally, supplier integration encourages cost effectiveness. Given that one of the 
cornerstones of SCM is information sharing, this outcome was anticipated. In contrast to typical 
industrial supply chains, the relationship between supplier integration and cost performance in 
services is built on the flow of information rather than tangible items (Teng, and Tsinopoulos, 
2021). 
SCM practices especially focused on supplier integration, not only creates benefits for the 
manufacturing firms, but the service sector can also achieve their targets through 
implementation of these approaches. Results clearly show that the service sector can benefit 
from lessons learnt about the function of supplier integration in SCM research. Managers in 
service businesses will benefit from the findings' insights into the operational effects of 
developing levels of supplier integration. Without effective service supply management, a 
company is probably spending too much, losing leverage, becoming more complex, and 
running a higher risk of going out of business (Liu et al., 2019). Furthermore, in this context, 
 
80 
 
services shouldn't be seen as a single homogeneous category. For instance, there are disparities 
between shops who maintain some physical inventory and consulting businesses whose people 
costs account for the majority of costs. When seeking to benchmark integration procedures 
across service industries, managers must exercise prudence. When constructing a level, it is 
crucial for service organisations to take sector-specific variables into account in relation to 
supplier integration (Prajogo et al., 2018).  
Supplier integration helps working on the progression of tasks by taking out the postponements 
from providers' sides which eventually bring about end of deferrals in end result conveyance. 
It additionally helps controlling the variances in market costs of the items. As value vacillations 
adversely influence the client assumptions (Yang et al., 2009), however the centre reason for 
TQM practices is to meet the clients' assumptions so supplier incorporation likewise helps 
dispensing with the adverse consequence of such changes. Through creating sound associations 
with the suppliers through their incorporation, producers might take advantage of fixed 
expenses of materials for lengthier agreement terms. Also, having an unmistakable expense 
construction of the provisions helps associations organising their costs with next to no 
equivocalness that might hurt the clients' assumptions in future (Marian and Soare, 2020). 
A drawn-out connection between the two players through supplier incorporation considers free 
progression of thoughts and criticism over the long run. Such sort of smoothed out compelling 
chain brings about sure effect on both; client administrations and expenses at the two ends. 
Carrying out TQM focuses on the parts of new item improvement, starting new requesting 
cycles and control of stock turns into a joint endeavour that outcome in critical functional and 
monetary advantages at the two ends of the affiliation. Working intimately with the suppliers, 
associations can fit their store network as indicated by their singular requirements which 
additionally helps them solidifying the cycles, decrease of expenses and eventually upgrades 
in the finished result for the clients (Amoako-Gyampah, 2019).  
2.21.  The Role of Internal Integration 
The issue of internal integration is associated with the performance of businesses because it 
promotes knowledge management and sharing. Effective integration across the organisation 
reduces the potential of organisational silos, where departments and functions are working in 
isolation from each other. Internal integration has been cited as a beneficial tool for promoting 
organisational integration and improving the collaboration within an organisation (Tozan and 
 
81 
 
Ertürk, 2015). In relation to TPM, internal integration is a key mediator that can promote 
organisations’ capacity to adopt a holistic approach (Swink and Schoenher, 2015).  
The conceptual link between TPM and internal integration comes from the assumption that 
they both require the collection of internal information for stakeholders to address critical 
organisational issues. It is expected that effective integration of internal functions and strategies 
can improve the performance of an organisation to better respond to its core issues including 
maintenance practices, planning operations and responsivity to market needs (Huo, 2012).  It 
is expected that internal integration contributes to the practice of TPM; having all internal 
functions and stakeholders working together allows implementing the key elements of TPM in 
the operation such as knowledge sharing, effective maintenance planning and the involvement 
of all employees in autonomous maintenance. The focus of TPM is, therefore, to empower all 
employees within an organisation to participate in proactive maintenance and develop a fault 
free operating environment. This requires the involvement of all organisational stakeholders in 
determining the occurrence of faults and maintenance challenges and mitigating them, which 
promotes effective production planning and low maintenance and capital expenses (Iqbal et al., 
2018).  
Internal integration is majorly focused on information sharing and integrating data associated 
with different levels of operations of the organisations. It incorporates looking for supply, 
operation, and demand information in real-time, and integrating operations across several 
functional areas. Cross-functional collaboration, or teaming up across several functions for 
process improvement, is another aspect of internal integration (Feng et al., 2013). The value of 
internal integration in raising performance has been stressed more and more. Lack of internal 
integration may lead to conflicting efforts, resource waste, and wasted effort, all of which can 
be damaging to the effectiveness of a business. Internal integration enhances the connections 
between various functions by removing spatial and hierarchical boundaries. People from many 
departments within the company can collaborate and share information thanks to improved 
horizontal linkages (Feng et al., 2013). 
The approaches of quality management and productive maintenance are considered to be 
incomplete without internal integration (Amoako-Gyampah, 2019). Wakchaure et al. (2014) 
point to the urgent need to treat TPM as an integrated effort across the organisation. This 
integration raises the ability of stakeholders to succeed through effectively intervening in all 
production processes to attain better benefits for the organisation. Effective internal integration 
 
82 
 
is, therefore, necessary as a tool for promoting the implementation of TPM. Since the 
modalities of TPM require actively involving all internal stakeholders, having a high level of 
internal integration can improve the effectiveness and relevance of TPM in promoting 
organisational success (Huo, Gu, and Wang, 2018). High levels of internal integration allow 
firms to respond to internal maintenance, which improves the outputs of the production lines. 
The degree to which departments and employees collaborate to address organisational 
challenges decides their ability to effectively implement TPM and derive benefits from it 
(Iqbal, Huq, and Bhutta, 2018). 
Internal integration does not only establish the benefits at operational level, but for all 
integrated departments as well. Through improved information exchange and cross-functional 
collaboration, internal integration is said to create better intra-firm goal alignment among 
various functions (such as purchasing, planning, manufacturing, marketing, and logistics). 
Internal integration also makes it possible to use the skills and strengths of each function, which 
can enhance organisational performance (Huo et al., 2018). Internal integration also broadens 
the information that manufacturers can access, which lessens their level of uncertainty when 
responding to consumer demand. These benefits of internal integration offer a foundation for 
wise decision-making. A time-efficient preparation of the resources required to meet client 
demand, for instance, can be made possible by early information from the marketing and 
buying departments. Additionally, regular exchanges and debate with opposing viewpoints 
may lessened errors and waste, do away with unreasonable procedures, avoid delays, present 
opportunities for simplification, and prompt market response (Huo et al., 2018).  
Internal integration further ensures the effectiveness of quality management approaches. 
Amoako-Gyampah (2019) believes that internal integration improves the success of TQM 
implementation since it not only allows for the open flow of information across departments, 
but it also allows employees to share their expertise when it is needed, particularly during the 
equipment handling stage. As a result, it aids in the development of a more focused and 
competent team environment, which improves operational performance by streamlining 
workflow across the organisation. Furthermore, rather of operating alone, the cost-sharing 
mentality aids in lowering operational expenses without sacrificing quality or meeting 
deadlines (Kwasi et al., 2019). 
Internal integration also increases the consistency of management systems in the business, 
reducing the complexity of management systems and assisting employees in developing a 
 
83 
 
better knowledge of corporate goals. Employees that have a clear knowledge of the 
organisation's goals collaborate to achieve them. Employees that have a clear knowledge of the 
organisation's goals work collaboratively to achieve them, such as optimising the organisation's 
resources and processes to save expenses. It also aids in minimising the time it takes for staff 
to complete particular tasks, as well as eliminating disruptions and delays, all of which lead to 
cost savings (Talib at al., 2011). Furthermore, Internal integration entails coordinated efforts 
that are far more than the sum of their parts, resulting in increased efficiency and effectiveness 
of organisational operations. (Amoako, 2019) show that internal integration has a considerable 
beneficial influence on TQM practise implementation in terms of improved quality, safety, and 
operational performance. It also aids in the elimination of risks related with production 
efficiency as well as errors at various phases of product development. Internal integration also 
has a substantial favourable impact on the consistent understanding of corporate objectives, 
policies, resources, and processes, which improves the efficient use of organisational resources. 
Internal integration improves the efficiency of overall quality management strategies used in 
the organisation. TQM methods are primarily concerned with improving product quality while 
minimising waste, expenses, and delays. When management systems or diverse operations 
within an organisation are integrated, each function aligns itself toward a common goal, hence 
increasing the organisation's total operational performance (Thakur and Panghal, 2021). 
Furthermore, because effective implementation of quality management principles necessitates 
employee training and assessment, internal integration improves the efficacy of training by 
facilitating the flow of information among employees from various areas within the business. 
Integration of numerous roles creates a unified set of processes, resources, and goals that are 
aligned with the system and improve accountability, all of which contribute to increased 
operational performance (Mendez and Ramon, 2017). 
2.22.  SCM and the Resource Based View 
The RBV enables the organisations for determination of their core competencies which are 
really critical for attaining the targeted outcomes of enhancement of organisational 
performance (Huo et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2018). So, the RBV is also applicable to the SCM 
of the organisation because the resource-based view emphasises maintenance of key activities 
through collaboration of different stakeholders i.e. supplier collaboration and internal key 
functions that can create competitive advantage for the organisation (Shibin et al., 2020). So, 
 
84 
 
the resource-based view considers all stakeholders, involved at any stage of the supply chain, 
as a key resource for organisation that could be incorporated efficiently for enhancing the 
efficiency of supply chain. Although active collaboration of these stakeholders i.e., suppliers 
and customers are not necessary at all stages of decision making process, but still their 
collaboration can enhance organisational performance and could create a competitive 
advantage for the organisation (Huo et al., 2016; Nandi et al., 2020).  
As seen by the increasing emphasis on environmental sustainability in SCM research, 
sustainable SCM has elevated to the top of the research agenda. Sustainability, and in particular 
environmental sustainability, has come to be seen as a crucial component of contemporary 
supply chains and SCM paradigms. As a result of recent events, such as the COVID-19 
pandemic and the ensuing supply chain crisis, which highlighted the risks of pushing the 
planet's limits and continuing with the traditional approaches to SCM, there has been a growing 
recognition in the field that the world cannot afford unsustainable supply chains 
(Karamchandani et al., 2021).  
2.22.1. Resource Based View towards Supply Chain Integration 
The resource based view has major focus on integration of internal resources of the 
organisation for gaining the competitive edge but it also emphasises that the actual value of 
internal resources could only be determined through the environment surrounding the 
organisation including external resources i.e. suppliers, hence integrating these external 
resources with the internal sources of the organisation could provide efficient results in terms 
of enhanced organisational performance (Shibin et al., 2020). Thus, in this way the resource-
based view could be implemented for the management of supply chain for achieving the 
targeted outcomes in effective and efficient manner (Nandi et al., 2020).  
The resource-based view is now not limited to focusing on the internal resources of 
organisation and their efficient management rather it also emphasises on considering the 
external sources of information as a significant resource for organisation. That’s why 
successful organisations emphasise on information sharing among different stakeholders 
involved at different points of the supply chain (Huo et al., 2016). Organisations even rely on 
information sharing in supply chains for developing coordination among processes at different 
points of their supply chains including internal production processes, customer and sales 
services and procurement processes as well (Dong et al., 2009). In fact, in dynamic business 
 
85 
 
environments, the acquisition of appropriate and sufficient information has become vital for 
the survival of organisations and the flow of information in supply chains helps smoothing the 
flow of other business operations like inventory management, finances etc. Moreover, the flow 
of information helps creating integration among internal and external resources of the 
organisations e.g. internal integration requires strong coordination among internal resources 
while supplier integration requires strong coordination among suppliers and the organisation, 
and all such coordination depends on the quality of information being shared at both ends which 
serves as a significant resource that contributes signiﬁcantly in enhancing the performance of 
business operations (Huo et al., 2016). Based on the above literature review, the previous 
researchers have been summarized in the form of table which is as follows:
