Chapter 3 Research Models and Hypotheses Development 
After discussing the theoretical foundation of the different research constructs, this chapter 
sheds the light on discussing the various research models, which are related to the current study. 
Additionally, the chapter incorporates an investigation regarding the previous work efforts, 
those are reviewed for the aim of developing the study’s hypotheses. The current chapter is 
also focusing on providing the basic theoretical underpinning for data analysis, research 
findings and research contributions. 
 
 
3.1. Hypotheses Development 
3.1.1. The impact of TPM on operational performance 
3.1.1.1. 
Impact of TPM on Cost 
TPM is seen as a comprehensive program for the improvement that is originated from TQM’s 
idea of zero production defects applied to equipment. In this regard, Sahoo (2018) argued that 
TPM is a program that has a major concern of reducing the number of losses in an organisation. 
Therefore, TPM declines the availability losses, product quality losses and performance losses 
by optimising the overall equipment performance. Reducing the aforementioned losses in a 
firm cut down on reworking the defects in products and the production process. Sahoo and 
Yadav (2020) found that TPM significantly contributes to a firm’s operational performance. 
More specifically, positive impacts were observed on the firm’s quality, operational 
performance, and costs. While TPM impact on costs reduction among other benefits that are 
celebrated in many industries across the world, the Indian manufacturing firms still hold 
divergent perspectives; the implementation of TPM is capital intensive and that results are 
long-term (Sahoo and Yadav, 2020). TPM is a proactive problem-solving program that reduces 
equipment defects to ensure consistent operational performance and supply of quality products. 
The timeliness approach to problem-solving in a firm through continuous or autonomous 
maintenance ensures that operational performance costs are minimised (Huo, Gu, and Wang, 
2018).  
 
93 
 
The practice of TPM involves a proactive problem-solving strategy, through which the focus 
will be on reducing production defects that may adversely influence the performance of an 
operation. The approach addresses maintenance in a highly dynamic way through the pillars of 
autonomous maintenance, planned maintenance and focused improvement and training of the 
employees. TPM concentrates on improving the operating environment and operational 
processes in an organisation. The approach is geared towards addressing the operational 
challenges facing an organisation such as equipment failure losses, idling and minor stoppage, 
adjustment losses, defects and rework losses and start-up losses (Ahuja and Khamba, 2008). 
During the TPM implementation, the maintenance department allocates more resources and 
time to preventive maintenance with the aim of addressing the identified losses.   
The focus of these interventions is to address the losses an organisation may suffer by providing 
cost-saving benefits. The implementation of TPM strategies in operations provides cost-saving 
benefits that are associated with having zero waste and reduced maintenance costs that are 
associated with higher maintainability of the equipment. According to Wakjira and Ajit (2012), 
planned maintenance allows lower maintenance costs by 20% and improves the reliability of 
equipment by 50%.  
The relationship between TPM and business performance has mostly been associated with the 
development of a working organisational environment in which a competitive advantage is 
achieved through cost and quality improvement. These issues provide savings in terms of the 
higher performance and reduced equipment failure and stoppages. Management focuses on 
integrating total quality and maintenance into their operational strategies. The critical aspect 
from the empirical evidence on the practice is that TPM has a synergistic effect on business 
performance as it acts as a moderator of the impact of TQM implementation on the operational 
performance of businesses by managing their overall costs. TPM was found to help achieving 
these goals by improving the operational performance, safety, hygiene, and delivery time of 
organisations (Ahmad et al., 2019).  In this regard, researchers including Ahmad et al. (2019) 
argued that TPM helps organisations eliminating the internal failure costs especially that are 
incurred due to machine break downs because when equipment’s and machinery are under 
supervision of the operators, they keep these equipment’s well maintained and in efficient 
working conditions hence eliminating the defects in operational performance. Similarly, if any 
problem occurs, they are able to resolve it immediately without disturbing the production 
process and the final products. Hence, as a result the final products are always defect-free. 
 
94 
 
TPM also helps organisation eliminating the external failure costs. External failure costs are 
the quality costs bear by the organisations in case the defect in products is identified by the 
customers after product delivery. It is considered as one of the most expensive classes of quality 
costs. It includes product returns, warranty claims, repairs and most importantly the lost 
reputation and ultimately the loss of business of the organisation. Hence, quality management 
and TPM help organisations eliminating these costs by ensuing high quality end products being 
delivered to the customers (Marian and Soare, 2020). In addition, research on the use of TPM 
in varied contexts has focused on the effects of the interventions on cost aspects.  
Aspinwall and Elgharib (2013) evaluated the impact of TPM on different aspects of operational 
performance. Their results indicated that cost reduction was scored as the most important factor 
or variable. It was among the most significant aspects of measuring the operational 
performance of organisations followed by service delivery and quality. The lower costs 
incurred by organisations were derived from having lower water bills and cost of energy, lower 
maintenance costs and lower inventory levels. The implementation of TPM was found to be 
effective in producing the expected benefits as it focused on cost optimisation activities that 
are significant in aligning the goals with the mission and vision of organisations (Ahmad et al., 
2019). From the explored literature, it is apparent that the implementation of TPM produces 
organisational benefits by effectively improving the management of their costs. These 
improvements occur from eliminating unnecessary complications and concentrating on cost 
optimisation. Thus, best on the above discussion proposed research hypothesis is as follows:  
H1a: Total productive maintenance practice (TPM) has a positive effect on cost performance. 
3.1.1.2. 
Impact of TPM on Flexibility 
Customers in the contemporary business world have constantly changing demands for products 
that should meet the highest quality standards at a favourable price (Sahoo, 2019). This, 
coupled with the volatile supplier requirements, directly impacts the value chain. In addition to 
the above, globalisation as a complex concept requires businesses to strategise on the most 
efficient ways to conduct their daily activities. TPM is a science of machinery health that 
integrates production and maintenance through promoting team working, good working 
practices, adopting novel technologies and continuous improvement of machinery through 
preventive as well as autonomous maintenance practices (Kaur et al., 2013).  
 
95 
 
TPM promotes good working conditions by involving everyone in the maintenance process, 
which eventually impacts the performance flexibility. The adoption of new technologies and 
promoting team working can positively influence decision-making in a firm, which, thus, 
reduces the gap of competition between firms. TPM, being a production-driven methodology, 
optimises reliability in equipment and ensures efficiency in plant utilisation by involving and 
empowering employees (Kumar et al., 2014). Involving employees in the firm practices and 
decision-making, helps designing jobs in a manner that enhances operational flexibility to 
match consumer specifications. This allows achieving the organisational objectives through 
ensuring optimal conditions of assets (Hooi and Leong, 2017).  
From a theoretical perspective, since TPM is a production-driven approach for improving the 
operational performance of an operation, it can respond directly to the needs of customers. The 
approach improves the flexibility and reduces the costs of operations since all stakeholders are 
involved in preventive and scheduled maintenance (Marian and Soare, 2020). Additionally, as 
long as the equipment is in a good working order, changing the production schedules and 
volumes can be effectively accomplished (Kaur et al., 2013).  Accordingly, empirical research 
on the practice of TPM has investigated the different performance measures and considerations 
that improve the operational performance of organisations. Wickramasinghe and Perera (2016) 
explored the application of TPM in the manufacturing industry. Their findings indicated a 
positive association with aspects of manufacturing performance such as product quality, 
delivery, and volume flexibility. Planned maintenance enhances the flexibility of operations 
through strategies that raise the capacity of firms to change (Kumar et al., 2014; Mishra et al., 
2019).  
The effectiveness of TPM as a tool for enhancing operations is associated with the capacity to 
influence volume flexibility. Motivation management and small group activities are considered 
the key aspects in ensuring that all employees actively contribute to the required improvements. 
Wickramasinghe and Perera (2016) focused on the implementation of lean manufacturing 
strategies in manufacturing firms. One of the key benefits of implementing lean manufacturing 
was that it improved the level of volume flexibility. This refers to the capacity of the firms to 
respond to changes in demand and inventory levels and, thus, to maintain low inventory and 
reduce stock outages. With TPM interventions, firms can alter their production schedules, 
especially when having different aspects of program implementation. Differing views have 
been presented by empirical evidence such as the work from McKone et al. (2001) who found 
 
96 
 
that the implementation of TPM was not significantly associated with flexibility. McKone et 
al. indicated that the potential reason for the non-significant relationship was the way in which 
flexibility was measured. Additionally, they argued that the small proportion of maintenance 
costs in relation to the total operating costs was a key factor in influencing the association.  
TPM gives the ownership of the machines or equipment’s to the employees and develops a 
feeling of pride in them that enables them to handle the equipment in more efficient manner as 
well as modifying its working according to the demand fluctuations. Organisations can gain 
competitive advantage in the market if they are able to respond according to the changing 
demands of the markets and sometimes these demands are so high that the ones who 
successfully grab this opportunity can beat the markets operational flexibility is quite necessary 
to enjoy such kind of success, while TPM helps organisation attaining such kind of operational 
flexibility (Mendez and Ramon, 2017).  Amoako-Gyampah (2019) argue that TPM contribute 
positively to enhancing the operational flexibility of the organisation because when 
equipment’s and machines are maintained appropriately, they are always in well working 
conditions that they can provide the desired operational performance levels even during 
uncertain times.  
Moreover, TPM enhances the operational efficiency of the equipment’s and machines by 
eliminating the operating issues, defects, and delays so organisations also become able to attain 
the operational flexibility. When equipment’s and machines are always ready to produce 
without any delays and defects then customers’ demands, and needs could be better fulfilled. 
Hence operational flexibility could be achieved through effective implementation of the TPM 
(Thakur and Panghal, 2021).  
Another important aspect of flexibility in production operations is the need to change the 
product mix and other similar aspects of product volume and type. This plays a major role in 
influencing the capacity of the firm to respond to changes in the preferences and expectations 
of customers. Belekoukias et al. (2014) found that TPM had a significant impact on product 
mix and volume flexibility. However, this was associated with the view that the investigated 
firms did not implement the interventions effectively as expected. In the same vein, Hallgren 
and Olhager (2009) presented earlier findings that support the hypothesis that TPM improves 
organisational flexibility and capacity to respond in terms of product mix and volumes (Mishra 
et al., 2019). The relevance of flexibility as an aspect of production operations and firms in 
general is that it enables a firm to void static aspects of their operations that may have a negative 
 
97 
 
effect on their overall outcomes by preventing responsiveness to customer expectations and the 
operating environment in general. Thus, based on above discussion the proposed hypothesis is 
as follows: 
H1b: Total productive maintenance practice (TPM) has a positive effect on flexibility 
performance. 
 
3.1.2. The impact of TQM on Operational Performance 
3.1.2.1. 
Impact of TQM on Cost 
Pham (2020) defines TQM as a management concept that manages people and processes in an 
organisation to ensure the satisfaction of internal and external customers in all stages. TQM 
cumulatively amplify business gains through promoting its growth and good relationships, 
increasing operational performance, mainstreaming communication, and increasing market 
share. Pham (2020) further observed that TQM is a tool that promotes quest for excellence in 
an organisation through reducing defects. Reducing defects in products and processes saves the 
extra costs incurred during the process remedy, which enhances customer trust.  
TQM has been cited as one of the most important strategies for enhancing the overall 
operational excellence and quality for a firm. This is because it promotes the adoption of good 
working relationships and teamwork. The overall tenet of TQM adoption in organisations is to 
reduce defects and provide a production environment where the right quality is achieved at all 
levels of an organisation. It enables customer-oriented strategies to be adopted in responding 
to the needs and requirements of customers, which, hence, reduces the need for reworks and 
waste associated with scrap. Defect reduction in products and processes are achieved through 
focusing on employee training and customer-oriented production. Products are designed to 
meet customer requirements equally reduce the adjustment costs. Kober et al. (2012) asserted 
that implementing TQM in SMEs has positively impacted their performance while reducing 
rework and scrap. The amount of rework and scrap in firms adversely impacts firm costs. An 
increased investment in the prevention costs (TQM practices implementation) leads to the 
reduction of appraisal costs, internal and external failure costs, and overall costs through 
persistent quality control measures (Gupta, et al. 2021).  
Persistent quality control measures and consistent quality levels are adopted by organisations 
to enable controlling and improving the quality of production to meet customer demands. By 
 
98 
 
eliminating defects in products and processes, the implementation of TQM encourages a low-
cost production environment since there are no losses associated with waste. Therefore, TQM 
can improve the cost performance of an organisation through eliminating reworks and waste 
(Wakchaureet al., 2014).   
The integrated manufacturing programs consisting of TQM as a key component of how they 
approach continuous improvement can play a major role in addressing industrial cost issues. 
The optimisation of costs for an organisation involves reducing the unnecessary, and enhancing 
the quality of, production in the first instance. In empirical studies such as Kaynak and Hartley 
(2009), the implementation of TQM alongside just in time (JIT) inventory management has 
had a positive and significant effect on lowering the cost, and ensuring the speed, of delivery 
(Kaynak & Hartley, 2009). The benefits of interventions are increasing the effectiveness of 
operations and lowering the costs by eliminating redundant processes. In an empirical study 
that was conducted by Wicaksono and Bambang (2019), the effect of TQM on quality costs 
was evaluated for SMEs in Java. The findings indicated that the different aspects of TQM, such 
as the focus on customers, obsession with quality, continuous improvement, training, and 
benchmarking, had positive and significant effects on cost efficiency for manufacturers. This 
allows focusing on customer needs and elimination of production waste, which leads to a cost-
effective organisation (Ahmad et al., 2019).  
Developing the understanding of the customers’ wants, demands and expectations has become 
vital for organisations. When the organisations are successfully directing all of their efforts 
towards attaining such kinds of goals the resulting operational performance is always according 
to the expectations of customers (Gupta, et al. 2021). Satisfied customers are always ready to 
pay more for fulfilling their expectations again and again that result in increased sales and 
revenues of the organisations. It also restrains inventory pileup due to rejected and returned 
products and unsold items as well as saves the warranty claims, recall costs and product liability 
costs (Amoako-Gyampah, 2019). Moreover, when organisations take careful consideration of 
customer requirements, the outcome is always according to the customers’ specifications 
thereby eliminating defective products, associated cost and eventually saves the time as well. 
Hence, customer focus significantly contributes in enhanced operational performance by 
eliminating the internal and external failure costs along with assurance of highly demanding 
products that would be sold out immediately (Chaurey et al., 2021). 
 
99 
 
TQM is based on the belief that it’s better for organisations to bar the prevention costs because 
prevention of defects is better than dealing with failures, hence the cost of defect prevention is 
cheaper for organisation than the cost of quality failures (Gupta, et al. 2021). In other words, if 
organisations are able to eliminate defects level, then the total quality costs incurred by the 
organisation are also reduced. Some important quality costs incurred by organisations are 
prevention cost, internal failure cost, appraisal cost and external failure cost that are some kinds 
of extra burden on the organisations while implementation of the TQM practices helps 
eliminating all these kinds of costs (Abdallah and Nabas, 2018).  A study by Amoako-Gyampah 
(2019) debated that organisations have to incur cost in order to make efforts for reducing poor 
quality termed as the prevention cost. It includes improvements in the designs of the products 
to make them durable, providing training to the employees for enabling them to perform a good 
job, certifying suppliers for ensuring the supply of high-quality products and services by the 
suppliers. 
Under systematic management approach, organisational operational performance is enhanced 
through better products, improved responsiveness, shortening of cycle times for novel products 
and services, effective marketing, production, and engineering strategies. Moreover, by 
elimination of errors, wastage and defects, the organisations are also capable of enhancing 
operational performance by having sufficient control on costs (Mishra et al., 2018). The above 
findings indicate that, with effective implementation of TQM in the management of 
organisational operation, the efficiency of cost saving can be understood to be the main 
expected benefit. Keeping the focus on continuous improvement and teamwork for addressing 
customer concerns and lowering potential faults, reduces the costs incurred by an organisation 
in serving its customers (Sila, 2012). The expectation is that with the implementation of TQM 
strategies in organisations, cost effectiveness is a guaranteed outcome. Managers who focus on 
different aspects of TQM enhance all aspects of cost control (Gupta, et al. 2021). It is 
hypothesised from the available literature that TQM implementation improves organisational 
performance in terms of cost management and optimisation. Thus, based on above discussion 
the proposed hypothesis is as follows:  
H2a: Total quality management (TQM) has positive significant influence on cost performance. 
 
 
100 
 
3.1.2.2. 
Impact of TQM on Flexibility  
In today's business environment, customers’ requirements about products vary greatly with the 
increased number of customer’s numbers and their changing preferences. High competition 
due to industrial expansions is immensely pressuring on the firm creativity. Innovation is the 
new normal in the field of business as firms aim to increase their market share and expand their 
operations. Zehir et al. (2012) concluded that TQM provides opportunity for improving a firm’s 
innovative capability and providing goods that meet the quality standards of consumers. 
Enhancing innovation capacity of firms enables them to provide goods and services that fairly 
compete in the global marketplace. Firms’ ability to create new products in a timely manner 
and stay relevant depends on three key success factors: management leadership, customer focus 
and continuous improvement (Gupta, et al. 2021). TQM is a management concept that bridges 
the gap between the management and its employees. Bridging the gap between employees and 
management enables a firm to make informed decisions that lead to integrate the employee 
thinking in product design and customer feedback to launch customer-oriented products. This 
eventually improves flexibility performance in the firm. Studies on this matter have focused on 
the effectiveness of TQM strategies and tools in developing organisations that are able to 
perform in a flexible manner (Phan et al., 2019).   
The issue of flexibility, in relation to the impact of TQM, is associated with the fact that 
consumers are increasingly seeking diversity and variety in their product choices. The 
application of TQM in these settings provides an opportunity for organisations to focus on the 
issues of interest to the customers. A key aspect of TQM is responsiveness to customer needs 
and expectations, which should be considered in making decisions on what and how-to produce 
(Gupta, et al. 2021). The focus of organisations on customer complaints provides more 
opportunities to produce high quality goods. It also allows organisations to improve their 
operational performance by better responding to customer needs.  
TQM involves training and education as well as benchmarking other best practices and 
persistently improving production quality. Such practices and persistence play a beneficial role 
in enhancing the ability of an industrial firm to better compete in its market by allowing more 
flexibility in changing its product mix and well as volumes, which, in turn, helps to achieve its 
strategic objectives. Flexible product mix manufacturing can be considered as a key component 
of organisational success because of the impact it has on enabling modular production 
 
101 
 
strategies and processes for responding to varying demands. In a study from Iqbal et al. (2018), 
the application of TQM was evaluated in conjunction to the use of a just-in-time (JIT) 
production strategy. It was found that TQM application involved a pull production system 
consisting of small lot sizes and low set up time. TQM principles were found to be highly 
beneficial in enabling the agile production strategies that were reliant on common external 
infrastructure and direct relationships with the targeted customers.  
Operational flexibility is found to be closely associated with the TQM practices 
implementation since the two concepts have some common orientations. Organisations adopt 
TQM practices for ensuring improved products’ quality, reduced costs, and enhancement of 
customer satisfaction. Likewise, organisations try to attain flexibility performance that may 
eliminate unnecessary expenses and excessive production costs (Abdallah and Nabas, 2018). 
Amoako-Gyampah (2019) argues that TQM practices provide organisations’ higher adjustment 
ability in their strategies and structural administration, according to the changing market 
requirements. Moreover, it is found that TQM emphasises on moving beyond the firm-centric 
approach towards expansion of quality management practices to up steam and down steam 
supply chain partners so that organisations could be able to cope with high velocity business 
environments.  
Consistently, Gurahoo et al. (2018) evaluated the determinants of service quality in the service 
sector in line with the application of TQM. They identified that the most important aspects of 
TQM practices for the investigated firms were the demand-led production and joint new 
product development. This provides a highly beneficial tool for addressing firm flexibility 
through virtual supply chains and deployment of flexible workforce (Alolayyan et al., 2013). 
One of their key findings indicated that flexible or agile manufacturing required lean 
implementation strategies in order to achieve the expected benefits for organisations. 
Organisations seek to achieve flexibility in production by avoiding unnecessary expenses and 
having high levels of stock. Continuous improvement in daily routines is encouraged through 
TQM implementation. A key aspect of this relationship is that TQM provides higher 
adjustment capability in response to market requirements (Dubey, et al., 2021). 
The effectiveness of these strategies is also associated with the administration of structures that 
enable quality management to be extended to other stakeholders in the supply chain outside the 
firm. Both upstream and downstream supply chain actors must be involved in the 
implementation of TQM (Flynn and Flynn, 2005). These findings present the view that 
 
102 
 
effective implementation of TQM is likely to have a positive influence on the flexibility of 
organisations. Thus, based on above discussion the proposed hypothesis is as follows: 
H2b: Total quality management (TQM) has positive significant influence on flexibility 
performance. 
 
3.1.3. The Impact of TQM on TPM 
TPM is a firm management tool that informs decision-making and helps in job designing, 
problem solving, employee recruitment and selection, performance appraisal and promotion. 
By performing these functions for both small and large firms, a lot of positive impacts have 
been realized: improved firms’ performance, increased market share and employee satisfaction. 
While some firms implement TPM as their main management tool, others integrate both TQM 
and TPM as their management tools, which have brought about significant positive results to 
the respective firms. Konecny (2011) hypothesised that the conjoint implementation of TPM 
and TQM presents more positive results when compared to the implementation of either one. 
Implementing both practices in combination leads to increased benefits due to the fact that they 
complement each other in ensuring quality results for firms. Kaur et al. (2013) held that TPM 
adds to TQM practices and techniques to ensure zero defects in equipment. TQM and TPM 
share commonalities regarding to employee involvement in the management practice, cross 
functional approach, and continuous improvement. TQM is, thus, an important tool that aids 
TPM in many managerial aspects within the industrial firms.  
From a theoretical perspective, the implementation of TPM evolved with the concepts of lean 
management and they both share similar elements. This is because TPM is meant to reduce or 
eliminate equipment failure. Many scholars consider TPM as an innovative practice to machine 
maintenance that complements TQM and other manufacturing strategies, including continuous 
improvement and JIT production (McKone and Schroeder, 2001). This means that applying 
the two strategies is simultaneously meant to enhance the capacity of an organisation to respond 
to its challenges and improve its output quality. Thus, it is clear from the conceptual aspects of 
the two methodologies that they are similar in many ways, especially their focus on involving 
all employees and stakeholders in addressing quality and operational integrity (Dubey, et al., 
2021). While TQM is customer centric in which it seeks to optimise the overall quality offered 
to customers, TPM maximises equipment effectiveness by engaging all employees. Planned 
maintenance is applied as a key element of the strategy to ensure that all equipment work 
 
103 
 
effectively, while employees are highly motivated to undertake autonomous maintenance 
activities. TPM eliminates breakdowns in machinery and equipment, while TQM focuses on 
reducing defects and waste by ensuring that the right products and other aspects are achieved 
without the need for reworking in order to reduce cost and time.  
The interest in TQM as an approach that enhances TPM implementation is based on the thought 
that both approaches are effective in enhancing firms’ operational performance through holistic 
initiatives that address all critical production and quality aspects. According to Konecny and 
Thun (2011), TQM and TPM are relatively similar approaches for addressing organisational 
issues related to operational performance. A key element of the association is that TPM and 
TQM should be implemented before the adoption of lean production strategies (Dubey, et al., 
2021). There are two sets of critical factors of implementing TPM and TQM that influence 
their effectiveness and applicability. These include universally significant implementation 
factors such as leadership, strategic planning, and process management. These approach 
specific factors are also critical to the implementation of the two strategies such as effective 
equipment management and customer satisfaction. Ahmad et al. (2015) studied the effect of 
TPM as a mediator of the link between TQM and business performance in the automotive 
industry. The results indicated that TQM was a key factor in mediating the association. TQM 
was found to have an indirect effect of 0.25, meaning it has a high mediation effect. The 
findings of Ahmad et al.’s (2015) study, as well as other studies such as the one from Konecny 
and Thun (2011), indicate that TQM is positively correlated with the application of TPM in 
organisations.  
Marian et al. (2020) indicate that TQM practices not only focus on improving the quality of 
products through enhancing quality inputs while reducing costs as well, but it also emphasises 
on achieving the operational efficiency for ensuring the effective utilisation of organisational 
resources. Hence, in the production processes, the equipment’s and machines are among the 
most significantly contributing resources of the organisations, so enhancing the efficiency of 
these resources is also emphasised by TQM (Dubey, et al., 2021). Therefore, TQM emphasise 
on incorporation of TPM in the production process so that instead of wasting time of detecting 
issues when they actual occur in the production process and cause delays, these should be 
controlled at initial stages. So TPM enables employees to keep their equipment’s in good 
working conditions so that delays and defects could be eliminated that is the prime focus of 
TQM practices. Hence, effective implementation of TQM practices contributes significantly in 
 
104 
 
TPM (Chaurey et al., 2021). Moreover, the available literature from different organisational 
settings indicates that the implementation of TPM is correlated with that of TQM. Although 
they can be implemented in a conjoined manner, TPM is a mediator of TQM and, hence, it can 
be hypothesized as such in influencing the outcomes of organisations. Thus, based on above 
discussion the proposed hypothesis is as follows:  
H3: TPM is significantly supported by TQM. 
 
3.1.4. The Impact of Supplier/Internal Integration on TPM 
3.1.4.1. 
Supplier Integration and TPM 
TPM aims at maximising customer satisfaction by reducing equipment defects, continuously 
improving the production process, and involving employees throughout the process. Research 
on the moderating role of supply chain integration in the impacts of TPM is scant (Dubey, et 
al., 2021). The supply chain is a source of important information that enables firms to manage 
consumer needs and preferences. Suppliers provide the information on the changing tastes and 
preferences of customers to firms that, in turn, interpret and design their products to meet these 
tastes (Habidinet al., 2018). Suppliers also enhance the innovation capacity of firms by 
providing the necessary resources that are needed to exceed the usual production norms. Firms 
involve suppliers in all stages to better manage their competition. The changing business 
environment with the entry of many businesses has resulted in an immense pressure on firms. 
Suppliers complement TPM in designing operations that enables producing unique products 
that increase the market share of firms (Jain et al., 2018). Firms that do not implement novel 
management techniques have poor access to market information and, thus, have insufficient 
supplying quality. As a result, they design their work practices without considering other 
important stakeholders, which, eventually, leads to high production defects and costs. Thus, 
involving supplier’s enables firms maintain quality products and increase customer 
satisfaction.  
TPM holistically addresses all organisational aspects of autonomous maintenance through 
empowering and engaging employees at all levels. According to TPM practices, employees 
should be motivated and empowered to take matters in their own hands when addressing the 
key issues in their organisations. In order to achieve the full potential of TPM and TQM, an 
organisation must ensure that the understanding of related strategies is not limited to a specific 
 
105 
 
managerial level. The integration of other stakeholders in the supply chain provides a beneficial 
framework for ensuring that the proposed interventions are applicable at all levels of the 
organisation (Modgil and Sharma, 2016).  
Integrating all stakeholders in the management of an organisation is critical to the achievement 
of organisational goals. In the case of TPM, supplier integration can be considered as a critical 
factor in addressing the overall goals of organisations. This is because it presents a situation 
where the suppliers can be actively involved in the formulation of strategies and development 
of solutions that work within the whole organisation. Operational performance is associated 
with TPM and supplier’s integration as the approach provides a suitable opportunity to address 
all issues affecting the entire value chain of the product or process (Dubey, et al., 2021). The 
four main constructs of TPM include information tracking, disciplined maintenance, and 
housekeeping and operator involvement. In Modgil and Sharma’s (2016) study TPM 
implementation was shown to conduct a critical role in improving plant level operational 
excellence. TPM was also found to have a significant impact on technology management, 
product innovation and research and development research and development. These issues can 
be significantly addressed through the active involvement of all stakeholders such as suppliers 
and customers. 
Supplier integration allows the flow of information from the suppliers to the different units of 
the organisations including the production facility as well. The flow of information in this 
relationship is two ways, like it’s not limited to gain the knowledge and expertise of the 
suppliers, but it also incorporates the involvement of suppliers at decision making stage of 
product development (Thakur and Panghal, 2021). In this way, suppliers are well aware about 
the production capacity and production efficiency of the organisation, so supplier integration 
enhances the effectiveness of TPM in a way that suppliers provide their valuable opinions about 
handling of inventory for improving the operational efficiency of equipment’s and machines. 
Moreover, the availability of inventory also has significant impact on the TPM practices like 
the delays in inventory may lead to machine shut down for short- or long-term basis while the 
flow of information between suppliers and the production employees helps eliminating such 
deficiencies and contributes significantly to enhancing the effectiveness of TPM (Mendez and 
Ramon, 2017).  
It is known that active involvement of suppliers in firm operations increases their responsivity 
and effectiveness in addressing market needs. In this case, the effectiveness of the TPM 
 
106 
 
approach, as well as the outcomes it provides for organisations, is evaluated in relation of the 
involvement of suppliers. Considering that supplier involvement is a key predictor of TQM 
effectiveness, it is expected that the same level of impact and excellence can be achieved with 
TPM. This suggests that integrating suppliers into the operations and strategies of a firm can 
have a significant and positive effect on TPM. Since TPM involves equipment maintenance for 
consistent performance, suppliers can play a key role in providing the necessary support for 
enhancing organisational outcomes. Thus, based on above discussion the proposed hypothesis 
is as follows:  
H4a: Supplier integration has positive effect on TPM. 
3.1.4.2. 
Internal Integration and TPM 
TPM has been known for the great success it has brought to firms. Consistent positive results 
have been reported for many firms across the globe on the importance of TPM. Such impacts 
include increased profitability due to reduced operation costs and improved product quality. 
While these results continue to emerge, there has been constant change being experienced in 
the field of business that require more rigorous and strategic steps to remain relevant over time. 
Swink and Schoenher (2015) posited that internal integration enables firms to gather important 
business information, interpret it and translate it to improve the internal functions to influence 
strategic decisions. TPM practices rely on involving employees in managerial roles. Internal 
integration aids the role of TPM in enhancing the participation of employees in the decisions 
and processes that aim to optimise resource allocation and ensure maximum profits for firms. 
Failure to involve employees through internal integration causes resources loss, which leads to 
increased production costs. Internal integration is argued to increase interfaces in the internal 
functions to simultaneously improve communications and encourage collaborative efforts to 
achieve higher business profitability.  
The issue of internal integration is associated with the performance of organisations because it 
promotes information management and knowledge sharing. Having effective integration across 
the organisation reduces the potential or relevance of organisational silos, where departments 
and functions are isolated from each other. Internal integration has been cited as a beneficial 
tool for promoting integration and collaboration within an organisation. In relation to TPM, 
internal integration is expected to be a key contributor as it can promote the capacity of an 
organisation to adopt a holistic approach (Swink and Schoenher, 2015).  
 
107 
 
The conceptual link between TPM and internal integration is that they both require the 
collection of internal information across the organisation and that they used by stakeholders to 
address critical issues in the organisation. It is expected that effective integration of all internal 
functions and strategies can enhance the performance of an organisation in responding to its 
core issues such as maintenance practices, planning operations and responsiveness to market 
needs. Further, it is logically expected that internal integration supports the practice of TPM. 
By having all internal functions and stakeholders working together, the elements of TPM can 
be implemented in the operations such as knowledge sharing, effective maintenance planning 
and involvement of employees in autonomous maintenance. The focus of TPM is to empower 
employees within an organisation to contribute to maintenance and development of a fault-free 
operating environment. This requires involving all stakeholders in determining the occurrence 
of faults and maintenance challenges to mitigate them, which results in effective production 
planning and low maintenance and capital expenses. 
Internal integration allows the flow of information among different departments and functions 
of the organisation and contributes significantly to eliminating production deficiencies. When 
different functions work in coordination then the magnitude of delays and defects reduces 
(Mendez and Ramon, 2017). Moreover, as in the case of TPM the employees are made 
responsible for the maintenance and cleanliness of their equipment’s and machines, so internal 
integration allows them keeping in touch with the maintenance staff and getting their guidance 
when required. So, such flow of information and collaboration among employees and other 
departments contributes significantly to enhancing the effectiveness of TPM (Chaurey et al., 
2021).  
Wakchaure et al. (2014) indicate the critical need to integrate TPM across organisations. This 
integration of stakeholders raises the probability to success through effective interventions in 
the suitable choice of products (Dubey, et al., 2021). Effective internal integration is, therefore, 
necessary as a tool for promoting the implementation of TPM. Since the modalities of TPM 
require an active involvement of all internal stakeholders, having a high level of internal 
integration can improve the capability of TPM to promote organisational success. The ability 
of a firm to effectively respond to internal challenges in maintenance and ensuring high 
efficiency of the production lines is strongly impacted by the level of internal integration. The 
proper collaboration among employees and departments in addressing challenges also 
 
108 
 
determines the degree to which TPM can be implemented to derive organisational benefits. 
Thus, based on above discussion the proposed hypothesis is as follows:  
H4b: Internal integration has positive impact on TPM. 
 
3.1.5. The Impact of Supplier/Internal Integration on TQM 
3.1.5.1. 
Supplier Integration and TQM 
Garvin (1987) observed that a firm’s capability to produce quality products lies in its ability to 
collaborate with the supply chain. Garvin argued that those firms that cannot establish strong 
relationships with their major suppliers cannot be consistent in producing quality products. 
Managing the supply chain during the implementation of TQM practices results in optimal 
standards of products and lead to customer satisfaction. Rosenzweig et al. (2003) observed that 
when specific suppliers emphasise their own goals at the expense of other suppliers, suboptimal 
quality of processes and products is usually the result. On the contrary, when suppliers work in 
harmony and collaborate together, the technical and task specific know-how accumulates in a 
firm, which improves its operational performance. Supplier integration makes it easy for TQM 
to manage the quality of products by ensuring the technical know-how is shared between the 
various suppliers and the firm for optimal performance. Another important aspect of supplier 
integration is the timely interpretation of information that is received and transformed into 
creative designs that guarantee customer satisfaction through quality products. 
The need for consistency in producing high quality goods and following a demand-driven 
approach, in which inputs from customers are considered to be a major element, is highly 
important. TQM focuses on following quality across the entire value chain by ensuring that 
faults are eliminated, and costs are reduced through minimising the associated reworks of 
operations. The market is a key component of the inputs considered in the implementation of 
TQM because it requires stakeholders to work together in determining what is expected by the 
customers and providing the necessary internal resources to accomplish it (Sahoo and Yadav, 
2020).  In addition, Rosenzweig et al. (2003) observed that high intensity of integration can 
positively influence the product quality and, consequently, customer satisfaction. High 
intensity of integration among the main stakeholders can have a significant impact on 
implementation of effective TQM strategies within organisations.  
 
109 
 
The critical issue in this respect is the significance of integrating suppliers into the internal 
processes and the strategies adopted for TQM implementation (Dubey, et al., 2021). Since 
TQM focuses on ensuring that the right commodities are produced at the right time and with 
no defects, the suppliers play a key role. They must provide material and supplies that are 
effective in responding to the needs of the firm and its customers. Lack of responsiveness by 
the suppliers can be costly for organisations because it would increase the costs of remedying 
defects and returning material to reach the right specifications (Flynn and Flynn, 2005). This 
means that suppliers need to be directly involved and integrated in internal operations and 
strategies of the organisation to ensure that it operates properly and contributes to the 
effectiveness of TQM.  
The core purpose of implementing manufacturing practices like TQM is to enhance the quality 
of products and attaining efficient operational performance according to the demands and 
expectations of customers. On the other hand, SCM is practiced by the organisations for 
controlling manufacturing processes for enhancing product quality and eliminating the risks of 
recalls as well as building a strong customer brand (Amoako-Gyampah, 2019). Hence, both 
approaches have a similar purpose of quality enhancement and customer satisfaction. The only 
difference between both elements is the way to approach the quality i.e., within the organisation 
in case of manufacturing practices (TQM) and across the supply chain in SCM (Marian et al., 
2020). 
SCM and the TQM have been considered as two most significant approaches for firms like 
services, manufacturing, and small and medium enterprises (SME). These approaches have 
become the vital ingredient for organisational success and attaining competitive advantage in 
the industry. Initially these two approaches were not considered to be adopted at a same time 
in an organisation due to similarity of purpose but with the passage of time, the integration of 
these two approaches in an organisation proved to strengthen the competitive advantage of the 
organisation as well as enhanced customer satisfaction (Talib et al., 2011). Hence, in recent 
era, applications of TQM principles in supply chains have become common practices especially 
in the field of SCM (Marian et al., 2020). 
Considering the critical role played by suppliers in enabling the smooth running of operations 
and providing material that enhances the quality of production, the integration of suppliers in 
an organisation is critical to the success of TQM. Empirical research on this link is relatively 
limited and most of the related studies focused on how the interventions promote organisational 
 
110 
 
success and manufacturing excellence. The role played by integrating suppliers requires further 
evaluated to better understand it and provide a clear view of how it acts as a moderating factor 
in promoting organisational success (So and Sun, 2010). The integration of suppliers in the 
development of TQM strategies and frameworks can be highly beneficial in developing a 
powerful organisational environment where the material supplied is in line with the needs of 
the entire value chain. Suppliers contribute to the reduction of the associated costs as well as 
the quality challenges that may arise for the firm. In this respect, the hypothesis is that effective 
supplier integration for a firm is a significant promoter of TQM. Thus, based on above 
discussion the proposed hypothesis is as follows: 
H5a: Supplier integration has a positive effect on TQM. 
3.1.5.2. 
Internal Integration and TQM 
TQM as a managerial concept includes all works conducted to reduce defects in equipment 
through autonomous maintenance. In so doing, TQM relies on the active involvement of 
employees to fix problems that emerge on a day-to-day basis to minimise machinery and 
equipment failure. Internal integration enhances the communication of internal functions by 
creating several interfaces for collaboration. These communication interfaces enhance JIT 
production management, which, thus, increases the firm’s operational performance. Swink and 
Schoenherr (2015) argued that firms that achieve high levels of internal integration experience 
increased profits than their counterparts. Internal integration in a firm may foster strong 
relationships between different functions and promote preferable organisation outcomes. TQM 
builds on these established relationships to better drive quality management initiatives through 
harnessing and utilising feedback to design work practices for maximum profits. Swink et al. 
(2007) mentioned that internal integration lowers the need for asset buffers such as inventory 
through improved planning and increased coordination among functions that are involved in 
the TQM practice. Internal integration, therefore, can be argued to positively influence the role 
of TQM in maximising firm profits.  
Chaudhary et al. (2018) indicate that internal integration enhances the effectiveness of TQM 
practices implemented in the organisation. The main focus and emphasis of TQM practices is 
to improve the quality of products while reducing waste, costs and delays. Therefore, when 
management systems or different functions within the organisation are integrated, then each 
function lines up towards a single goal enhancing the overall operational performance of the 
 
111 
 
organisation. Internal integration incorporates coordinated efforts which are much higher than 
the sum of individual parts which not only improves efficiency but also effectiveness of the 
organisational operations (Mishra et al., 2018).   
Abdallah and Nabas (2018) indicate that internal integration have significant positive impact 
on the implementation of TQM practices in the form of enhanced quality, safety and resulting 
operational performance. It also helps eliminating risks associated with the production 
efficiency as well as helps eliminating defects at different stages of product development. 
Another significant positive impact of internal integration is the uniform understanding of 
organisational objectives, policies, resources, and processes that enhances efficient utilisation 
of organisational resources (Thakur and Panghal, 2021).Moreover, the implementation of 
quality management practices requires training and assessment of employees for effective 
implementation, so internal integration enhances the effectiveness of training through 
appropriate implementation of practices by the flow of information among employees from 
different functions of the organisation. Integration of multiple functions establishes cohesive 
processes, resources, objectives that align with the system and improve accountability that 
ultimately helps improving productive efficiency (Amoako-Gyampah, 2019). 
Internal integration involves the consideration of all key stakeholders in determining the value 
that is expected in the final output and working together to achieve it (Dubey, et al., 2021). 
Internal integration refers to the adoption of strategies that work across organisational 
boundaries or departments. The focus of internal integration is the elimination of departmental 
silos that may adversely affect the achievement of the overall goal of the organisation. 
Organisational success relies on the presence of strong working relationships among 
stakeholders to enable the accomplishment of internal organisational goals. Success and 
excellence require effective accomplishment of internal collaboration among stakeholders to 
ensure that they work together in responding to the changing needs of customers.  
Alongside the goal of internal integration for organisational success, firms use TQM as a tool 
for ensuring that they address their quality needs and issues across the value chain. The focus 
on ensuring total quality is associated with the goal of achieving the highest probable outcome 
using lesser resources. The focus of the research is on the significance of responding to 
organisational needs by integrating all employees and getting them to work together. How 
strongly the employees are integrated in an organisation can have a serious impact on 
organisational goals and excellence in aspects such as quality, cost, and responsiveness.  
 
112 
 
The link between the internal integration of the different stakeholders and the application of 
TQM can be significant in determining the accomplishment of organisational excellence. As 
indicated in prior studies, such as Ahmad et al (2019), TQM is strongly linked to business 
performance. This, hence, contributes to the success of TQM as a tool that promotes high 
responsiveness to organisational needs by eliminating unnecessary costs and wastage across 
all levels. With effective internal integration, all internal stakeholders work together without 
departmental boundaries to achieve shared excellence goals. In this case, functional boundaries 
are no more perceived as barriers to knowledge sharing and the implementation of strategies 
for improving quality along the value chain. In this way, it can support the development of 
effective TQM strategies and applications to generate the expected excellence levels such as 
improved quality, low costs, and high profitability. 
Thakur and Panghal (2021) argued that internal integration improves the consistency of 
management systems in the organisation which eliminates the complexity of management 
system and helps employees developing a better understanding of organisational objectives. 
Employees having good understanding of organisational objectives work together to achieve 
these objectives; like optimising resources and processes of the organisation that result in 
reduced costs. It also helps reducing the time taken for performing certain activities by 
employees as well as eliminating disruptions and delays that also contribute to cost reduction. 
Considering that TQM requires the integration of all stakeholders in the development of 
effective strategies for addressing quality issues across the organisation through customer focus 
and continuous improvement, it should be strongly associated with, and supported by, the level 
of internal integration in organisations. This supports the hypothesis that TQM is significantly 
supported by the level of internal integration in organisations through better communication, 
cooperation, and knowledge sharing. Thus, based on above discussion the proposed hypothesis 
is as follows:  
H5b: Internal integration has a positive effect on TQM. 
 
 
113 
 
3.1.6. The Impact of Supplier Integration on Operational Performance 
3.1.6.1. 
Supplier Integration and Cost Performance 
In a study conducted by Rosenzweig et al. (2003), it was established that the higher percentage 
of revenues received from new products is resulted from the increased supply chain integration. 
Rosenzweig et al. (2003) added that integrating suppliers in the development of new products 
reduces the costs and speeds up the lead time. Integrating suppliers enhances the firm’s 
competitive capacity, which results in better business performance. Supplier integration allows 
firms to achieve cost leadership due to several reasons. First, an integrated business 
environment provides firms with market intelligence. Suppliers can support firms to acquire 
market intelligence through their higher access to information during the interaction with other 
suppliers and customers. Market intelligence helps firms to redesign and redevelop their 
products to fit with customer requirements. Besides, market intelligence helps firms to 
strategise on how to overcome competition. On another perspective, the integration of suppliers 
reduces the firm inventory, which reduces the cost of operations. In so doing, the firm’s overall 
performance is enhanced through redirecting the money invested in inventories to the research 
and development of other productive products and practices. 
Supplier integration is critical for the current business environment where the involvement of 
stakeholders enables organisations to respond to the needs of their customers. Businesses have 
become increasingly competitive and, therefore, require clear frameworks for integrating and 
enhancing value. One such area of cooperation and integration is supplier integration where 
firms develop inter-connected relationships with their suppliers through knowledge exchange 
and SCM information systems. Instead of simply integrating within the organisation, 
knowledge exchange and the expansion of the supply chain to involve upstream and 
downstream players can noticeably enhance organisational performance and operational 
performance. Firms nowadays seek excellence across all fronts and forms with issues such as 
quality management and timely delivery being central to the accomplishment of goals 
(Konecny and Thun, 2011). To achieve these benefits, organisations must cooperate with their 
suppliers to ensure that the right quality is delivered, and changes are effectively communicated 
from the targeted customers. Communication and coordination across the value chain are 
significant in ensuring that customer demands, and preferences are comprehended by all 
internal stakeholders including suppliers to ensure the delivery of the right material. Having an 
ineffective approach to supplier integration can result in additional costs to firms because of 
 
114 
 
stock outages and inventory costs. Receiving the wrong supplies can result in a situation where 
the production does not meet the market standards, which dissatisfies customers.  
TQM practices are not only focused on enhancing the quality of products but also have 
significant emphasis on reducing the costs to improve profitability. Hence, supplier integration 
contributes significantly towards quality management practices because being in mutual 
beneficial relationship suppliers also strive for saving costs at different stages of product 
development (Mishra et al., 2018). Supplier integration not only helps reducing the delivery 
costs but also helps controlling the quality issues, availability problems and delays leading to 
a better service for customers. Moreover, development of a good communication flow between 
suppliers and manufacturers results in developing a thorough understanding of the end products 
which enhances suppliers’ ability to serve the business accordingly (Marian and Soare, 2020). 
However, considering the adverse effects that ineffective supplier integration can have on 
organisational goals and excellence, it is apparent that improving it enhances the overall 
organisation. The critical issue for organisations that apply TQM is the need to ensure that the 
right quality is provided at the right time. Managers also focus on the need to have their 
facilities in perfect working conditions and their deliveries arriving in time to avoid long lead 
times. Ineffective integration with suppliers and other stakeholders presents additional costs 
for firms in terms of defective goods and reworking arrangements (Kaynak and Hartley, 2009). 
Effective supplier integration, on the other hand, is likely to promote positive outcomes for 
organisations in terms of operational costs reduction and unnecessary expenses elimination. 
Supplier integration is expected to be an effective approach for responding to organisational 
needs and customer requests. It can have a positive impact on costs by saving on enormous 
capital expenditures of firms. Appropriate supplier integration lowers the costs incurred by 
firms and, hence, raises their profit numbers. It is then hypothesised that supplier integration 
has a significant positive impact on the cost performance of firms. Thus, based on above 
discussion the proposed hypothesis is as follows:  
H6a: Supplier integration has positive impact on cost performance. 
3.1.6.2. 
Supplier Integration and Flexibility Performance 
The current volatile business environment requires flexibility in operations to gain competitive 
advantages and improve overall performance. Literature on flexibility performance in firms 
has been increasing in the recent past with the research work of Tiwari et al. (2015), extending 
 
115 
 
this research to include the flexibility of supply chain components. The flexibility of processes 
is a current and urgent centre of focus based on the nature and pace of change in the business 
environment. The ability of firms to respond to these changes determines their success level. 
In order for firms have flexible internal operations, key external capabilities must be developed. 
Supplier integration provides firms with the external capabilities that enable responding to 
desired changes appropriately (Huo et al., 2018). Supplier integration involves having a high 
level of flexibility in the management strategies about how suppliers are engaged or selected. 
A flexible approach to supplier selection represents significant considerations and benefits for 
organisations to attain the best quality and the highest value for money. Supplier flexibility 
links the focal firm and its suppliers, which enables it to efficiently react to variances within 
the procurement process through aligning its resources and routines with suppliers. Inflexibility 
of the supply chain leads to increased lead time and hinders the firm’s ability to respond to the 
changes in customer tastes and preferences.  
Supplier integration has a positive association with aspects of manufacturing performance such 
as delivery time, product quality and volume flexibility. Having clear integration with suppliers 
provides firms with an environment that allows higher capability of maintaining the needed 
supplies at all times. Clear integration with suppliers also contributes to effective performance 
management because it means that all aspects of the supply chain work together in responding   
to customer needs. This is one of the most important aspects of SCM that influences firms’ 
adoption of strategies such as TPM and TQM that rely on managing the entire organisation. 
Planned maintenance promotes the flexibility of operations through strategies that enhance the 
capacity of the firm to change (Kumar et al., 2014).  
Marian and Soare (2020) argue that companies having good supplier relationships along with 
integrated internal systems have enhanced flexibility to respond according to the customers’ 
changing requirements as well as fluctuations in the market and industry. Organisations’ ability 
to be adaptive and flexible to varying market situations is significant in attaining the 
competitive advantage in industry. Integration at supplier and internal levels facilitate 
organisations to respond so quickly and effectively that could never be possible without such 
integration. Supplier integration helps organisations in attaining the inventory efficiency like 
only carrying the needed inventory while ensuring the further availability as soon as it is 
required by the production facility. It helps eliminating carrying costs as well as saving the 
space at manufacturing facility. Organisations could not enjoy such benefits in the absence of 
 
116 
 
supplier integration because in such case they may have to face certain delays as well (Chaurey 
et al., 2021). 
Flexibility performance has a critical role in responding to changing market conditions such as 
increase in demand or variations in customer preferences. With supplier integration, a firm is 
able to determine the relevant strategies to ensure that it responds to changes in the perceptions 
of customers. Integrating suppliers in operations and decision-making increases the capacity of 
the firm to adopt various product volumes and types. Supplier integration represents a highly 
beneficial tool for addressing flexibility for firms through virtual supply chains and dynamic 
workforce (Alolayyan et al., 2013). Organisations seek to achieve flexibility in their production 
strategies by avoiding unnecessary expenses or excessive stock. Continuous improvement in 
daily routines is encouraged through TQM implementation and high adjustment capability in 
response to market requirements. From the available literature, it is expected that implementing 
supplier integration strategies noticeably improves flexibility performance. It is also expected 
that effective integration of suppliers in the management of firms, especially those involved in 
manufacturing, allows more efficient performance. There are many dimensions of flexibility 
performance, but it is apparent from the literature that integrating suppliers in the organisation 
and involving them in decision-making can improve both the volume flexibility and product 
mix flexibility. Including flexibility in the selection of suppliers can influence the performance 
of an organisation in responding to market changes, which adds more agility to operations. 
Thus, based on above discussion the proposed hypothesis is as follows:  
H6b: Supplier integration has positive impact on flexibility performance. 
3.1.7. The Impact of Internal Integration on Operational Performance 
3.1.7.1. 
Internal Integration and Cost Performance 
Internal integration increases the internal capabilities of firms, which directly impacts their 
profit through reducing production costs. Ragatz et al. (1997) observed that coupling suppliers 
in internal capabilities provides avenues for knowledge and skills sharing among stakeholders, 
which reduces the time and cost of converting a business idea into a product. Rosenzweig et 
al. (2003) indicated that highly integrated firms with the capacity to provide competitive prices 
in the initial stages of a new product development process are able to overcome competition 
and derive high revenues from their new products. Roth (1996) observed a high cost-effective 
process and higher revenues in the firms with tightly integrated firms when compared to their 
 
117 
 
competitors. The ability of firms to develop better systems of communication, corporation and 
coordination between the functional departments help to reduce the costs of new productions. 
Reducing these costs of internal operations allows the firms to have a competitive position and 
results in increased sales. For example, Walmart was able to reduce around 20% of their 
production costs by simply mainstreaming the internal systems (Rosenzweig et al., 2003).  
Internal systems are critical to the achievement of organisational goals because of the need to 
work together in order to avoid inefficiencies. Since internal systems promote the performance 
of firms by enabling the value chain to operate effectively, they allow responding to the various 
weaknesses of inefficient coordination that may adversely affect organisational outcomes. 
Inadequate coordination and communication among internal stakeholders such as departments 
can influence the operational performance of the operation or organisation. The functional 
areas of an organisation need to coordinate their activities to ensure that they effectively 
communicate and respond to the challenges of operations. Open communication systems and 
strategies can influence the costs incurred by firms through minimising the likelihood of errors 
(Flynn and Flynn, 2005).  
Some of the most important factors to be considered in determining the profitability of an 
operation are its capacity to maintain efficiency while reducing costs. Cost effectiveness of an 
operation arises from having highly integrated and coordinated internal functions. The internal 
functions of a firm need to work together in coordinating responsiveness and communicating 
how to better compete according to market changes. The cost performance of a firm presents a 
significant point of competitiveness because it allows using the available resources in the most 
effective way to create more value for money (Phan et al., 2019). The element of internal 
integration involves ensuring that all employees are on board and can work together to achieve 
quality and excellence. With close coordination and cooperation among internal stakeholders, 
firms benefit from better responsiveness to customer needs and lower costs associated with 
errors and stock outages. Their employees can coordinate activities such as maintenance and 
order processing to avoid delays that may cause loss of customers. Thus, based on above 
discussion the proposed hypothesis is as follows:  
H7a: Internal integration has positive impact on cost performance. 
 
118 
 
3.1.7.2. 
Internal Integration and Flexibility Performance 
A firm is stated to have two capabilities for internal integration: coordination and planning. 
While coordination refers to the ability of firms to integrate across organisational processes, 
planning is where firms are able to combine their activities with the information provided by 
the members of the supply chain. Although internal integration is prioritised to manage the 
supply chain, there is an urgent need for specific firms to align intra- and inter-organisational 
resources with suppliers. For example, the structure of the supply chain could be aligned with 
organisational resources. Roberts and McKay (1998) held a contrary opinion that internal 
integration is an important, yet not a beneficial, approach for the overall firm performance. 
Subramani (2004) observed that internal integration improves the efficiency of firms’ 
operations such as controlling inventory, order processing and information exchange. Buyers 
and suppliers may exchange critical information regarding unforeseen problems that interrupt 
the procurement process and lead to a less productive business environment (McKone‐Sweet 
and Lee, 2009; Danese, 2013). Thus, performance operations are influenced by higher level of 
internal integration in a firm. 
Internal integration is essential to achieve organisational excellence where employees and 
departments must work together in order to avoid lack of responsiveness to customer needs 
and inefficiencies in the operation. Internal coordination contributes to the performance of 
firms by enabling the value chain to operate effectively. Employees have a significant role in 
responding to organisational challenges and inefficient coordination that may adversely affect 
organisational outcomes. Inadequate coordination and communication among internal 
stakeholders such as departments can influence the flexibility of operations in responding to 
emergent issues, which affects the operational performance of organisations. The functional 
areas of an organisation need to coordinate their activities to ensure that they effectively 
respond to emergent changes and new needs. Having better information and knowledge flow 
can influence the costs incurred by firms through reducing the errors and increasing the 
flexibility of their approaches and strategies to respond to market needs (Flynn and Flynn, 
2005). 
The internal functioning of a firm needs to work together in coordinating responsiveness to 
changes and communicating how to compete in the market. The element of internal integration 
involves ensuring that all onboard employees cooperate to achieve quality, responsiveness, and 
 
119 
 
excellence. Close coordination and cooperation among internal stakeholder’s means that firms 
benefit from better responsiveness to customer needs and lower costs associated with errors 
and stock outages. With highly integrated internal stakeholders, firms can effectively respond 
to changes in demands and other elements of production strategies such as small lot sizes and 
better coordination of maintenance activities. With internal integration, firms can make their 
operations more agile and redeploy employees and other resources to other products to ensure 
higher profitability. Internal integration provides a beneficial framework for organisations to 
take advantage of the resources at their disposal to develop products that are essential to 
customers. The relevant strategies for firms to ensure that they can effectively respond to the 
needs of their customers involve extending the supply chain and promoting information 
exchange to enable determining the demand levels and how to address them. These approaches 
of agile manufacturing were found to have a significant and beneficial effect on the 
performance of firms as they emphasise flexibility as a competitive advantage. Thus, based on 
above discussion the proposed hypothesis is as follows: 
H7b: Internal integration is positively impacting flexibility performance. 
 
 
120
