3.3. Chapter Summary 
Through relying upon the previous discussion, it is found that the above chapter concerns with 
displaying the various models and hypotheses, those are developed in this research in an 
attempt to accomplish the core aim and objectives. Additionally, the chapter also concentrates 
on elaborating the theoretical backgrounds behind each of these models in order to provide a 
comprehensive view of the logic behind developing the different research hypotheses. The next 
chapter discusses the methodology and design that were used to guide the effort of collecting 
and analysing the empirical data as a base to obtain the research findings, contributions, and 
conclusion.  
  
 
122 
 
Chapter 4 Research Design and Methodology 
This chapter provides a detailed description of the research methods, those are used to identify, 
gather, process, and analyse the needed data of this research for the aim of testing hypotheses, 
answering the study’s enquiries as well as fulfilling its core aim and objectives. The chapter is 
technically describing the entire procedure that is used to get credible information that can 
stand as new contribution for the advancement of related knowledge. It is divided into several 
sections: research philosophy, approach and method, sampling and data collection, pilot study, 
questionnaire design, non-response bias, measures, and conclusions.  
4.1. Research Philosophy 
A research philosophy represents the conceptual guidelines on how research data should be 
collected, analysed, and used to produce findings and conclusions for the development of 
theory (Galli, 2019). The basic assumption of a research philosophy is that it can transform 
what is considered to be true into what is known to be true. In other words, the purpose of a 
research philosophy is to transform things that scholars believe to be true into things that are 
evidently true. The popular philosophies in management research are the interpretivism school 
of thought and the positivism school of thought. This study leans towards the positivist 
philosophy, which relies on an epistemological stance that posits that reality can be only 
apprehended directly with no subjective bias from the researcher. It embraces an objective 
ontology that assumes the reality to be independent of human minds.  Thus, in order to conduct 
research on a phenomenon related to social sciences, the researcher should decide on his 
underlying philosophical stance towards the research and chooses the research method based 
on it.   
In general, in order to understand the problems and reality of the basic research elements, many 
perspectives of the underlying philosophy can be considered. These views differ in terms of 
defining the reality of phenomena and how to gain knowledge about them. For that, different 
philosophies can be considered such as positivism, interpretivism, critical realism and post 
structuralism (Poster, M., 2019). These philosophies differ in the way they answer the three 
fundamental questions that arise about ontology, epistemology, and methodology. These 
questions are: what is reality? What is the nature of knowing about the reality of this 
phenomenon? How is this knowledge obtained? By selecting a philosophical stance, the 
researcher implicitly follows specific research design to gain knowledge within the theoretical 
 
123 
 
boundaries of that philosophy. In addition, the required research approach and other relevant 
research methods are revealed. 
As this research follows the fundamental rules and basis of the positivist paradigm, it favours 
using the quantitative method to investigate the research problem (O'Gorman and MacIntosh, 
2014). In these methods, the researcher assumes that the necessary knowledge about the studied 
phenomenon can be obtained objectively and without any subjective bias. It should be noted 
that social phenomena, especially those that deal with human behaviour, cannot be observed in 
a completely objective way and without being exposed to the researcher’s bias. Quantitative 
and qualitative research methods represent the two main types of methods in social sciences. 
They use correct and real knowledge rather than knowledge based on belief, which, in turn, 
allows for avoiding personal practice (Collis and Hussey, 2013).  
The debate over the nature of quantitative research is a living debate with the philosophical 
movement of ontology and epistemology. Nevertheless, the question arises as to why the 
ontology and knowledge of science should be found in the form of a plan or program that is 
sufficient in research is not accepted. Often ontological and epistemological issues tend to 
integrate and connect with each other. This research follows the positivism approach, as this is 
based on the cause-and-effect relationship. Moreover, the deductive reasoning is adopted for 
this study which means, the theoretical framework was proposed on the basis on theories and 
then hypothesis was formulated and after that data was gathered through research questionnaire 
or survey. The data technique was quantitative analysis. As the study needs to highlight the 
role of manufacturing practices and their combined effect with the SCM practices to enhance 
the operational performance of Jordanian industrial firms. So, the study chose to collect 
quantitative data from the concerned respondents i.e., working in Jordanian industries. The 
quantitative data collected from these respondents help identifying the effect of variables under 
study. Therefore, this study employs sampling technique and data was analysed through SPSS 
and AMOS.  
4.1.1. Ontology 
The ontological stance is a study of tangible aspects, in that the researcher’s knowledge of the 
phenomena under investigation is certain based on experience (Scotland, 2012). As this 
research studies how the Jordan industrial firms perform in diverse aspects and conditions, 
objective ontology is adopted to study this phenomenon. The findings of a survey on these 
 
124 
 
entities may help reach the research objectives and objectively address the reality about the 
phenomenon concerned. 
Ontological questions concern the nature of reality of a phenomenon (Bahari, 2010). These 
questions are about both physical reality and social reality. In social sciences and research, 
ontological assumptions include low-depth realist ontology, conceptual realist ontology 
(Stanley and Mackenzie-Davey, 2012), cautious realist ontology (Magnus, 2018), deep realist 
ontology (Patomäki and Wight, 2000) and idealistic ontology (Hall and Wicaksono, 2020). 
Ontology refers to the general beliefs about what things or essences exist in the real world that 
refers to a range of beings and relationships accepted within a specific scientific field. Research 
ontology also refers to the assumptions of a particular approach of social research about the 
nature of social reality (Ansari et al., 2016) and the theory of social entities and everything that 
exists for research. The ontology of research reflects the researcher's attitude about the nature 
of the world (Maarouf, 2019) and her/his approach about what the social world constitutes and 
how it should be studied.  In short, research ontology justifies a particular set of beliefs about 
the nature of social reality.  
4.1.2. Epistemology 
Epistemology is the theory of knowledge and perception in science (Lemos, 2020). It clarifies 
what is the acceptable knowledge in any system of thought and how research findings can be 
judged. In other words, it clarifies what makes the findings of one research more appropriate 
than the findings of another in the same field. Epistemology raises the question of what tests 
must be passed in order for data and information to be considered legitimate knowledge, and 
what classification of subjects can be recognised. Epistemology c9ncerns the relation between 
the knower or the researcher and the real-life truth (Roots, 2007). In social science research, 
epistemological assumptions include empiricism, rationalism, constructivism, contractism and 
neorealism (Krause and Williams, 2002).  
Epistemology studies the nature of knowledge and how and what can be known (Krause and 
Williams, 2002).  It can be considered as a justification for knowledge.  In short, epistemology 
of research refers to assumptions about how to recognise whatever is accepted. Moreover, the 
epistemology of this study or the knowledge claim is concerned with how the researcher 
gathers data for analysis and consumption (Scotland, 2012). This study deliberately focuses on 
objectivism, which is the assumption that reality is independent and distinct from human minds 
and their subjective assumptions. Objectivism provides reliability as it suggests that the results 
 
125 
 
of the research are consistent. It also provides external validity as it allows gaining replicable 
and applicable results in other contexts. In conclusion, the research philosophy here aims at 
deducing evidence from the response of many stakeholders and top managers, which is a gained 
based on quantitative methods.  
4.2. Research Approach 
Based on the methods of knowledge acquisition, the two main research approaches are the 
inductive and deductive research approaches (Kothari, 2004). The inductive research approach 
moves from specific to general (i.e., collecting specific observations and drawing general 
conclusions from them). The deductive research approach moves from general to specific (i.e. 
starting from general statements and data collection while drawing specific conclusions from 
them). While inductive research involves collecting and interpreting qualitative data, deductive 
research incorporates collecting and interpreting quantitative data (Walliman, 2011).  
The Deductive approach is applicable in this study considering the broad scope that the 
research questions are covering, which could require receiving different treatments for the 
different questions (Woiceshyn and Daellenbach, 2018).  The deductive approach expects the 
researcher to commence her/his investigation from a position where there is a theory that 
requires testing. This theoretical grounding is the reason why the deductive approach 
commences with a hypothesis that includes a null and alternative query. The idea behind this 
is that the new study tests whether the existing theory remains valid or not, then proposes a 
new set of knowledge with respect to the theory.  
The inductive approach, on the other hand, concentrates on the data and the findings that are 
produced from it, which then validates existing theories or creates the base for new theories. 
This focus on data and results is the reason why the inductive approach relies on questions that 
confine the scope of the research into areas that are addressable to the researcher. While the 
deductive approach works better within the context of quantitative research, the inductive 
approach is mostly associated with qualitative studies. Owing to the depth of the study and the 
nature of the research variables, as expressed in the research questions, the researcher prefers 
applying the elements of the deductive approach for data collection. 
4.3. Research Method 
Methodology concerns how research is conducted (Katz, 2015); it is the collection and analysis 
of assumptions, principles, and methods of implementation in a particular approach to research. 
 
126 
 
The methodological part of the research consists of the methods, techniques, instruments, and 
tools for gathering and analysing data. Research methods are mediators and consequences of 
research practice. In simpler terms, a methodology justifies the way data is collected and 
analysed. Therefore, this research adopts the quantitative method in order to emphasise the 
gathering of larger sets of measurable data (Park and Park, 2016). This entails the collection of 
objective data that can easily undergo statistical analysis using data collection tools such as 
questionnaires. These tools deliberately design questions to stimulate answers from 
respondents, which fit into categories and allow the researcher to assign numerical values. This 
leads to produce measures that can be interpreted by the researcher in descriptive and inferential 
analysis. 
The quantitative method is appropriate for this study for various reasons. First, it is convenient 
for the researcher to investigate and determine the relation between various independent and 
dependent variables. Second, the quantitative method allows the researcher to conduct a 
descriptive investigation in which subjects are considered only once to establish associations 
and inform the industry and policy. There is no need to go to the extent of identifying causality 
as happens with action research. All that is necessary at this stage for the identification of an 
existent to non-existent relation between the independent and dependent variables. Third, the 
quantitative method fits with the objective ontology that is adopted in this research with a 
convergent focus aiming at fulfilling the study objectives (i.e., convergent reasoning and ideas). 
Fourth, quantitative results drawn from a large sample are generalisable to a population. This 
means that the discovered relationships between the independent and dependent variables is 
applicable to all Jordanian industries, the middle east region and possibly to countries beyond 
that region. For instance, if the sample chosen in this research responds affirmatively to the 
question on whether the manufacturing practices has influenced the operational performance 
the Jordanian industrial firms, then it is possible to generalise this to other sectors in the country 
and possibly draw similar conclusions in other countries with similar economies and market 
dynamics. Hence, the quantitative method provides solutions to various challenges that the 
study is seeking to address, which adds to existing knowledge about the phenomenon under 
investigation.   
The aim of this study is to measure the relation between the manufacturing practices (i.e. TPM, 
TQM) with the focus on the moderation effect of SCM supplier integration and internal 
integration on the operational performance (i.e. cost, flexibility) of the Jordanian industrial 
 
127 
 
sector. To achieve this aim, the study relies on the survey as a tool for collecting the data of 
this research. According to Polland (2005), the survey is a suitable way to collect information 
under the following conditions: (1) when the objectives are quantitative as is the case in this 
research; (2) when the information is specific to the respondent and (3) when the researcher 
has prior knowledge of possible responses. 
The responses of the sample members can be obtained through both the traditional written 
responses and the electronic responses. This saves time and cost and allows higher response 
rates during the Covid-19 pandemic, which facilitated better access to the required data and 
more representing results of this study. 
Survey research requires the researcher to be interested in developing the research tool. It 
requires the questionnaire to be carefully chosen and that the questions to be clear and 
comprehensive for respondents to clearly answer the questions to understand the relationships 
between variables. The population of this research consists of the Jordanian industrial firms 
with the sample is selected as the middle and top managers in the targeted firms. 
4.4. Questionnaire Development 
Survey development involves several key steps that enable the researcher to move from the 
point of having plain concepts and constructs to administering the survey. It happens as two 
broad simultaneous processes with planning happening at the same time as administrative 
preparation (Green and Frantom, 2002).  
In planning, several steps should take place. The researcher must clarify the study purpose, 
decide on results usage, formulate the research questions, and create a timeline. Then, she/he 
should prepare the tool content, create an outline for topics, identify the constructs and consult 
the literature. Then, the researcher must compare her/his survey with other existing surveys or 
consider an already tested data tool to guide the process. Later, the researcher needs to prepare 
and gather the tool elements either by borrowing from existing tools or by creating her/his own. 
After that, the researcher should pre-test the survey to understand the robustness of the tool. 
Finally, the researcher must revise the survey draft and conduct a pre-test study to the verify 
its reliability and validity before data collection.  
In administration, a number of processes will also apply. First, the researcher needs to plan 
survey administration, which involves defining a target population and deciding on the best 
 
128 
 
way to reach it. Second, the researcher needs to determine the sample size. This involves 
creating a sample frame, choosing sampling strategies and selecting a sample. Third, the 
researcher needs to arrange connecting with the respondents by preparing survey invitations, 
tracking non-responsive participants and keep on communicating for the follow-up stage. Once 
this is complete, the researcher is in a suitable position to administer the survey. 
In this research, questionnaire is used as a data collection instrument to gather the required data 
to reflect the participants’ attitudes towards the relationship between research variables. 
However, there are various methods are used to determine the validity of the instrument. These 
methods are apparent validity, content validity, concurrent validity, predictive validity, and 
construct validity. Each of the above methods is used for specific purposes (Yang et al., 2014). 
Content validity is used to confirm the content of the instrument. Concurrent validity is used 
when a standard questionnaire is available. Predictive validity is used to predict a future 
phenomenon. Structural validity is used to structurally adapt the tool to previous theories. In 
order to adapt the appearance and determine the content range of the questionnaire, the method 
of content validity is used.  
As the study adopts a survey method, the questionnaire was used as a tool for collecting data 
from individuals. It is important when designing the questionnaire that the researcher focuses 
on clarifying the independent, dependent, and intermediate variables. The survey has been 
carried out in a series of stages: in the beginning, the questions related to manufacturing 
practices (i.e., TPM, TQM), operational performance (i.e., cost, flexibility) and SCM (i.e. 
supplier integration, internal integration) were formulated. Then, the questionnaire was 
presented to a group of academics with specialisation in the same field of study to express their 
opinions on the formulation of the questions and suggest the necessary amendments. Later, the 
questions were tested with a sample of four respondents and, as a result of the test, the content 
of the questionnaire was revised and amended to be clearer and more comprehensive. 
4.5. Questionnaire Design 
When designing the questionnaire, the following recommendations were considered according 
to (Dillman et al., 2014): 
1- Simplicity: the questions asked in the questionnaire were as simple as possible so that 
participants can easily understand the meaning and answer accordingly. For this, the 
researcher avoided mentioning any technical terms or specialised expressions that are 
 
129 
 
difficult to understand. To reduce ambiguity in the closed questions, the answers were 
provided as a range of standard options such as: strongly agree, agree, neutral, disagree 
and strongly disagree. In this type of scale, it is assumed that the middle option is a 
neutral point between the options. In some cases, it is possible that the construction of 
a response scale is not balanced.   
2- Length and appearance of the questionnaire: it should be noted that the longer the 
questionnaire, the lower the response rate. There is no specific rule for the number of 
questions in the questionnaire and, thus, it depends on the intelligence and literacy of 
the respondents and their interest in the subject. The appearance of the questionnaire is 
also effective in increasing the response rate. The use of question boxes, the type of 
questionnaire and the clear and explicit expression of the questions help respondents to 
pay more attention and encourage them to response. Moreover, it is better to use the 
same scale throughout the questionnaire if the response scale has a certain shape, such 
as a -point grading scale. 
3- Translation process: it requires observing its own rules and principles. It should be 
noted that in addition to translating the questionnaire, cultural adaptation of the meaning 
of the phrases is very important. This is because in order to adapt the questionnaire to 
the cultural context of the Jordanian society, semantic and content equality of phrases 
must be carried out. Appropriate translation with cultural adaptation allows comparing 
the concepts in both target and reference societies. The values, concepts and meanings 
reflected by the tool may be different from one culture to another. Therefore, the 
translation quality and validity of the translated version of the tool have an important 
role in the validation of the translated version.   
4- Questionnaire preparation: the questions and instructions related to the questionnaire 
should be clear and understandable for respondent. The questionnaire should be written 
or expressed in a way that encourages respondents to participate. Instructions, 
questions, and tips should be designed in such a way to make participants interested in 
continuing their cooperation until returning the completed questionnaires.  
5- Purpose and problem statement: the first step in implementing the questionnaire of the 
current research was to prepare a list of goals that the researcher intends to achieve. It 
is almost impossible to prepare a questionnaire without having a clear understanding of 
 
130 
 
what is required to be achieved. The purpose of the questionnaire is to describe the 
behaviour, attitudes, practices, thoughts, and feelings and also to test the relationship 
between two or more variables.  
6- Determining the community and selecting the sample: determining the community in 
most cases is done based on several points such as subject importance, generalizability 
and the researcher interest and information resources. In this study, exploring the full 
research context is not easily possible due to the large community size. In addition, to 
describe the characteristics of the community and test the related hypothesis, it is not 
necessary to study all the members of the community. Instead, sample members were 
selected from the community to be treated as representatives. Before distributing the 
questionnaire in its final form to the sample, the researcher ensured that the design of 
the questionnaires is following the advice obtained from the experimental sample. This 
was achieved through modifying the questions to design a clear questionnaire so that 
the sample members can easily answer. 
4.6. Pilot Study 
A pilot study is a feasibility pre-research study that a researcher conducts with the intention to 
testing and verifying the strength of the data collection instruments (Arain et al., 2010; Lowe, 
2019).). It is also a useful way to test the reliability of the target population (In, 2017). It is a 
way of trying to tease out any possible problems that may arise when the main survey begins. 
In this ‘rehearsal’ survey, the researcher gets an opportunity to test the methods, design, tools, 
personnel, target, and population. Piloting enables the researcher to test some important aspects 
of the study. First, she/he gets to test the sample and its size and whether the recruitment 
methods are manageable and practical. Second, she/he gets to test the collected data at analysis 
stage to determine whether the proposed approach is suitable to the study and whether results 
are reasonable in the given context. Finally, the results of the pilot test enable the researcher to 
check the reliability and validity of the data collection’s tool. However, through a Cronbach’s-
Alpha test, the researcher will be able determining whether the questions in the survey are 
appropriate to be in the survey and fit with each other. The test indicates which question is 
weakening or strengthening the entire questionnaire, enabling the researcher to alter, improves 
or eliminates the problem questions before the main survey takes place. The pilot questionnaire 
was distributed among the Jordanian industrial firms and has received responses from all four 
firms, which allowed bettering developing the main questionnaire.  
 
131 
 
Cronbach’s alpha measures the stability of values, as it is clear from the empirical study the 
study that the values and that they confirm the internal consistency of the elements/items. To 
ensure the reliability of the study, Cronbach's Alpha test was conducted in order to ensure that 
the values give reliable results for the research, where the values should be >= 0.6 (Taber, 
2018), Table 4-1 show the pilot study figures.

4.7. Sample and Data Collection 
The target population for this study consists of managers in the Jordanian industrial firms who 
have a rich knowledge about people, processes, problems, and solutions that affect the 
industrial sector. The data about the industrial firms where those managers work was obtained 
from the Ministry of Industry, Trade and Supply and Amman Chamber of Industry. Amman 
Chamber of Industry has significant contribution in this research. The chamber supported the 
research in data collection phase. The names and number of industrial companies working in 
Jordanian economy are provided. The study incorporated SMEs and MNCs working in Jordan. 
The reason for incorporation of these two different types of organisations is to highlight the 
effects of manufacturing and supply chain practices on the operational performance of all 
organisations operating in Jordan, so that the true scenario can be depicted. The researcher was 
given the approval for data collection. Then the researcher adopted random sampling 
technique. The employees of the organisations working in Jordanian industries were randomly 
targeted as a target population of this study. Then the consent of these employees/managers is 
taken. After that, the researcher targeted those managers who are willing to take the online 
survey.  The researcher relies on the hosting portal of the online survey to stratify the sample 
as it has built-in stratification features that enable the sample to reflect the targeted population; 
thereby, increasing the credibility and enabling the generalisability of the obtained findings 
(Acharya et al., 2013). As the online questionnaire was not enough to collect the data needed 
for this research due to a relatively poor response rate, the questionnaire was traditionally 
distributed through visiting the targeted industrial firm and collecting their responses. After 
excluding eight incomplete responses, 224 completed responses were received from the 
 
132 
 
targeted managers as a result of distributing both the traditional and online questionnaires 
(Table 4-2).

4.8. Non-response Bias 
The non-response bias occurs when some of the participants in a survey are distinctly different 
from the rest of the sample, which makes them neglect the study because it is not applicable to 
them (for example, old people in a survey about night clubs) (Sedgwick, 2014). This bias is 
usually a result of poor targeting methods that mislead researchers to individuals who may not 
be suitable for the study. In some instances, the survey may not reach its intended target (for 
example, an invite email that ends up in a potential respondent’s spam folder). To minimise 
non-response bias, the researcher ensured that the targeting methods are precise (for example, 
 
133 
 
using direct social media channels such as WhatsApp to reach participants, share links to the 
hosted survey and maintain a good rapport thereafter to stimulate good answers). Furthermore, 
all questions were close ended in keeping with the quantitative design, which respondents find 
easier to handle than open-ended questions.

4.9. Common method Bias 
Common method bias describes the measurement error that is compounded by the sociability 
of respondents who want to provide positive answers (Chang, v. Witteloostuijn and Eden, 
2010). This research collected data on both the independent and dependent variables from the 
same respondents at one point in time, thus raising potential common method variance as false 
internal consistency might be present in the data. therefore, it is essential to check the 
percentage of variance through the common method bias test using Harman’s single-factor. 
There is no problem with the common method bias test using Harman’s single-factor in this 
data since the total variance extracted by one factor is 30.418% and it is less than the 
recommended threshold of 50%.

4.10. Measures 
To safeguard the credibility of this research process, the following measures were pursued by 
the researchers (Dooly et al., 2017). First, an invitation message was sent to respondents that 
contain a detailed description of their rights and duties regarding the research. Second, consent 
was embedded within the questionnaire that participants must approve before they begin 
answering the survey questions. Third, the questionnaire was maintained confidential, and the 
identities of participants were kept as anonymised. Forth, the respondents were asked to 
indicate the extent of their agreement with statements according to the actual situation using a 
 
137 
 
Likert scale where 1 indicates ‘strongly disagree’ and 7 indicates ‘strongly agree’. Theoretical 
literature has been used to develop resolution measures (Table 4-6).

4.11. Chapter Summary 
This chapter has discussed the leading philosophies and approaches in management studies and 
selected the most suitable ones for this research. The chapter also clarified the methods and 
techniques on how the research community and its sample members were identified and 
approached. The chapter further described the design and process used to prepare the survey 
as a data collection tool that fits with the quantitative method that was adopted in this research. 
In the next chapter, the focus is on explaining the steps pursued to conduct the descriptive data 
analysis of the research. 
 
139 
 
 
Figure 4-1: Research Methods for Business and Management (O'Gorman and MacIntosh, 2014). 
 
140 
 
Chapter 5 Data Analysis 
The main aim of this research is scrutinizing the effect of manufacturing practices (TPM, TQM) 
on operational performance (cost, flexibility) when moderated by SCM (supplier integration, 
internal integration). To attain this aim, the study concentrates on compelling data through 
developing a questionnaire of twenty-two items. This questionnaire was distributed to a sample 
of 441 industrial firms and achieved a response rate of 51%; in that, 243 responses were 
obtained, and after excluding nineteen non-analysable questionnaires, 224 were fit for 
statistical analysis. These questionnaires included the research data that was analysed through 
the ‘statistical package for social sciences (SPSS)’ and AMOS quantitative analysis software. 
This package was used to conduct the various tests of validity, stability, normality, exploratory 
and confirmatory factor analysis, and path analysis that were employed to examine the 
collected data related to the research variables. By doing so, the chapter discusses the 
descriptive analysis of the research data to extract the results and test the hypotheses that are 
later presented. 
5.1. Missing Data and Outliers 
The collected data from the Jordanian industrial firms was analysed by different tests to obtain 
results about the study hypotheses. It was subjected to different types of tests and focused on 
the completed questionnaires to obtain accurate results. When the design of the questionnaire 
was made, the researcher tried to develop questions that may allow representing answers to 
obtain the needed data to reach conclusions. For that, some questions were excluded and some 
of which were modified to be easier and clearer. Every invalid questionnaire was excluded due 
to its incompleteness, the refusal of some respondents to provide sufficient information about 
their firms (for example, information about cost, revenue, size) and the difficulty in reaching 
some firms during the covid-19 pandemic. The completed questionnaires were used to allow 
accurate results by using a 7-point Likert scale. This design, through conducting the exploratory 
factor analysis, helped to check for outliers. As a result, no outliers were found which increased 
the validity of the data and the obtained results.

5.3.  Normality, Validity and Reliability 
In order to obtain accurate results, and before conducting hypotheses testing, a set of tests were 
conducted to verify and ensure that the data entered into the analysis software is valid for 
analysis and enough to fulfil the objective of the study. 
5.4.1. Normality 
 The normal distribution is a symmetrical distribution around the mean; it shows that the data 
close to the mean is more frequent than the data far from the mean. It can be confirmed that the 
data was subjected to a normal distribution by skewness and kurtosis. The value of deviation 
was acceptable for the set of test scores normally distributed as it is close to zero, and when it 
deviates from zero, the positive value indicates the possibility of a positive skewed distribution, 
and when it is negative, it indicates the probability of a negative skewed distribution, and the 
kurtosis values are considered to represent a normal distribution when the values are close to 
one (Brown, 1997). 
The normal distribution of all variables tested to use the statistical analysis, firstly, the 
independent variables that describe two components were selected (TPM, TQM, Supplier 
integration, Internal integration). In table 5.10, the values of the mean, standard deviation, 
skewness, kurtosis. This means that it follows a normal distribution.

5.4.2. Reliability and Validity 
For the purpose of verifying the stability of the study measures, the internal consistency method 
was used by applying Cronbach's Alpha coefficient to test the internal stability of the study 
tool. It is known that the stability values of the variables must be greater than 0.6 (Pallant, 
2005), table 5.17 shows the acceptable results of the reliability test for the study variables. 
5.4.3. Exploratory Factors Analysis (EFA) 
EFA is employed for the reduction of number of variables to a small number named as factors. 
This mathematical process is employed aiming at simplifying the correlations among different 
 
147 
 
variables under study (Plucker, 2003). Initially in EFA factors are extracted through different 
extraction techniques while PCA (principal component analysis) is among the most widely 
employed extraction techniques that generate principal factors, so current research has also 
employed EFA on 224 observations and 22 items.  
The main focus of EFA is the validity of tools incorporated in the study, to ensure sufficient 
loading of factors of each variable of interest. For this the acceptable factor value is greater 
than 0.5 and for Eigen value it should be greater than 1 (Laher, 2010). Results of EFA for 
current research are reported in table 5.15.  
Exploratory factor analysis could be performed in two ways: 
1. First method incorporates exploring and obtaining the number of factors that affect the 
variables of interest as well as identifying the number of factors due for that variable. 
For example, incorporating all the information in analysis for obtaining new factors by 
restructuring the study questionnaire and then analysing it again to obtain new results. 
Then making comparison of the results of initial questionnaire and the second 
questionnaire.  
2. In the second method, all paragraphs associated with variables are entered separately, 
while ensuring that paragraphs associated with one variable refer to that particular 
variable. It confirms the credibility and validity of the construction of associated 
paragraphs ensuring that the values for all parameters of the clauses should be greater 
than 0.4. The current research has incorporated first method which is as follows:

5.4.4. Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) 
Based on the results of the exploratory factor analysis (EFA), the confirmatory factor analysis 
(CFA) was used to verify the structural validity of the scale. This procedure is common in the 
confirmatory factor analysis to determine the selected model. Thus, if the confirmatory factor 
analysis achieves the assumed model of measurement, the conformity quality indicators are 
achieved. It is possible to judge the sincerity of its expressions or its dimensions, and in this 
analysis, the saturations with which it is reduced are rejected regression weights; validity or 
saturation coefficients on the latent variable should be about 0.5 (Hair et al., 2010).

indicating the relationships of variables in a way that cost performance is found to be positively 
affected by supplier integration while flexibility performance is found to be positively affected 
by TPM, TQM and internal integration. On the other hand, TPM is found to be positively 
affected by TQM while supplier integration and internal integration are found to have positive 
effect on TPM and TQM. All these findings indicate the interrelationships of variables in a way 
that the operational performance of organisations is influenced by manufacturing practices and 
supplier and internal integration play significant role in enhancing operational performance 
through the implementation of manufacturing practices. It means that if organisations adopt 
and implement manufacturing practices to enhance their operational performance then they 
should also consider establishing supplier and internal integration for attaining desired 
outcomes of operational performance of organisations.

5.6.  Discussion 
It is important that manufacturing practices receive academic attention, as this study focused 
on knowing the impact of manufacturing practices on cost and flexibility, in industrial 
companies in the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan, where it was clarified how to design the 
model in the previous chapters, the current study clarified the role of four independent variables 
(TPM , TQM , supplier , internal) on Two dependent variables (cost , flexibility), where a set 
of hypotheses was designed, data were collected and a description of the statistical analysis 
was extracted, and then a hypothesis test was extracted to know the effect of each variable on 
the other. 
This chapter contains a presentation of the results of the study and a discussion of the meaning 
of the results, as it presents the limits and limitations, and future studies . 
5.6.1. Overview of the Study 
The current research focuses on identifying the key determinants of operational performance 
in the industrial sector, by exploring the impact of the manufacturing practices (TQM, TPM) 
on operational performance (Cost, Flexibility) while studying the role of supplier integration 
and internal integration, in the Jordanian industrial sector. The focus of this research is on the 
significance of internal and external integration in companies as a strategy for improving 
operational performance and other outcomes. These include the assessment of the identified 
benefits derived from the adoption of TPM and TQM in organisations to enhance operational 
performance and excellence. It focuses on these innovations in the industrial sector, which are 
designed to improve efficiency and operational performance of manufacturing processes. It is 
essential to conduct empirical research to identify how these manufacturing practices influence 
suppliers and internal integration as well as operational performance in the industrial sector. 
 
158 
 
 
Figure 5-2: Model of Study 
In an attempt to understand the relationship between manufacturing practices on operational 
performance, the research model was developed and tested through the use of survey data 
collected by Jordanian industrial companies, where the results path analysis showed the 
relationship between the variables, in relation to the results explained in the previous chapter . 
It has been found that TPM has an impact on the cost, and this means that the more attention 
is paid to TPM it will help reduce the costs that industrial companies are affected by, As for 
TQM quality is important for any company because it aims to strengthen the competitive 
position of companies, in light of recent changes in technology and means of communication. 
In this study, TQM did not prove an effect on costs. As for the effect of TQM on TPM it 
clarified the existence of Effect, as for the elasticity variable, the TPM and TQM variables 
proved their effect on flexibility in this study, this study also prove the impact of the supplier 
on flexibility and cost, where the effect on flexibility was marginally effect, but on cost the 
effect was positive, on the other hand, the effect of Internal on flexibility was positive, but the 
effect is effect on cost. Figure 5-3 shows the model summary.
